description: Find the volume of a right rectangular prism with whole-number side lengths by packing it with unit cubes and show that the volume is the same as would be found by multiplying the edge lengths, equivalently by multiplying the height by the area of the base. Represent threefold whole-number products as volumes.
description_plain: Your child can find the volume of a box by filling it with cubes and by multiplying its sides, showing both ways give the same answer.

subject: Math
description: Relate volume to the operations of multiplication and addition and solve real world and mathematical problems involving volume.
description_plain: Your child understands that volume can be found by multiplying and adding, and can solve problems using volume.

subject: Math
description: Measure volumes by counting unit cubic cm, cubic in, cubic ft. and improvised units.
description_plain: Your child can measure volume by counting small cubes or other units like cubic inches or feet.

subject: Math
description: A solid figure which can be packed without gaps or overlaps using n unit cubes is said to have a volume of n cubic units.
description_plain: Your child knows that if a solid can be filled exactly with a certain number of cubes, its volume is that number of cubic units.

subject: Math
description: A cube with side length 1 unit, called a "unit cube," is said to have "one cubic unit" of volume and can be used to measure volume.
description_plain: Your child understands that a cube with sides one unit long is called a unit cube and measures one cubic unit of volume.

subject: Math
description: Recognize volume as an attribute of solid figures and understand concepts of volume measurement.
description_plain: Your child understands that volume is how much space a solid takes up and learns how to measure it.

subject: Math
description: Identify and gather data for statistical questions focused on both categorical and numerical data. Select an appropriate data display (bar graph, pictograph, dot plot). Make observations from the graph about the questions posed.
description_plain: Your child can collect data for questions about categories or numbers, choose the best graph to show the data, and explain what the graph tells.

subject: Math
description: Convert among different size measurement units (mass, weight, liquid volume, length, time) within one system of units (metric system, U.S. standard system and time).
description_plain: Your child can change measurements like weight, length, volume, and time between different units in the same system.

subject: Math
description: Solve real world problems involving division of unit fractions by non-zero whole numbers and division of whole numbers by unit fractions.
description_plain: Your child can solve real problems that involve dividing fractions by whole numbers and dividing whole numbers by fractions.

subject: Math
description: Interpret division of a whole number by a unit fraction and compute such quotients.
description_plain: Your child understands and can calculate what happens when you divide a whole number by a fraction.

subject: Math
description: Interpret division of a unit fraction by a non-zero whole number and compute such quotients.
description_plain: Your child understands and can calculate what happens when you divide a fraction by a whole number.

subject: Math
description: Apply and extend previous understandings of division to divide unit fractions by whole numbers and whole numbers by unit fractions.
description_plain: Your child can divide fractions by whole numbers and whole numbers by fractions using what they already know about division.

subject: Math
description: Solve real world problems involving multiplication of fractions and mixed numbers.
description_plain: Your child can solve real problems that involve multiplying fractions and mixed numbers.

subject: Math
description: Explaining why multiplying a given number by a fraction greater than 1 results in a product greater than the given number (recognizing multiplication by whole numbers greater than 1 as a familiar case); explaining why multiplying a given number by a fraction less than 1 results in a product smaller than the given number; and relating the principle of fraction equivalence a/b = (n × a)/(n × b) to the effect of multiplying a/b by 1.
description_plain: Your child can explain why multiplying by a fraction bigger than 1 makes a number bigger, and multiplying by a fraction less than 1 makes it smaller.

subject: Math
description: Comparing the size of a product to the size of one factor on the basis of the size of the other factor, without performing the indicated multiplication.
description_plain: Your child can compare the size of a multiplication answer to one of the numbers being multiplied without actually multiplying.

subject: Math
description: Interpret multiplication as scaling (resizing), by:
description_plain: Your child understands multiplication as resizing or scaling numbers.

subject: Math
description: Find the area of a rectangle with fractional side lengths by tiling it with squares of the appropriate unit fraction side lengths and show that the area is the same as would be found by multiplying the side lengths. Multiply fractional side lengths to find areas of rectangles and represent fraction products as rectangular areas.
description_plain: Your child can find the area of rectangles with fractional sides by covering them with small squares and by multiplying the side lengths.

subject: Math
description: Interpret the product (a/b) × q as a parts of a partition of q into b equal parts; equivalently, as the result of a sequence of operations a × q ÷ b.
description_plain: Your child understands multiplying a fraction by a whole number as dividing the whole number into equal parts and taking some of those parts.

subject: Math
description: Apply and extend previous understanding of multiplication to multiply a fraction or whole number by a fraction.
description_plain: Your child can multiply fractions and whole numbers by fractions using what they already know about multiplication.

subject: Math
description: Interpret a fraction as division of the numerator by the denominator (a/b = a ÷ b). Solve word problems involving division of whole numbers leading to answers in the form of fractions or mixed numbers by using visual fraction models or equations to represent the problem.
description_plain: Your child understands that a fraction means dividing the top number by the bottom number and can solve word problems using this idea.

subject: Math
description: Use benchmark fractions and number sense of fractions to estimate mentally and assess the reasonableness of answers.
description_plain: Your child can use familiar fractions to estimate answers and check if their answers make sense.

subject: Math
description: Solve word problems involving addition and subtraction of fractions referring to the same whole, including cases of unlike denominators.
description_plain: Your child can solve word problems that add or subtract fractions with different bottom numbers.

subject: Math
description: Solve word problems involving addition and subtraction of fractions.
description_plain: Your child can solve word problems involving adding and subtracting fractions.

subject: Math
description:
Efficiently add and subtract fractions with unlike denominators (including mixed numbers) by
  • using reasoning strategies, such as counting up on a number line or creating visual fraction models
  • finding common denominators
description_plain: Your child can add and subtract fractions with different bottom numbers by using number lines, pictures, or finding common denominators.

subject: Math
description: Relate the strategy to a written method and explain the reasoning used.
description_plain: Your child can explain the steps and reasons for adding, subtracting, multiplying, and dividing decimals.

subject: Math
description:
Add, subtract, multiply and divide decimals to hundredths using…
  • concrete models or drawings
  • strategies based on place value
  • properties of operations
  • the relationship between addition and subtraction
description_plain: Your child can add, subtract, multiply, and divide decimals to hundredths using models, place value, and properties of operations.

subject: Math
description: Operations with decimals to hundredths.
description_plain: Your child can do math operations with decimals up to two decimal places.

subject: Math
description: Illustrate and explain the calculation by using equations, rectangular arrays and/or area models.
description_plain: Your child can show and explain division problems using equations, arrays, or area models.

subject: Math
description:
Find whole-number quotients of whole numbers with up to four-digit dividends and two-digit divisors using
  • strategies based on place value
  • the properties of operations
  • the relationship between multiplication and division
description_plain: Your child can divide numbers with up to four digits by two-digit numbers using place value and properties of operations.

subject: Math
description: Divide up to four-digit dividends by two-digit divisors.
description_plain: Your child can divide numbers up to four digits by two-digit numbers.

subject: Math
description: Fluently multiply multi-digit whole numbers (not to exceed four-digit by two-digit multiplication) using an algorithm.
description_plain: Your child can multiply multi-digit numbers (up to four digits by two digits) accurately using a step-by-step method.

subject: Math
description: Use place value understanding to round decimals to any place.
description_plain: Your child can round decimals to any place value using their understanding of place value.

subject: Math
description: Compare two decimals to thousandths based on meanings of the digits in each place, using >, =, and < symbols to record the results of comparisons.
description_plain: Your child can compare two decimals up to thousandths place and use >, =, or < to show which is bigger or if they are equal.

subject: Math
description: Read and write decimals to thousandths using base-ten numerals, number names and expanded form.
description_plain: Your child can read and write decimals up to thousandths using numbers, words, and expanded form.

subject: Math
description: Read, write and compare decimals to thousandths.
description_plain: Your child can read, write, and compare decimal numbers up to the thousandths place.

subject: Math
description:
Multiply and divide by powers of 10.
  • Explain patterns in the number of zeros of the product when multiplying a number by powers of 10.
  • Explain patterns in the placement of the decimal point when a decimal is multiplied or divided by a power of 10.
  • Use whole-number exponents to denote powers of 10.
description_plain: Your child can multiply and divide numbers by 10, 100, 1000, and explain how zeros and decimal points move in these problems.

subject: Math
description: Recognize that in a multi-digit number, a digit in one place represents 10 times as much as it represents in the place to its right and 1/10 of what it represents in the place to its left.
description_plain: Your child understands that in a multi-digit number, each digit is 10 times bigger than the digit to its right and one-tenth the size of the digit to its left.

subject: Math
description: Use tables, ordered pairs and graphs to represent the relationship between the quantities.
description_plain: Your child can use tables, ordered pairs, and graphs to show how two quantities are related.

subject: Math
description: Generate patterns using one or two given rules (x, y).
description_plain: Your child can create number patterns using one or two rules.

subject: Math
description: Generate a rule for growing patterns, identifying the relationship between corresponding terms (x, y).
description_plain: Your child can find a rule that explains how a growing pattern changes between numbers.

subject: Math
description: Generate numerical patterns for situations.
description_plain: Your child can make number patterns based on different situations.

subject: Math
description: Write simple expressions with numbers and interpret numerical expressions without evaluating them.
description_plain: Your child can write simple math expressions and understand what they mean without solving them.

subject: Math
description: Use parentheses, brackets or braces in numerical expressions and evaluate expressions that include symbols.
description_plain: Your child can use parentheses, brackets, or braces in math expressions and solve problems that include these symbols.

subject: Math
description: Look for and express regularity in repeated reasoning.
description_plain: Your child can notice and explain patterns or repeated steps when solving problems.

subject: Math
description: Look for and make use of structure.
description_plain: Your child can notice and use the way math problems are put together to help solve them.

subject: Math
description: Use appropriate tools strategically.
description_plain: Your child can choose and use the right tools, like calculators, rulers, or graphs, to help solve math problems.

subject: Math
description: Construct viable arguments and critique the reasoning of others.
description_plain: Your child can explain their math thinking and listen to and question the ideas of others.

subject: Math
description: Reason abstractly and quantitatively.
description_plain: Your child can think about math problems in different ways, using numbers and real-life examples.

subject: Math
description: Make sense of problems and persevere in solving them.
description_plain: Your child can understand what a math problem is asking and keep working until they find a solution.

subject: Math
description: Relating the choice of measures of center and variability to the shape of the data distribution.
description_plain: Your child can choose the best way to describe data based on how the data looks.

subject: Math
description: Describing distributions of numerical data qualitatively relating to shape (using terms such as cluster, mode(s), gap, symmetric, uniform, skewed-left, skewed-right and the presence of outliers) and quantitatively relating to spread/variability (using terms such as range and interquartile range).
description_plain: Your child can describe data by talking about its shape and how spread out the numbers are, using words like cluster, gap, or range.

subject: Math
description: Determining quantitative measures of center (median and/or mean) to describe distribution of numerical data.
description_plain: Your child can find the middle or average number to describe a set of data.

subject: Math
description: Describing the nature of the attribute under investigation, including how it was measured and its units of measurement.
description_plain: Your child can explain what kind of data was collected, how it was measured, and the units used.

subject: Math
description: Reporting the number of observations.
description_plain: Your child can tell how many pieces of data were collected.

subject: Math
description: Summarize numerical data sets in relation to their context, such as by:
description_plain: Your child can summarize data by describing what the numbers mean in the situation.

subject: Math
description: Display the distribution of numerical data in plots on a number line, including dot plots, histograms and box plots.
description_plain: Your child can show data on number lines using dot plots, histograms, and box plots.

subject: Math
description: Recognize that a measure of center for a numerical data set summarizes all of its values with a single number to describe a typical value, while a measure of variation describes how the values in the distribution vary.
description_plain: Your child understands that the center of data shows a typical value and variation shows how much the data changes.

subject: Math
description: Understand that a set of numerical data collected to answer a statistical question has a distribution which can be described by its center, spread and overall shape.
description_plain: Your child understands that data has a center, spread, and shape that describe it.

subject: Math
description: Recognize a statistical question as one that anticipates variability in the data related to the question and accounts for it in the answers.
description_plain: Your child knows that a good statistical question expects different answers and uses data to find them.

subject: Math
description: Analyze Data: Select appropriate graphical methods and numerical measures to analyze data by displaying variability within a group, comparing individual to individual and comparing individual to group.
description_plain: Your child can pick the right graphs and numbers to compare data within groups and between individuals.

subject: Math
description: Collect Data: Design and use a plan to collect appropriate data to answer a statistical question.
description_plain: Your child can plan and collect the right data to answer a question.

subject: Math
description: Formulate Questions: Formulate a statistical question as one that anticipates variability and can be answered with data.
description_plain: Your child can make a question that expects different answers and can be answered with data.

subject: Math
description: Apply the four-step investigative process for statistical reasoning.
description_plain: Your child can follow four steps to investigate and understand data.

subject: Math
description: Analyze the relationship between the dependent and independent variables using graphs and tables and relate these to the question.
description_plain: Your child can use graphs and tables to understand how one quantity changes when another changes.

subject: Math
description: Write an equation to express one quantity, thought of as the dependent variable, in terms of the other quantity, thought of as the independent variable.
description_plain: Your child can write an equation that shows how one quantity depends on another.

subject: Math
description: Appropriately recognize one quantity as the dependent variable and the other as the independent variable.
description_plain: Your child can tell which quantity depends on the other in a problem.

subject: Math
description: Use variables to represent two quantities in a real-world problem that changes in relationship to one another;
description_plain: Your child can use letters to show two related quantities in a real-world problem.

subject: Math
description: Write an inequality of the form x > c, x < c, x ≥ c, or x ≤ c to represent a constraint or condition in a real-world or mathematical problem. Recognize that inequalities of these forms have infinitely many solutions; represent solutions of such inequalities on vertical and horizontal number lines.
description_plain: Your child can write and solve inequalities like x > 5 to show limits in problems and can show these solutions on number lines.

subject: Math
description: Solve real-world and mathematical problems by writing and solving equations of the form x + p = q and px = q for cases in which p, q and x are all nonnegative rational numbers.
description_plain: Your child can solve real-world problems by writing and solving simple equations like x + 3 = 7.

subject: Math
description: Use variables to represent numbers and write expressions when solving a real-world or mathematical problem; understand that a variable can represent an unknown number, or depending on the purpose at hand, any number in a specified set.
description_plain: Your child can use letters to stand for numbers and write expressions to solve problems.

subject: Math
description: Understand solving an equation or inequality as a process of answering a question: which values from a specified set, if any, make the equation or inequality true? Use substitution to determine whether a given number in a specified set makes an equation or inequality true.
description_plain: Your child understands that solving an equation means finding which numbers make it true and can check answers by substituting numbers.

subject: Math
description: Identify when two expressions are equivalent when the two expressions name the same number regardless of which value is substituted into them.
description_plain: Your child can tell when two expressions are equal because they give the same result for any number.

subject: Math
description: Apply the properties of operations to generate equivalent expressions.
description_plain: Your child can use math rules to create expressions that mean the same thing.

subject: Math
description: Evaluate expressions for specific values of their variables, including values that are non-negative rational numbers. Include expressions that arise from formulas used in real-world problems. Perform arithmetic operations, including whole-number exponents, in the conventional order when there are no parentheses to specify a particular order (Order of Operations).
description_plain: Your child can calculate expressions with numbers and letters, including powers, following the correct order of operations.

subject: Math
description: Identify parts of an expression using mathematical terms (sums, term, product, factor, quotient, coefficient); view one or more parts of an expression in a single entity.
description_plain: Your child can identify parts of an expression like terms, factors, and coefficients.

subject: Math
description: Write expressions that record operations with numbers and with letters standing for numbers.
description_plain: Your child can write math expressions using numbers and letters to show operations.

subject: Math
description: Write, read and evaluate expressions in which letters stand for numbers.
description_plain: Your child can write, read, and solve expressions where letters stand for numbers.

subject: Math
description: Write and evaluate numerical expressions involving whole-number exponents.
description_plain: Your child can write and solve math expressions that include whole-number exponents.

subject: Math
description: Solve real-world and mathematical problems by graphing points in all four quadrants of the coordinate plane. Include use of coordinates and absolute value to find distances between points with the same first coordinate or the same second coordinate.
description_plain: Your child can solve problems by plotting points in all four parts of the coordinate plane and find distances using coordinates and absolute value.

subject: Math
description: Distinguish comparisons of absolute value from statements about order.
description_plain: Your child can tell the difference between comparing absolute values and comparing order of numbers.

subject: Math
description: Understand the absolute value of a rational number as its distance from 0 on the number line; interpret absolute value as magnitude for a positive or negative quantity in a real-world situation.
description_plain: Your child understands that absolute value means how far a number is from zero on a number line and can explain this distance in real-life situations.

subject: Math
description: Write, interpret and explain statements of order for rational numbers in real-world contexts.
description_plain: Your child can write and explain which rational numbers come before or after others in real-world examples.

subject: Math
description: Interpret statements of inequality as statements about the relative position of two numbers on a number line diagram.
description_plain: Your child understands that inequality statements show which number is bigger or smaller by looking at their positions on a number line.

subject: Math
description: Understand ordering and absolute value of rational numbers.
description_plain: Your child understands how to order rational numbers and knows what absolute value means.

subject: Math
description: Understand signs of numbers in ordered pairs as indicating locations in quadrants of the coordinate plane; recognize the similarity between whole numbers, their negative opposites and their positions on a number line, ordered pairs differ only by signs and their locations on one or both axes.
description_plain: Your child knows that the signs (+ or -) in number pairs tell where points are located in the four sections (quadrants) of a coordinate plane.

subject: Math
description: Find and position integers and other rational numbers on a horizontal or vertical number line diagram; find and position pairs of integers and other rational numbers on a coordinate plane.
description_plain: Your child can find and place whole numbers, negative numbers, and fractions on number lines and on a coordinate plane.

subject: Math
description: Recognize opposite signs of numbers as indicating locations on opposite sides of 0 on the number line; recognize 0 is its own opposite and the opposite of a positive number is a negative, and the opposite of a negative number is a positive, such as−(−3)=3.
description_plain: Your child understands that numbers with opposite signs are on opposite sides of zero on a number line and that zero is its own opposite.

subject: Math
description: Understand a rational number as a point on the number line. Extend number line diagrams and coordinate axes, using appropriate range and intervals, to represent points on the line and in the plane, that include negative numbers and coordinates.
description_plain: Your child understands that rational numbers are points on a number line and can use number lines and coordinate planes to show positive and negative numbers.

subject: Math
description: Understand that positive and negative numbers are used together to describe quantities having opposite directions or values; use positive and negative numbers to represent quantities in real-world contexts, explaining the meaning of 0 in each situation.
description_plain: Your child understands that positive and negative numbers can show opposite directions or values and can explain what zero means in different real-life situations.

subject: Math
description: Use the distributive property to express a sum of two whole numbers 1 – 100 with a common factor as a multiple of a sum of two whole numbers with no common factor.
description_plain: Your child can use the distributive property to rewrite sums of two whole numbers with common factors as multiples of simpler sums.

subject: Math
description: Fluently add, subtract, multiply and divide multi-digit decimals using an algorithm for each operation.
description_plain: Your child can quickly and accurately add, subtract, multiply, and divide decimals with many digits using step-by-step methods.

subject: Math
description: Know that the decimal form of a rational number terminates in 0s or eventually repeats.
description_plain: Your child knows that when a rational number is written as a decimal, it either ends with zeros or has digits that repeat over and over.

subject: Math
description: Convert a rational number to a decimal using long division.
description_plain: Your child can change a fraction or rational number into a decimal by dividing the numbers longhand.

subject: Math
description: Fluently divide multi-digit numbers using an algorithm.
description_plain: Your child can divide large numbers using a step-by-step method.

subject: Math
description: Interpret and compute quotients of fractions and solve word problems involving division of fractions by fractions.
description_plain: Your child can divide fractions by other fractions and solve word problems that involve dividing fractions.

subject: Math
description: Use ratio reasoning to convert measurement units; manipulate and transform units appropriately when multiplying or dividing quantities.
description_plain: Your child can use ratios to change measurement units and correctly adjust units when multiplying or dividing.

subject: Math
description: Solve rate problems including those involving unit pricing and constant speed.
description_plain: Your child can solve problems about rates, like finding unit prices or calculating constant speeds.

subject: Math
description: Make tables of equivalent ratios relating quantities with whole-number measurements, find missing values in the tables and plot the pairs of values on the coordinate plane. Use tables to compare ratios.
description_plain: Your child can make tables showing equivalent ratios with whole numbers, find missing numbers, and plot these pairs on a graph to compare ratios.

subject: Math
description: Use ratio and rate reasoning to solve real-world and mathematical problems.
description_plain: Your child can use ratios and rates to solve real-life and math problems.

subject: Math
description: Understand the concept of a unit rate a/b associated with a ratio a:b with B ≠ 0 and use rate language in the context of a ratio relationship.
description_plain: Your child understands what a unit rate is and can talk about rates using correct language.

subject: Math
description: Understand the concept of a ratio and use ratio language to describe a ratio relationship between two quantities.
description_plain: Your child understands what a ratio is and can use words to describe the relationship between two amounts.

subject: Math
description: Classify three-dimensional figures including cubes, prisms, pyramids, cones and spheres.
description_plain: Your child can identify and sort 3D shapes like cubes, prisms, pyramids, cones, and spheres.

subject: Math
description: Draw polygons in the coordinate plane given coordinates for the vertices; use coordinates to find the length of a side joining points with the same first coordinate or the same second coordinate. Apply these techniques in the context of solving real-world and mathematical problems.
description_plain: Your child can draw shapes on a coordinate plane using points and find the length of sides when points share an x- or y-coordinate, using these skills to solve problems.

subject: Math
description: Find the volume of a right rectangular prism with rational number edge lengths. Apply the formulas V = lwh and V = Bh to find volumes of right rectangular prisms with rational number edge lengths in the context of solving real-world and mathematical problems.
description_plain: Your child can find the volume of rectangular boxes with edges that are whole or fractional numbers, using formulas to solve problems.

subject: Math
description: Find the area of right triangles, other triangles, special quadrilaterals and polygons by composing into rectangles or decomposing into triangles and quadrilaterals; apply these techniques in the context of solving real-world and mathematical problems.
description_plain: Your child can find the area of triangles, special four-sided shapes, and other polygons by breaking them into rectangles and triangles, and use this to solve problems.

subject: Math
description: Look for and express regularity in repeated reasoning.
description_plain: Your child can notice and explain patterns or repeated steps when solving problems.

subject: Math
description: Look for and make use of structure.
description_plain: Your child can notice and use the way math problems are put together to help solve them.

subject: Math
description: Use appropriate tools strategically.
description_plain: Your child can choose and use the right tools, like calculators, rulers, or graphs, to help solve math problems.

subject: Math
description: Construct viable arguments and critique the reasoning of others.
description_plain: Your child can explain their math thinking and listen to and question the ideas of others.

subject: Math
description: Reason abstractly and quantitatively.
description_plain: Your child can think about math problems in different ways, using numbers and real-life examples.

subject: Math
description: Make sense of problems and persevere in solving them.
description_plain: Your child can understand what a math problem is asking and keep working until they find a solution.

subject: Math
description: Design and use a simulation to generate frequencies for compound events.
description_plain: Your child can create and use simulations to find how often combined events happen.

subject: Math
description: Represent sample spaces for compound events described in everyday language using methods such as organized lists, tables and tree diagrams.
description_plain: Your child can show all possible outcomes for combined events using lists, tables, or tree diagrams.

subject: Math
description: Explain just as with simple events, the probability of a compound event is the fraction of outcomes in the sample space for which the compound event occurs.
description_plain: Your child understands that the chance of combined events is the fraction of all possible outcomes where those events happen.

subject: Math
description: Find probabilities of compound events using organized lists, tables, tree diagrams and simulation.
description_plain: Your child can find the chances of combined events using organized lists, tables, tree diagrams, and simulations.

subject: Math
description: Develop a probability model (which may not be uniform) by observing frequencies in data generated from a chance process.
description_plain: Your child can create a probability model based on data from chance events, even if outcomes are not equally likely.

subject: Math
description: Develop a uniform probability model by assigning equal probability to all outcomes and use the model to determine probabilities of events.
description_plain: Your child can create a probability model where all outcomes are equally likely and use it to find chances of events.

subject: Math
description: Develop a probability model and use it to find probabilities of events. Compare probabilities from a model to observed frequencies; if the agreement is not good, explain possible sources of the discrepancy.
description_plain: Your child can make a probability model, use it to find chances of events, and compare these to actual results, explaining differences.

subject: Math
description: Approximate the probability of a chance event by collecting data on the chance process that produces it and observing its long-run relative frequency and predict the approximate relative frequency given the probability.
description_plain: Your child can estimate the chance of an event by collecting data and observing how often it happens over time.

subject: Math
description: Describe the probability of a chance event is a number between 0 and 1, which tells how likely the event is, from impossible (0) to certain (1). A probability near 0 indicates an unlikely event, a probability around ½ indicates an event that is neither unlikely nor likely and a probability near 1 indicates a likely event.
description_plain: Your child understands that probability is a number between 0 and 1 that shows how likely an event is, from impossible (0) to certain (1).

subject: Math
description: Calculate and use measures of center (mean and median) and measures of variability (interquartile range when comparing medians and mean absolute deviation when comparing means) for numerical data from random samples to draw informal comparative inferences about two populations.
description_plain: Your child can calculate and use averages and measures of spread to compare two groups of numbers and make simple conclusions.

subject: Math
description: Describe the degree of visual overlap (and separation) from the graphical representations of two numerical data distributions (box plots, dot plots) with similar variabilities with similar contexts (same variable), measuring the difference between the centers (medians or means) by expressing this difference as a multiple of a measure of variability (interquartile range when comparing medians or the mean absolute deviation when comparing means).
description_plain: Your child can describe how much two sets of data overlap or differ by looking at graphs and comparing their centers and spreads.

subject: Math
description: Gauge how far off an estimate or prediction might be related to a population character of interest.
description_plain: Your child can estimate how much an answer might be off when making predictions about a group.

subject: Math
description: Generate multiple samples (or simulated samples) of numerical data to gauge the variation in estimates or predictions.
description_plain: Your child can create many samples of number data to see how much predictions might vary.

subject: Math
description: Generate multiple samples of categorical data of the same size to gauge the variation in estimates or predictions.
description_plain: Your child can create many samples of category data to understand how predictions might change.

subject: Math
description: Use data from a random sample to draw inferences about a population with an unknown characteristic of interest.
description_plain: Your child can use data from a random sample to make guesses about a larger group when the exact information is unknown.

subject: Math
description: Understand that statistics can be used to gain information about a population by examining a sample of the population; generalizations about a population from a sample are valid only if the sample is representative of that population. Understand that random sampling tends to produce representative samples and support valid inferences.
description_plain: Your child understands that looking at a small group (sample) can help learn about a bigger group (population), but the small group must fairly represent the bigger one, and random picking helps make a fair sample.

subject: Math
description: Create displays, including circle graphs (pie charts), scaled pictographs and bar graphs, to compare and analyze distributions of categorical data from both matching and different-sized samples.
description_plain: Your child can make and use pie charts, pictographs, and bar graphs to compare and understand different groups of data.

subject: Math
description: Solve word problems leading to inequalities of the form px + q > r, px + q < r, px + q ≥ r, px + q ≤ r, where p, q and r are specific rational numbers. Graph the solution set of the inequality and interpret it in context of the problem.
description_plain: Your child can solve word problems that use inequalities like greater than or less than, draw the solutions on a number line, and explain what the answers mean.

subject: Math
description: Solve word problems leading to equations of the form px + q = r and p(x + q) = r, where p, q and r are specific rational numbers. Solve equations of these forms. Graph the solution set of the equality and interpret it in context of the problem.
description_plain: Your child can solve word problems with equations like px + q = r, graph the solutions, and explain what the answers mean in the problem.

subject: Math
description: Use variables to represent quantities in a real-world or mathematical problem and construct equations and inequalities to solve problems by reasoning about the quantities.
description_plain: Your child can use letters to stand for numbers in real problems and write equations or inequalities to find answers by thinking about the quantities.

subject: Math
description: Solve real-life and mathematical problems posed with positive and negative rational numbers in any form, using tools strategically. Apply properties of operations to calculate with numbers in any form; convert between forms as appropriate; and assess the reasonableness of answers using mental computation and estimation strategies.
description_plain: Your child can solve problems using positive and negative numbers, choose the right tools, change numbers between forms, and check if answers make sense by estimating.

subject: Math
description: Understand that rewriting an expression in different forms in a problem context can clarify the problem and how the quantities in it are related.
description_plain: Your child understands that changing how an expression looks can make a problem easier to understand and show how the numbers relate.

subject: Math
description: Apply properties of operations as strategies to add, subtract, factor and expand linear expressions with rational coefficients.
description_plain: Your child can add, subtract, factor, and expand simple expressions with numbers using rules for operations.

subject: Math
description: Solve real-world and mathematical problems involving the four operations with rational numbers.
description_plain: Your child can solve real-life and math problems using addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division with rational numbers.

subject: Math
description: Apply properties of operations as strategies to multiply and divide rational numbers.
description_plain: Your child can multiply and divide rational numbers using operation rules.

subject: Math
description: Understand that integers can be divided, provided that the divisor is not zero and every quotient of integers (with non-zero divisor) is a rational number. If p and q are integers, then –(p/q) = (–p)/q = p/(–q). Interpret quotients of rational numbers by describing real-world contexts.
description_plain: Your child understands that dividing whole numbers works as long as you don’t divide by zero, and the answers are rational numbers; they can explain division with real examples.

subject: Math
description: Understand that multiplication is extended from fractions to rational numbers by requiring that operations continue to satisfy the properties of operations, particularly the distributive property, leading to products such as (–1)(–1) = 1 and the rules for multiplying signed numbers. Interpret products of rational numbers by describing real-world contexts.
description_plain: Your child understands how multiplying fractions extends to all rational numbers, including rules for multiplying negatives, and can explain multiplication with real examples.

subject: Math
description: Apply and extend previous understandings of multiplication and division and of fractions to multiply and divide rational numbers.
description_plain: Your child can multiply and divide rational numbers by building on what they know about fractions.

subject: Math
description: Apply properties of operations as strategies to add and subtract rational numbers.
description_plain: Your child can add and subtract rational numbers using operation rules.

subject: Math
description: Understand subtraction of rational numbers as adding the additive inverse, p - q = p + (-q). Show that the distance between two rational numbers on the number line is the absolute value of their difference and apply this principle in real-world contexts.
description_plain: Your child understands that subtracting a number is the same as adding its opposite and can find the distance between numbers on a number line, using this idea in real situations.

subject: Math
description: Understand p + q as the number located a distance |q| from p, in the positive or negative direction depending on whether q is positive or negative. Show that a number and its opposite have a sum of 0 (are additive inverses). Interpret sums of rational numbers by describing real-world contexts.
description_plain: Your child understands adding a positive or negative number moves along the number line and knows that a number plus its opposite equals zero; they can explain this with real examples.

subject: Math
description: Describe situations in which opposite quantities combine to make 0.
description_plain: Your child can describe situations where opposite amounts cancel each other out to make zero.

subject: Math
description: Apply and extend previous understandings of addition and subtraction to add and subtract rational numbers; represent addition and subtraction on a horizontal or vertical number line diagram.
description_plain: Your child can add and subtract rational numbers and show these operations on a number line going left-right or up-down.

subject: Math
description: Use proportional relationships to solve multistep ratio and percent problems.
description_plain: Your child can solve multi-step problems involving ratios and percents using proportional relationships.

subject: Math
description: Find a percent of a quantity as a rate per 100; solve problems involving finding the whole, a part and a percent, given two of these.
description_plain: Your child can find a percent of a number as parts per 100 and solve problems when given two of the whole, part, or percent.

subject: Math
description: Use percents to solve mathematical and real-world problems.
description_plain: Your child can use percents to solve math and real-life problems.

subject: Math
description: Explain what a point (x, y) on the graph of a proportional relationship means in terms of the situation, with special attention to the points (0, 0) and (1, r) where r is the unit rate.
description_plain: Your child can explain what points on a graph mean in a proportional relationship, especially the points (0,0) and (1, unit rate).

subject: Math
description: Represent proportional relationships by equations.
description_plain: Your child can write equations that show proportional relationships.

subject: Math
description: Identify the constant of proportionality (unit rate) in tables, graphs, equations, diagrams and verbal descriptions of proportional relationships.
description_plain: Your child can find the unit rate (constant ratio) in tables, graphs, equations, pictures, and words.

subject: Math
description: Decide whether two quantities represent a proportional relationship.
description_plain: Your child can decide if two amounts have a proportional relationship.

subject: Math
description: Recognize and represent proportional relationships between quantities.
description_plain: Your child can recognize and show proportional relationships between amounts.

subject: Math
description: Compute unit rates associated with ratios of fractions, including ratios of lengths, areas and other quantities measured in like or different units.
description_plain: Your child can find unit rates for ratios involving fractions, like lengths or areas, even when units are different.

subject: Math
description: Solve real-world and mathematical problems involving volume and surface area, using nets as needed, of three-dimensional objects including cubes, pyramids and right prisms.
description_plain: Your child can solve problems about volume and surface area of 3D shapes like cubes, pyramids, and prisms, using nets when needed.

subject: Math
description: Solve real-world and mathematical problems involving area of two-dimensional objects composed of triangles, quadrilaterals and other polygons.
description_plain: Your child can solve problems about the area of 2D shapes made of triangles, quadrilaterals, and other polygons.

subject: Math
description: Solve problems involving area of two-dimensional objects and surface area and volume of three-dimensional objects.
description_plain: Your child can solve problems about area of flat shapes and surface area and volume of 3D shapes.

subject: Math
description: Apply properties of supplementary, complementary, vertical and adjacent angles in a multi-step problem to write and solve simple equations for an unknown angle in a figure.
description_plain: Your child can use angle rules about supplementary, complementary, vertical, and adjacent angles to write and solve equations for unknown angles.

subject: Math
description: Explore and understand the relationship between the radius, diameter, circumference and area of a circle.
description_plain: Your child understands how the radius, diameter, circumference, and area of a circle are related.

subject: Math
description: Apply the formulas for the area and circumference of a circle to solve real-world and mathematical problems.
description_plain: Your child can use formulas for the area and circumference of circles to solve real and math problems.

subject: Math
description: Use formulas for area and circumference of circles and their relationships.
description_plain: Your child can use and understand formulas for circle area and circumference and how they connect.

subject: Math
description: Describe the two-dimensional figures that result from slicing three-dimensional figures, as in plane sections of right rectangular prisms and right rectangular pyramids.
description_plain: Your child can describe the flat shapes made when slicing 3D shapes like rectangular prisms and pyramids.

subject: Math
description: Draw (freehand, with ruler and protractor and with technology) geometric shapes with given conditions. Focus on constructing triangles from three measures of angles or sides, noticing when the conditions determine a unique triangle, more than one triangle, or no triangle.
description_plain: Your child can draw shapes by hand, with tools, or technology using given measurements, especially triangles, and understand when the measurements make one, more than one, or no triangle.

subject: Math
description: Solve problems involving scale drawings of geometric figures, including computing actual lengths and areas from a scale drawing and reproducing a scale drawing at a different scale.
description_plain: Your child can solve problems using scale drawings, including finding real lengths and areas and making new scale drawings.

subject: Math
description: Look for and express regularity in repeated reasoning.
description_plain: Your child can notice and explain patterns or repeated steps when solving problems.

subject: Math
description: Look for and make use of structure.
description_plain: Your child can notice and use the way math problems are put together to help solve them.

subject: Math
description: Use appropriate tools strategically.
description_plain: Your child can choose and use the right tools, like calculators, rulers, or graphs, to help solve math problems.

subject: Math
description: Construct viable arguments and critique the reasoning of others.
description_plain: Your child can explain their math thinking and listen to and question the ideas of others.

subject: Math
description: Reason abstractly and quantitatively.
description_plain: Your child can think about math problems in different ways, using numbers and real-life examples.

subject: Math
description: Make sense of problems and persevere in solving them.
description_plain: Your child can understand what a math problem is asking and keep working until they find a solution.

subject: Math
description: Use the equation of a linear model to solve problems in the context of bivariate numerical data, interpreting the slope and intercept.
description_plain: Your child can use a line equation to solve problems with two related numbers and explain what the slope and intercept mean.

subject: Math
description: Know that lines are widely used to model relationships between two quantitative variables. For scatter plots that suggest a linear association, informally fit a line and informally assess the model fit by judging the closeness of the data points to the line.
description_plain: Your child knows that lines often show relationships between two numbers and can draw a line that fits data points on a scatter plot and judge how well it fits.

subject: Math
description: Construct and interpret scatter plots for bivariate numerical data to investigate patterns of association between two quantities. Describe patterns such as clustering, outliers, positive or negative association, linear association and nonlinear association.
description_plain: Your child can make and understand scatter plots to find patterns like clusters, outliers, and types of relationships between two sets of numbers.

subject: Math
description: Sketch a graph that exhibits the qualitative features of a function that has been described verbally.
description_plain: Your child can draw a graph that shows the main features of a function described in words.

subject: Math
description: Describe qualitatively the functional relationship between two quantities by analyzing a graph.
description_plain: Your child can explain how two quantities are related by looking at a graph.

subject: Math
description: Use graphs to represent functions.
description_plain: Your child can use graphs to show how one quantity depends on another.

subject: Math
description: Interpret the rate of change and initial value of a linear function in terms of the situation it models and in terms of its graph or a table of values.
description_plain: Your child can explain what the rate of change and starting value mean in a real situation, using a graph or table.

subject: Math
description: Determine the rate of change and initial value of the function from a description of a relationship or from two (x, y) values, including reading these from a table or from a graph.
description_plain: Your child can find the rate of change and starting value from a description, two points, a table, or a graph.

subject: Math
description: Construct a function to model a linear relationship between two quantities.
description_plain: Your child can create a function that shows a straight-line relationship between two amounts.

subject: Math
description: Identify and give examples of functions that are not linear.
description_plain: Your child can recognize and give examples of functions that are not straight lines.

subject: Math
description: Interpret the equation y = mx + b as defining a linear function, whose graph is a straight line.
description_plain: Your child understands that the equation y = mx + b makes a straight-line graph.

subject: Math
description: Understand properties of linear functions.
description_plain: Your child understands the main features of straight-line functions.

subject: Math
description: Compare properties of two functions each represented in a different way (algebraically, graphically, numerically in tables, or by verbal descriptions).
description_plain: Your child can compare two functions shown in different ways, like equations, graphs, tables, or words.

subject: Math
description: Understand that a function is a rule that assigns to each input exactly one output. The graph of a function is the set of ordered pairs consisting of an input and the corresponding output.
description_plain: Your child understands that a function gives one output for each input, and its graph shows these input-output pairs.

subject: Math
description: Solve real-world and mathematical problems leading to two linear equations in two variables.
description_plain: Your child can solve real-life and math problems that involve two linear equations with two variables.

subject: Math
description: Solve systems of two linear equations in two variables algebraically by using substitution where at least one equation contains at least one variable whose coefficient is 1 and by inspection for simple cases
description_plain: Your child can solve two linear equations with two variables using substitution or by looking for simple solutions.

subject: Math
description: Understand that solutions to a system of two linear equations in two variables correspond to points of intersection of their graphs, because points of intersection satisfy both equations simultaneously; understand that a system of two linear equations may have one solution, no solution, or infinitely many solutions.
description_plain: Your child understands that solutions to two linear equations are where their graphs cross, and that there can be one, none, or many solutions.

subject: Math
description: Analyze and solve a system of two linear equations.
description_plain: Your child can analyze and solve two linear equations together.

subject: Math
description: Solve linear equations with rational number coefficients, including equations whose solutions require expanding expressions using the distributive property and combining like terms.
description_plain: Your child can solve linear equations with fractions or decimals, including those needing distribution and combining like terms.

subject: Math
description: Give examples of linear equations in one variable with one solution, infinitely many solutions, or no solutions. Show which of these possibilities is the case by successively transforming the given equation into simpler forms, until an equivalent equation of the form x = a, a = a, or a = b results (where a and b are different numbers).
description_plain: Your child can show examples of linear equations that have one solution, many solutions, or no solutions by simplifying the equations.

subject: Math
description: Solve linear equations in one variable.
description_plain: Your child can solve linear equations with one variable.

subject: Math
description: Use similar triangles to explain why the slope, m, is the same between any two distinct points on a non-vertical line in the coordinate plane; know the equation y = mx for a line through the origin and the equation y = mx + b for a line intercepting the vertical axis at b.
description_plain: Your child can explain why the slope is the same between any two points on a line and knows the equations for lines through the origin and with a vertical intercept.

subject: Math
description: Graph proportional relationships, interpreting the unit rate as the slope of the graph. Compare two different proportional relationships represented in different ways.
description_plain: Your child can graph proportional relationships, understand the slope as the unit rate, and compare different proportional relationships.

subject: Math
description: Perform operations with numbers expressed in scientific notation, including problems where both decimal and scientific notation are used. Use scientific notation and choose units of appropriate size for measurements of very large or very small quantities. Interpret scientific notation that has been generated by technology.
description_plain: Your child can work with numbers in scientific notation, choose suitable units for very big or small measurements, and understand scientific notation from technology.

subject: Math
description: Use numbers expressed in the form of a single digit times an integer power of 10 (Scientific Notation) to estimate very large or very small quantities and express how many times larger or smaller one is than the other.
description_plain: Your child can use scientific notation to estimate very large or very small numbers and compare their sizes.

subject: Math
description: Use square root and cube root symbols to represent solutions to equations of the form x² = p and x³ = p, where p is a positive rational number. Evaluate square roots of small perfect squares and cube roots of small perfect cubes. Know that perfect squares and perfect cubes are rational.
description_plain: Your child can use square roots and cube roots to solve equations and calculate roots of small perfect squares and cubes.

subject: Math
description: Know and apply the properties of integer exponents to generate equivalent numerical expressions.
description_plain: Your child knows and uses the rules of exponents to rewrite numerical expressions.

subject: Math
description: Use rational approximations of irrational numbers to compare the size of irrational numbers, locate them approximately on a number line diagram and estimate the value of expressions.
description_plain: Your child can use decimal approximations to compare and estimate irrational numbers on a number line.

subject: Math
description: Understand informally that every number has a decimal expansion; the rational numbers are those with decimal expansions that terminate in 0s or eventually repeat. Know that other numbers are called irrational.
description_plain: Your child understands that every number can be written as a decimal, with rational numbers ending or repeating, and others called irrational.

subject: Math
description: Apply the formulas for the volumes and surface areas of cones, cylinders and spheres and use them to solve real-world and mathematical problems.
description_plain: Your child can use formulas to find the volume and surface area of cones, cylinders, and spheres to solve problems.

subject: Math
description: Apply the Pythagorean Theorem to find the distance between two points in a coordinate system.
description_plain: Your child can use the Pythagorean Theorem to find the distance between two points on a graph.

subject: Math
description: Apply the Pythagorean Theorem to determine unknown side lengths in right triangles in real-world and mathematical problems in two and three dimensions.
description_plain: Your child can use the Pythagorean Theorem to find missing side lengths in right triangles in real-life and math problems.

subject: Math
description: Explain a proof of the Pythagorean Theorem and its converse.
description_plain: Your child can explain why the Pythagorean Theorem and its reverse are true.

subject: Math
description: Use informal arguments to establish facts about the angle sum and exterior angle of triangles, about the angles created when parallel lines are cut by a transversal and the angle-angle criterion for similarity of triangles.
description_plain: Your child can use simple reasoning to understand facts about triangle angles, angles made by parallel lines, and when triangles are similar.

subject: Math
description: Understand that a two-dimensional figure is similar to another if the second can be obtained from the first by a sequence of rotations, reflections, translations and dilations. Given two similar two-dimensional figures, describe a sequence that exhibits the similarity between them.
description_plain: Your child understands that two shapes are similar if one can be changed into the other by moving, flipping, or resizing, and can describe how.

subject: Math
description: Describe the effect of dilations, translations, rotations and reflections on two-dimensional figures using coordinates.
description_plain: Your child can describe how moving, flipping, rotating, or resizing shapes changes their position using coordinates.

subject: Math
description: Understand that a two-dimensional figure is congruent to another if the second can be obtained from the first by a sequence of rotations, reflections and translations. Given two congruent figures, describe a sequence that exhibits the congruence between them.
description_plain: Your child understands that two shapes are congruent if one can be moved or flipped to match the other, and can explain how.

subject: Math
description:
Verify experimentally the properties of rotations, reflections and translations:
  • Lines are congruent to lines.
  • Line segments are congruent to line segments of the same length.
  • Angles are congruent to angles of the same measure.
  • Parallel lines are congruent to parallel lines.
description_plain: Your child can test and confirm that moving or flipping shapes keeps lines, segments, angles, and parallel lines the same.

subject: Math
description: Look for and express regularity in repeated reasoning.
description_plain: Your child can notice and explain patterns or repeated steps when solving problems.

subject: Math
description: Look for and make use of structure.
description_plain: Your child can notice and use the way math problems are put together to help solve them.

subject: Math
description: Use appropriate tools strategically.
description_plain: Your child can choose and use the right tools, like calculators, rulers, or graphs, to help solve math problems.

subject: Math
description: Construct viable arguments and critique the reasoning of others.
description_plain: Your child can explain their math thinking and listen to and question the ideas of others.

subject: Math
description: Reason abstractly and quantitatively.

subject: Math
description: Make sense of problems and persevere in solving them.
description_plain: Your child can understand what a math problem is asking and keep working until they find a solution.

description: Analyze how economic choices were made based on scarcity.

subject: Social Studies

subject: Social Studies
description:
Analyze the impact of fundamental documents and speeches on the development of the United States from 1600-1877 that shall include but are not limited to:
  • The Mayflower Compact;
  • The Declaration of Independence;
  • The Constitution of the United States;
  • The Federalist No. 1 (Alexander Hamilton);
  • The Federalist Nos. 10 and 51 (James Madison);
  • The June 8, 1789 speech on amendments to the Constitution of the United States by James Madison;
  • The first ten (10) amendments to the Constitution of the United States, also known as the Bill of Rights;
  • The 1796 Farewell Address by George Washington;
  • The United States Supreme Court opinion in Marbury v. Madison, 5 U.S. 137 (1803);
  • The Monroe Doctrine by James Monroe;
  • What to the Slave is the Fourth of July? speech by Frederick Douglass;
  • The United States Supreme Court opinion in Dred Scott v. Sanford, 60 U.S. 393 (1857);
  • Final Emancipation Proclamation by Abraham Lincoln;
  • The Gettysburg Address by Abraham Lincoln; and
  • Declaration of Rights of the Women of the United States by Susan B. Anthony, Matilda Joselyn Gage, and Elizabeth Cady Stanton.
description_plain: Your child can analyze how important documents and speeches, like the Declaration of Independence and the Bill of Rights, influenced the U.S. from 1600 to 1877.

subject: Social Studies

description_plain: Your child can explain political, geographic, social, and economic changes and similarities in different U.S. regions between 1860 and 1877.

subject: Social Studies
description:
Evaluate the impact of fundamental documents and speeches on the development of the United States from 1877 to present that shall include but are not limited to:
  • The September 18, 1895, Atlanta Exposition Address by Booker T. Washington;
  • Of Booker T. Washington and Others by W.E.B. Du Bois;
  • The United States Supreme Court opinion in Plessy v. Ferguson, 163 U.S. 537 (1896);
  • The August 31, 1910, New Nationalism speech by Theodore Roosevelt;
  • The January 11, 1944, State of the Union Address by Franklin D. Roosevelt;
  • The United States Supreme Court opinions in Brown v. Board of Education of Topeka, 347 U.S. 483 (1954) and Brown v. Board of Education of Topeka, 349 U.S. 294 (1955);
  • Letter from Birmingham Jail by Martin Luther King, Jr.;
  • The August 28, 1963, I Have a Dream speech by Martin Luther King, Jr.; and
  • A Time for Choosing by Ronald Reagan.
description_plain: Your child can explain how important speeches and documents, like those by Booker T. Washington, Martin Luther King Jr., and Supreme Court cases, shaped the U.S. from 1877 to today.

subject: Science
description: Optimizing the Design Solution - Because there is always more than one possible solution to a problem, it is useful to compare and test designs.
description_plain: Your child understands that there are many ways to solve a problem and can compare and test different designs.

subject: Science
description: Analyzing and Interpreting Data - Analyze data from tests of an object or tool to determine if it works as intended.
description_plain: Your child can look at test results to see if an object or tool works the way it should.

subject: Science
description: Analyze data from tests of two objects designed to solve the same problem to compare the strengths and weaknesses of how each performs.
description_plain: Your child can look at test results from two objects made to solve the same problem and talk about which one works better and why.

subject: Science
description: Structure and Function - The shape and stability of structures of natural and designed objects are related to their function(s).
description_plain: Your child understands that the shape and strength of things relate to what they do.

subject: Science
description: Developing Possible Solutions - Designs can be conveyed through sketches, drawings, or physical models. These representations are useful in communicating ideas for a problem's solutions to other people.
description_plain: Your child can draw or build simple models to show ideas for solving problems and share them with others.

subject: Science
description: Developing and Using Models - Develop a simple model based on evidence to represent a proposed object or tool.
description_plain: Your child can make a simple model based on evidence to show how an object or tool might work.

subject: Science
description: Develop a simple sketch, drawing, or physical model to illustrate how the shape of an object helps it function as needed to solve a given problem.
description_plain: Your child can draw or build a simple model to show how the shape of something helps it do its job.

subject: Science
description: Defining and Delimiting Engineering Problems - A situation that people want to change or create can be approached as a problem to be solved through engineering. Asking questions, making observations, and gathering information are helpful in thinking about problems. Before beginning to design a solution, it is important to clearly understand the problem.
description_plain: Your child understands how to ask questions and gather information to clearly understand a problem before trying to solve it.

subject: Science
description: Asking Questions and Defining Problems - Ask questions based on observations to find more information about the natural and/or designed world(s). Define a simple problem that can be solved through the development of a new or improved object or tool.
description_plain: Your child can ask questions about the world and define simple problems that can be solved by making new or better tools.

subject: Science
description: Ask questions, make observations, and gather information about a situation that people want to change to define a simple problem that can be solved through the development of a new or improved object or tool.
description_plain: Your child can ask questions, observe, and gather information to understand a problem that needs a new or improved tool.

subject: Science
description: Cause and Effect - Events have causes that generate observable patterns.
description_plain: Your child understands that events happen for reasons and create patterns we can see.

subject: Science
description: Developing Possible Solutions - Designs can be conveyed through sketches, drawings, or physical models. These representations are useful in communicating ideas for a problem's solutions to other people.
description_plain: Your child can use drawings or models to share ideas for solving problems with others.

subject: Science
description: Human Impacts on Earth Systems - Things that people do to live comfortably can affect the world around them. But they can make choices that reduce their impacts on the land, water, air, and other living things.
description_plain: Your child understands that what people do affects the Earth, and they can make choices to protect land, water, air, and living things.

subject: Science
description: Obtaining, Evaluating, and Communicating Information - Communicate solutions with others in oral and/or written forms using models and/or drawings that provide detail about scientific ideas.
description_plain: Your child can explain their solutions to others using words, drawings, or models that show scientific ideas.

subject: Science
description: Communicate solutions that will reduce the impact of humans on the land, water, air, and/or other living things in the local environment.
description_plain: Your child can share ideas to help reduce how people harm the land, water, air, or living things nearby.

subject: Science
description: Cause and Effect - Events have causes that generate observable patterns.
description_plain: Your child understands that events happen for reasons and create patterns we can see.

subject: Science
description: Defining and Delimiting an Engineering Problem - Asking questions, making observations, and gathering information are helpful in thinking about problems.
description_plain: Your child knows that asking questions and making observations help us understand problems.

subject: Science
description: Natural Hazards - Some kinds of severe weather are more likely than others in a given region. Weather scientists forecast severe weather so that the communities can prepare for and respond to these events.
description_plain: Your child understands that some kinds of bad weather happen more often in certain places and that weather experts warn people so they can be ready.

subject: Science
description: Obtaining, Evaluating and Communicating Information - Read grade-appropriate texts and/or use media to obtain scientific information to describe patterns in the natural world.
description_plain: Your child can read or watch age-appropriate materials to learn about patterns in nature.

subject: Science
description: Asking Questions and Defining Problems - Ask questions based on observations to find more information about the designed world.
description_plain: Your child can ask questions based on what they see to learn more about things people have made.

subject: Science
description: Ask questions to obtain information about the purpose of weather forecasting to prepare for, and respond to, severe weather.
description_plain: Your child can ask questions to learn why weather forecasts help people get ready for bad weather.

subject: Science
description: Systems and System Models - Systems in the natural and designed world have parts that work together.
description_plain: Your child understands that natural and man-made systems have parts that work together.

subject: Science
description: Natural Resources - Living things need water, air, and resources from the land, and they live in places that have the things they need. Humans use natural resources for everything they do.
description_plain: Your child understands that living things need water, air, and resources from the land, and people use these natural resources for many things.

subject: Science
description: Developing and Using Models - Use a model to represent relationships in the natural world.
description_plain: Your child can use simple models to show how things in nature are connected.

subject: Science
description: Use a model to represent the relationship between the needs of different plants or animals (including humans) and the places they live.
description_plain: Your child will use a model to show how different plants and animals (including people) need certain things and places to live.

subject: Science
description: Systems and System Models - Systems in the natural and designed world have parts that work together.
description_plain: Your child understands that natural and man-made systems have parts that work together.

subject: Science
description: Human Impacts on Earth Systems - Things that people do to live comfortably can affect the world around them. But they can make choices that reduce their impacts on the land, water, air, and other living things.
description_plain: Your child understands that what people do to live can affect the Earth, and they can make choices to protect land, water, air, and living things.

subject: Science
description: Biogeology - Plants and animals can change their environment.
description_plain: Your child knows that plants and animals can change the places where they live.

subject: Science
description: Engaging in Argument from Evidence - Construct an argument with evidence to support a claim.
description_plain: Your child can explain their ideas by using evidence to support what they think.

subject: Science
description: Construct an argument supported by evidence for how plants and animals (including humans) can change the environment to meet their needs.
description_plain: Your child can explain with examples how plants and animals (including people) change their surroundings to meet their needs.

subject: Science
description: Cause and Effect - Patterns in the natural world can be observed, used to describe phenomena, and used as evidence.
description_plain: Your child can notice patterns in nature and use them to explain what happens.

subject: Science
description: Weather and Climate - Weather is the combination of sunlight, wind, snow or rain, and temperature in a particular region at a particular time. People measure these conditions to describe and record the weather and to notice patterns over time.
description_plain: Your child understands that weather is made up of sunlight, wind, rain or snow, and temperature at a certain place and time, and people measure these to see patterns.

subject: Science
description: Analyzing and Interpreting Data - Use observations (firsthand or from media) to describe patterns in the natural world in order to answer scientific questions.
description_plain: Your child can use what they see or watch to find patterns in nature and answer science questions.

subject: Science
description: Use and share observations of local weather conditions to describe patterns over time.
description_plain: Your child will watch and share what the weather is like in your area to notice patterns over time.

subject: Science
description: Patterns - Patterns in the natural and human-designed world can be observed and used as evidence.
description_plain: Your child can find and use patterns in nature and things people make as clues to understand the world.

subject: Science
description: Organization for Matter and Energy Flow in Organisms - All animals need food in order to live and grow. They obtain their food from plants or from other animals. Plants need water and light to live and grow.
description_plain: Your child knows that animals need food to live and grow, getting it from plants or other animals, and plants need water and light to grow.

subject: Science
description: Analyzing and Interpreting Data - Use observations (firsthand or from media) to describe patterns in the natural world in order to answer scientific questions.
description_plain: Your child can use their own observations or videos to find patterns in nature and answer questions.

subject: Science
description: Use observations to describe patterns of what plants and animals (including humans) need to survive.
description_plain: Your child will watch and describe what plants and animals (including people) need to live and grow.

subject: Science
description: Cause and Effect - Events have causes that generate observable patterns.
description_plain: Your child understands that events happen because of causes that create patterns we can see.

subject: Science
description: Constructing Explanations and Designing Solutions - Use tools and materials provided to design and build a device that solves a specific problem or a solution to a specific problem.
description_plain: Your child can use tools and materials to build something that solves a problem.

subject: Science
description: Use tools and materials to design and build a structure that will reduce the warming effect of sunlight on an area.
description_plain: Your child can use tools and materials to build something that helps keep an area cooler by blocking sunlight.

subject: Science
description: Cause and Effect - Events have causes that generate observable patterns.
