description_plain: Your child knows that things happen because of causes that make patterns we can notice.

subject: Science
description: Conservation of Energy and Energy Transfer - Sunlight warms Earth's surface.
description_plain: Your child understands that sunlight warms the Earth's surface.

subject: Science
description: Planning and Carrying Out Investigations - Make observations (firsthand or from media) to collect data that can be used to make comparisons.
description_plain: Your child can watch or look at information to collect data that helps compare things.

subject: Science
description: Make observations to determine the effect of sunlight on Earth's surface.
description_plain: Your child will observe and describe how sunlight affects the Earth's surface.

subject: Science
description: Cause and Effect - Simple tests can be designed to gather evidence to support or refute student ideas about causes.
description_plain: Your child can help plan simple tests to find out if their ideas about causes are right or wrong.

subject: Science
description: Defining Engineering Problems - A situation that people want to change or create can be approached as a problem to be solved through engineering. Such problems may have many acceptable solutions.
description_plain: Your child understands that problems people want to fix or things they want to make can be solved in different ways through engineering.

subject: Science
description: Forces and Motion - Pushes and pulls can have different strengths. Pushing or pulling on an object can change the speed or direction of its motion and can start or stop it.
description_plain: Your child knows that pushes and pulls can be strong or weak and can change how things move.

subject: Science
description: Analyzing and Interpreting Data - Analyze data from tests of an object or tool to determine if it works as intended.
description_plain: Your child can look at test results to decide if an object or tool works the way it should.

subject: Science
description: Analyze data to determine if a design solution works as intended to change the speed or direction of an object with a push or a pull.
description_plain: Your child can look at data to decide if a design works to change how fast or which way an object moves when pushed or pulled.

subject: Science
description: Cause and Effect - Simple tests can be designed to gather evidence to support or refute student ideas about causes.
description_plain: Your child can help design simple tests to check if their ideas about causes are correct.

subject: Science
description: Relationship Between Energy and Forces - A bigger push or pull makes things speed up or slow down more quickly.
description_plain: Your child understands that a bigger push or pull makes things speed up or slow down faster.

subject: Science
description: Types of Interactions - When objects touch or collide, they push on one another and can change motion.
description_plain: Your child knows that when objects touch or bump into each other, they push and can change how they move.

subject: Science
description: Forces and Motion - Pushes and pulls can have different strengths and directions. Pushing or pulling on an object can change the speed or direction of its motion and can start or stop it.
description_plain: Your child understands that pushes and pulls can be different in strength and direction and can start, stop, or change how things move.

subject: Science
description: Planning and Carrying Out Investigations - With guidance, plan and conduct an investigation in collaboration with peers.
description_plain: With help, your child can plan and do a simple investigation with others.

subject: Science
description: Plan and conduct an investigation to compare the effects of different strengths or different directions of pushes and pulls on the motion of an object.
description_plain: Your child will plan and do an experiment to see how different pushes and pulls affect how an object moves.

subject: Science
description: Optimizing the Design Solution - Because there is always more than one possible solution to a problem, it is useful to compare and test designs.
description_plain: Your child understands that there are many ways to solve a problem and can compare and test different designs.

subject: Science
description: Analyzing and Interpreting Data - Analyze data from tests of an object or tool to determine if it works as intended.
description_plain: Your child can look at test results to see if an object or tool works the way it should.

subject: Science
description: Analyze data from tests of two objects designed to solve the same problem to compare the strengths and weaknesses of how each performs.
description_plain: Your child can look at test results from two objects made to solve the same problem and talk about which one works better and why.

subject: Science
description: Structure and Function - The shape and stability of structures of natural and designed objects are related to their function(s).
description_plain: Your child understands that the shape and strength of things relate to what they do.

subject: Science
description: Developing Possible Solutions - Designs can be conveyed through sketches, drawings, or physical models. These representations are useful in communicating ideas for a problem's solutions to other people.
description_plain: Your child can draw or build simple models to show ideas for solving problems and share them with others.

subject: Science
description: Developing and Using Models - Develop a simple model based on evidence to represent a proposed object or tool.
description_plain: Your child can make a simple model based on evidence to show how an object or tool might work.

subject: Science
description: Develop a simple sketch, drawing, or physical model to illustrate how the shape of an object helps it function as needed to solve a given problem.
description_plain: Your child can draw or build a simple model to show how the shape of something helps it do its job.

subject: Science
description: Defining and Delimiting Engineering Problems - A situation that people want to change or create can be approached as a problem to be solved through engineering. Asking questions, making observations, and gathering information are helpful in thinking about problems. Before beginning to design a solution, it is important to clearly understand the problem.
description_plain: Your child understands how to ask questions and gather information to clearly understand a problem before trying to solve it.

subject: Science
description: Asking Questions and Defining Problems - Ask questions based on observations to find more information about the natural and/or designed world(s). Define a simple problem that can be solved through the development of a new or improved object or tool.
description_plain: Your child can ask questions about the world and define simple problems that can be solved by making new or better tools.

subject: Science
description: Ask questions, make observations, and gather information about a situation that people want to change to define a simple problem that can be solved through the development of a new or improved object or tool.
description_plain: Your child can ask questions, observe, and gather information to understand a problem that needs a new or improved tool.

subject: Science
description: Patterns - Patterns in the natural world can be observed, used to describe phenomena, and used as evidence.
description_plain: Your child can find and use patterns in nature to describe and explain what happens.

subject: Science
description: Earth and the Solar System - Seasonal patterns of sunrise and sunset can be observed, described, and predicted.
description_plain: Your child can watch and describe how sunrise and sunset change with the seasons and predict what will happen.

subject: Science
description: Planning and Carrying Out Investigations - Make observations (firsthand or from media) to collect data that can be used to make comparisons.
description_plain: Your child can make observations to collect information that helps compare things.

subject: Science
description: Make observations at different times of year to relate the amount of daylight to the time of year.
description_plain: Your child can watch how the amount of daylight changes during different times of the year.

subject: Science
description: Patterns - Patterns in the natural world can be observed, used to describe phenomena, and used as evidence.
description_plain: Your child can notice patterns in nature and use them to explain what they see.

subject: Science
description: The Universe and Its Stars - Patterns of the motion of the sun, moon, and stars in the sky can be observed, described, and predicted.
description_plain: Your child can watch and describe how the sun, moon, and stars move in the sky and predict their patterns.

subject: Science
description: Analyzing and Interpreting Data - Use observations (firsthand or from media) to describe patterns in the natural world in order to answer scientific questions.
description_plain: Your child can use what they see or watch to find patterns in nature and answer science questions.

subject: Science
description: Use observations of the sun, moon, and stars to describe patterns that can be predicted.
description_plain: Your child can observe the sun, moon, and stars to find patterns that happen again and again.

subject: Science
description: Patterns - Patterns in the natural world can be observed, used to describe phenomena, and used as evidence.
description_plain: Your child can find and use patterns in nature to explain what happens.

subject: Science
description: Variation of Traits - Individuals of the same kind of plant or animal are recognizable as similar but can also vary in many ways.
description_plain: Your child understands that plants or animals of the same kind look alike but can also be different in many ways.

subject: Science
description: Inheritance of Traits - Young animals are very much but not exactly like their parents. Plants also are very much but not exactly like their parents.
description_plain: Your child knows that baby animals and plants look a lot like their parents but are not exactly the same.

subject: Science
description: Constructing Explanations and Designing Solutions - Make observations (firsthand or from media) to construct an evidence-based account for natural phenomena.
description_plain: Your child can use observations to explain natural events with evidence.

subject: Science
description: Make observations to construct an evidence-based account that young plants and animals are like, but not exactly like, their parents.
description_plain: Your child can observe young plants and animals and explain how they are similar but not exactly like their parents.

subject: Science
description: Patterns - Patterns in the natural world can be observed, used to describe phenomena, and used as evidence.
description_plain: Your child can find and use patterns in nature to explain what happens.

subject: Science
description: Growth and Development of Organisms - Adult plants and animals can have young. In many kinds of animals, parents and the offspring themselves engage in behaviors that help the offspring to survive.
description_plain: Your child understands that adult plants and animals have babies, and many animals and parents do things to help the babies survive.

subject: Science
description: Obtaining, Evaluating, and Communicating Information - Read grade-appropriate texts and use media to obtain scientific information to determine patterns in the natural world .
description_plain: Your child can read books and watch videos to find information about patterns in nature.

subject: Science
description: Read texts and use media to determine patterns in the behavior of parents and offspring that help offspring survive.
description_plain: Your child can read and watch to learn how parents and their babies behave in ways that help the babies survive.

subject: Science
description: Structure and Function - The shape and stability of structures of natural and designed objects are related to their function(s).
description_plain: Your child understands that the shape and strength of natural and made objects relate to what they do.

subject: Science
description: Information Processing - Animals have body parts that capture and convey different kinds of information needed for growth and survival. Animals respond to these inputs with behaviors that help them survive. Plants also respond to some external inputs.
description_plain: Your child understands that animals have body parts that help them sense things they need to grow and survive, and they act in ways that help them stay alive; plants also react to some things around them.

subject: Science
description: Structure and Function - All organisms have external parts. Different animals use their body parts in different ways to see, hear, grasp objects, protect themselves, move from place to place, and seek, find, and take in food, water, and air. Plants also have different parts (roots, stems, leaves, flowers, fruits) that help them survive and grow.
description_plain: Your child can name different body parts of animals and explain how they help animals see, hear, grab things, protect themselves, move, and find food, water, and air; your child also knows that plants have parts like roots, stems, leaves, flowers, and fruits that help them grow.

subject: Science
description: Constructing Explanations and Designing Solutions - Use materials to design a device that solves a specific problem or a solution to a specific problem.
description_plain: Your child can use materials to build something that solves a specific problem.

subject: Science
description: Use materials to design a solution to a human problem by mimicking how plants and/or animals use their external parts to help them survive, grow, and meet their needs.
description_plain: Your child can use materials to create a solution to a problem by copying how plants or animals use their body parts to survive and grow.

subject: Science
description: Cause and Effect - Simple tests can be designed to gather evidence to support or refute student ideas about causes.
description_plain: Your child can plan simple tests to find out if their ideas about what causes something are right or wrong.

subject: Science
description: Information Technologies and Instrumentation - People also use a variety of devices to communicate (send and receive information) over long distances.
description_plain: Your child understands that people use different devices to send and receive messages over long distances.

subject: Science
description: Constructing Explanations and Designing Solutions - Use tools and materials provided to design a device that solves a specific problem.
description_plain: Your child can use tools and materials to create a device that solves a specific problem.

subject: Science
description: Use tools and materials to design and build a device that uses light or sound to solve the problem of communicating over a distance.
description_plain: Your child can design and build a tool that uses light or sound to help people communicate from far away.

subject: Science
description: Cause and Effect - Simple tests can be designed to gather evidence to support or refute student ideas about causes.
description_plain: Your child can design simple tests to check if their ideas about causes are correct.

subject: Science
description: Electromagnetic Radiation - Some materials allow light to pass through them; others allow only some light through; and others block all the light and create a dark shadow on any surface beyond them, where the light cannot reach. Mirrors can be used to redirect a light beam. (Boundary: The idea that light travels from place to place is developed through experiences with light sources, mirrors, and shadows, but no attempt is made to discuss the speed of light.)
description_plain: Your child understands that some materials let light pass through, some let only some light through, and some block all light and make shadows; mirrors can change the direction of light.

subject: Science
description: Planning and Carrying Out Investigations - Plan and conduct investigations collaboratively in order to produce data to serve as the basis for evidence to answer a question.
description_plain: Your child can work with others to plan and do investigations that collect data to answer a question.

subject: Science
description: Plan and conduct an investigation to determine the effect of placing objects made with different materials in the path of a beam of light.
description_plain: Your child can plan and do an experiment to see what happens when light shines on objects made of different materials.

subject: Science
description: Cause and Effect - Simple tests can be designed to gather evidence to support or refute student ideas about causes.
description_plain: Your child can create simple tests to gather evidence to support or disprove their ideas about causes.

subject: Science
description: Electromagnetic Radiation - Objects can be seen if light is available to illuminate them or if they give off their own light.
description_plain: Your child understands that we can see objects when there is light shining on them or when they make their own light.

subject: Science
description: Constructing Explanations and Designing Solutions - Make observations (firsthand or from media) to construct an evidence-based account for natural phenomena.
description_plain: Your child can make observations to explain natural events using evidence.

subject: Science
description: Make observations to construct an evidence-based account that objects can be seen only when illuminated.
description_plain: Your child can observe and explain that we can only see objects when light shines on them.

subject: Science
description: Cause and Effect - Simple tests can be designed to gather evidence to support or refute student ideas about causes.
description_plain: Your child can design simple tests to find out if their ideas about what causes something are true.

subject: Science
description: Information Technologies and Instrumentation - People also use a variety of devices to communicate (send and receive information) over long distances.
description_plain: Your child knows that people use many devices to send and receive information over long distances.

subject: Science
description: Wave Properties - Sound can make matter vibrate, and vibrating matter can make sound.
description_plain: Your child understands that sound makes things vibrate, and vibrating things can make sound.

subject: Science
description: Planning and Carrying Out Investigations - Plan and conduct investigations collaboratively in order to produce data to serve as the basis for evidence to answer a question.
description_plain: Your child can work with others to plan and carry out investigations that collect data to answer questions.

subject: Science
description: Plan and conduct investigations to provide evidence that vibrating materials can make sound and that sound can make materials vibrate.
description_plain: Your child can plan and do experiments to show that vibrating things make sound and sound can make things vibrate.

subject: Science
description: Optimizing the Design Solution - Because there is always more than one possible solution to a problem, it is useful to compare and test designs.
description_plain: Your child understands that there are many ways to solve a problem and can compare and test different designs.

subject: Science
description: Analyzing and Interpreting Data - Analyze data from tests of an object or tool to determine if it works as intended.
description_plain: Your child can look at test results to see if an object or tool works the way it should.

subject: Science
description: Analyze data from tests of two objects designed to solve the same problem to compare the strengths and weaknesses of how each performs.
description_plain: Your child can look at test results from two objects made to solve the same problem and talk about which one works better and why.

subject: Science
description: Structure and Function - The shape and stability of structures of natural and designed objects are related to their function(s).
description_plain: Your child understands that the shape and strength of things relate to what they do.

subject: Science
description: Developing Possible Solutions - Designs can be conveyed through sketches, drawings, or physical models. These representations are useful in communicating ideas for a problem's solutions to other people.
description_plain: Your child can draw or build simple models to show ideas for solving problems and share them with others.

subject: Science
description: Developing and Using Models - Develop a simple model based on evidence to represent a proposed object or tool.
description_plain: Your child can make a simple model based on evidence to show how an object or tool might work.

subject: Science
description: Develop a simple sketch, drawing, or physical model to illustrate how the shape of an object helps it function as needed to solve a given problem.
description_plain: Your child can draw or build a simple model to show how the shape of something helps it do its job.

subject: Science
description: Defining and Delimiting Engineering Problems - A situation that people want to change or create can be approached as a problem to be solved through engineering. Asking questions, making observations, and gathering information are helpful in thinking about problems. Before beginning to design a solution, it is important to clearly understand the problem.
description_plain: Your child understands how to ask questions and gather information to clearly understand a problem before trying to solve it.

subject: Science
description: Asking Questions and Defining Problems - Ask questions based on observations to find more information about the natural and/or designed world(s). Define a simple problem that can be solved through the development of a new or improved object or tool.
description_plain: Your child can ask questions about the world and define simple problems that can be solved by making new or better tools.

subject: Science
description: Ask questions, make observations, and gather information about a situation that people want to change to define a simple problem that can be solved through the development of a new or improved object or tool.
description_plain: Your child can ask questions, observe, and gather information to understand a problem that needs a new or improved tool.

subject: Science
description: Patterns - Patterns in the natural world can be observed.
description_plain: Your child can notice and describe patterns found in nature.

subject: Science
description: The Roles of Water in Earth's Surface Processes - Water is found in oceans, rivers, lakes, and ponds. Water exists as solid ice and in liquid form.
description_plain: Your child knows that water is found in oceans, rivers, lakes, and ponds, and that it can be ice or liquid.

subject: Science
description: Obtaining, Evaluating, and Communicating Information - Obtain information using various texts, text features (e.g., headings, tables of contents, glossaries, electronic menus, icons), and other media that will be useful in answering a scientific question.
description_plain: Your child can find information from books, pictures, and other sources to help answer science questions.

subject: Science
description: Obtain information to identify where water is found on Earth and that it can be solid or liquid.
description_plain: Your child can find out where water is on Earth and understand that water can be ice or liquid.

subject: Science
description: Patterns - Patterns in the natural world can be observed.
description_plain: Your child can observe and describe patterns in the natural world.

subject: Science
description: Plate Tectonics and Large-Scale System Interactions - Maps show where things are located. One can map the shapes and kinds of land and water in any area.
description_plain: Your child can use maps to show where land and water are located and what shapes they have.

subject: Science
description: Developing and Using Models - Develop a model to represent patterns in the natural world.
description_plain: Your child can create a model to show patterns found in nature.

subject: Science
description: Develop a model to represent the shapes and kinds of land and bodies of water in an area.
description_plain: Your child can create a model that shows the shapes and types of land and water in a certain place.

subject: Science
description: Stability and Change - Things may change slowly or rapidly.
description_plain: Your child understands that things can change slowly or quickly.

subject: Science
description: Optimizing the Design Solution - Because there is always more than one possible solution to a problem, it is useful to compare and test designs.
description_plain: Your child knows that there is more than one way to solve a problem and can compare and test different ideas.

subject: Science
description: Earth Materials and Systems - Wind and water can change the shape of the land.
description_plain: Your child understands that wind and water can change the shape of the land.

subject: Science
description: Constructing Explanations and Designing Solutions - Compare multiple solutions to a problem.
description_plain: Your child can compare different solutions to a problem.

subject: Science
description: Compare multiple solutions designed to slow or prevent wind or water from changing the shape of the land.
description_plain: Your child can compare different ways people try to stop wind or water from changing the land’s shape.

subject: Science
description: Stability and Change - Things may change slowly or rapidly.
description_plain: Your child knows that some things change slowly and others change quickly.

subject: Science
description: The History of Planet Earth - Some events happen very quickly; others occur very slowly, over a time period much longer than one can observe.
description_plain: Your child understands that some events happen very fast and others happen very slowly over a long time.

subject: Science
description: Constructing Explanations and Designing Solutions - Make observations from several sources to construct an evidence-based account for natural phenomena.
description_plain: Your child can look at information from different places to explain natural events using evidence.

subject: Science
description: Use information from several sources to provide evidence that Earth events can occur quickly or slowly.
description_plain: Your child can use information from different places to show that Earth changes can happen fast or slowly.

subject: Science
description: Patterns - Patterns in the natural world can be observed.
description_plain: Your child can observe and describe patterns in living things.

subject: Science
description: Biodiversity and Humans - There are many different kinds of living things in any area, and they exist in different places on land and in water.
description_plain: Your child knows that many different kinds of living things live in different places on land and in water.

subject: Science
description: Planning and Carrying Out Investigations - Make observations (firsthand or from media) to collect data that can be used to make comparisons.
description_plain: Your child can make observations to collect data that helps compare things.

subject: Science
description: Make observations of plants and animals to compare the diversity of life in different habitats.
description_plain: Your child can watch plants and animals and compare how many different kinds live in different places.

subject: Science
description: Structure and Function - The shape and stability of structures of natural and designed objects are related to their function(s).
description_plain: Your child understands that the shape and strength of natural and man-made things relate to what they do.

subject: Science
description: Developing Possible Solutions - Designs can be conveyed through sketches, drawings, or physical models. These representations are useful in communicating ideas for a problem's solutions to other people.
description_plain: Your child can draw or build models to show ideas for solving problems and share these ideas with others.

subject: Science
description: Interdependent Relationships in Ecosystems - Plants depend on animals for pollination or to move their seeds around.
description_plain: Your child knows that plants need animals to help spread their pollen or seeds.

subject: Science
description: Developing and Using Models - Develop a simple model based on evidence to represent a proposed object or tool.
description_plain: Your child can make a simple model based on evidence to show how an object or tool works.

subject: Science
description: Develop a simple model that mimics the function of an animal in dispersing seeds or pollinating plants.
description_plain: Your child can make a simple model that shows how an animal helps spread seeds or pollinate plants.

subject: Science
description: Cause and Effect - Events have causes that generate observable patterns.
description_plain: Your child understands that events have causes that create patterns we can see.

subject: Science
description: Interdependent Relationships in Ecosystems - Plants depend on water and light to grow.
description_plain: Your child knows that plants need water and light to grow.

subject: Science
description: Planning and Carrying Out Investigations - Plan and conduct an investigation collaboratively in order to produce data to serve as the basis for evidence to answer a question.
description_plain: Your child can plan and do an investigation with others to collect data that answers a question.

subject: Science
description: Plan and conduct an investigation to determine if plants need sunlight and water to grow.
description_plain: Your child can plan and do an experiment to see if plants need sunlight and water to grow.

subject: Science
description: Cause and Effect - Events have causes that generate observable patterns.
description_plain: Your child understands that events have causes that create patterns we can observe.

subject: Science
description: Chemical Reactions - Heating or cooling a substance may cause changes that can be observed. Sometimes these changes are reversible, and sometimes they are not.
description_plain: Your child can observe how heating or cooling a substance causes changes, and understand that some changes can be undone while others cannot.

subject: Science
description: Engaging in Argument from Evidence - Construct an argument with evidence to support a claim.
description_plain: Your child can explain their ideas by using evidence to support their opinion.

subject: Science
description: Construct an argument with evidence that some changes caused by heating or cooling can be reversed and some cannot.
description_plain: Your child can explain with evidence which changes caused by heating or cooling can be undone and which cannot.

subject: Science
description: Energy and Matter - Objects may break into smaller pieces and be put together into larger pieces, or they may change shape.
description_plain: Your child can see how objects can break into smaller parts, be put together into bigger parts, or change shape.

subject: Science
description: Structure and Properties of Matter - Different properties are suited to different purposes. A great variety of objects can be built up from a small set of pieces.
description_plain: Your child understands that different things have different qualities that make them useful for certain jobs, and many objects can be made from a few basic pieces.

subject: Science
description: Constructing Explanations and Designing Solutions - Make observations (firsthand or from media) to construct an evidence-based account for natural phenomena.
description_plain: Your child can watch or read about natural events and explain what they see using evidence.

subject: Science
description: Make observations to construct an evidence-based account of how an object made of a small set of pieces can be disassembled and made into a new object.
description_plain: Your child can watch and describe how an object made of a few parts can be taken apart and made into something new.

subject: Science
description: Cause and Effect - Simple tests can be designed to gather evidence to support or refute student ideas about causes.
description_plain: Your child can plan simple tests to find out if their ideas about causes are right or wrong.

subject: Science
description: Structure and Properties of Matter - Different properties are suited to different purposes.
description_plain: Your child understands that things have different qualities that make them good for different uses.

subject: Science
description: Analyzing and Interpreting Data - Analyze data from tests of an object or tool to determine if it works as intended.
description_plain: Your child can look at test results to decide if an object or tool works the way it should.

subject: Science
description: Analyze data obtained from testing different materials to determine which materials have the properties that are best suited for an intended purpose.
description_plain: Your child can test different materials and decide which ones work best for a certain use.

subject: Science
description: Patterns - Patterns in the natural and human-designed world can be observed.
description_plain: Your child can notice and describe patterns in nature and things made by people.

subject: Science
description: Structure and Properties of Matter - Different kinds of matter exist and many of them can be either solid or liquid, depending on temperature. Matter can be described and classified by its observable properties.
description_plain: Your child understands that matter can be different kinds and can change between solid and liquid depending on temperature, and can describe things by what they look like or feel like.

subject: Science
description: Planning and Carrying Out Investigations - Plan and conduct an investigation collaboratively in order to produce data to serve as the basis for evidence to answer a question.
description_plain: Your child can work with others to plan and do an investigation that collects information to answer a question.

subject: Science
description: Plan and conduct an investigation to describe and classify different kinds of materials by their observable properties.
description_plain: Your child can plan and do an experiment to describe and sort materials by what they look and feel like.

subject: Science
description: Planning and Carrying Out Investigations - Plan and conduct an investigation collaboratively in order to produce data to serve as the basis for evidence, using fair tests in which variables are controlled and the number of trials is considered.
description_plain: Your child can work with others to plan and do fair tests that control variables and repeat trials to collect good information.

subject: Science
description: Plan and carry out fair tests in which variables are controlled and failure points are considered to identify aspects of a model or prototype that can be improved.
description_plain: Your child can plan and do fair tests by controlling variables and thinking about what might go wrong to find ways to improve a model or prototype.

subject: Science
description: Developing Possible Solutions - Research on a problem should be carried out before beginning to design a solution. Testing a solution involves investigating how well it performs under a range of likely conditions. At whatever stage, communicating with peers about proposed solutions is an important part of the design process, and shared ideas can lead to improved designs.
description_plain: Your child can research a problem before designing a solution, test how well the solution works in different situations, and share ideas with others to improve the design.

subject: Science
description: Constructing Explanations and Designing Solutions - Generate and compare multiple solutions to a problem based on how well they meet the criteria and constraints of the design problem.
description_plain: Your child can come up with several solutions to a problem and compare how well each one meets the goals and limits of the problem.

subject: Science
description: Generate and compare multiple possible solutions to a problem based on how well each is likely to meet the criteria and constraints of the problem.
description_plain: Your child can come up with and compare several possible solutions to a problem to see which ones best meet the goals and limits.

subject: Science
description: Defining and Delimiting Engineering Problems - Possible solutions to a problem are limited by available materials and resources (constraints). The success of a designed solution is determined by considering the desired features of a solution (criteria). Different proposals for solutions can be compared on the basis of how well each one meets the specified criteria for success or how well each takes the constraints into account.
description_plain: Your child understands that solutions to problems depend on what materials and resources are available, and can compare different ideas based on how well they meet the goals and limits.

subject: Science
description: Asking Questions and Defining Problems - Define a simple design problem that can be solved through the development of an object, tool, process, or system and includes several criteria for success and constraints on materials, time, or cost.
description_plain: Your child can define a simple design problem that includes goals to meet and limits like materials, time, or cost.

subject: Science
description: Define a simple design problem reflecting a need or a want that includes specified criteria for success and constraints on materials, time, or cost.
description_plain: Your child can describe a simple design problem based on a need or want, including what success looks like and limits like materials, time, or cost.

subject: Science
description: Cause and Effect - Cause-and-effect relationships are routinely identified, tested, and used to explain change.
description_plain: Your child can find and test cause-and-effect relationships to explain changes.

subject: Science
description: Natural Hazards - A variety of natural hazards result from natural processes. Humans cannot eliminate natural hazards but can take steps to reduce their impacts.
description_plain: Your child understands that natural hazards happen because of natural processes, and people can take steps to lessen their effects but cannot stop them completely.

subject: Science
description: Engaging in Argument from Evidence - Make a claim about the merit of a solution to a problem by citing relevant evidence about how it meets the criteria and constraints of the problem.
description_plain: Your child can make a claim about how good a solution is by using evidence about how it meets the problem’s goals and limits.

subject: Science
description: Make a claim about the merit of a design solution that reduces the impacts of a weather-related hazard.
description_plain: Your child can explain why a design helps reduce problems caused by bad weather.

subject: Science
description: Patterns - Patterns of change can be used to make predictions.
description_plain: Your child can use patterns of change to make predictions.

subject: Science
description: Weather and Climate - Climate describes a range of an area's typical weather conditions and the extent to which those conditions vary over years.
description_plain: Your child understands that climate means the usual weather in an area over many years and how much it changes.

subject: Science
description: Obtaining, Evaluating, and Communicating Information - Obtain and combine information from books and other reliable media to explain phenomena.
description_plain: Your child can find and combine information from books and other trusted sources to explain natural events.

subject: Science
description: Obtain and combine information to describe climates in different regions of the world.
description_plain: Your child will gather and combine information to describe different climates around the world.

subject: Science
description: Patterns - Patterns of change can be used to make predictions.
description_plain: Your child can use patterns of change to make predictions.

subject: Science
description: Weather and Climate - Scientists record patterns of weather across different times and areas so that they can make predictions about what kind of weather might happen next.
description_plain: Your child understands that scientists watch weather patterns over time and places to predict what weather might happen next.

subject: Science
description: Analyzing and Interpreting Data - Represent data in tables and various graphical displays (bar graphs and pictographs) to reveal patterns that indicate relationships.
description_plain: Your child can show data in tables and graphs like bar graphs and pictographs to find patterns and relationships.

subject: Science
description: Represent data in tables and graphical displays to describe typical weather conditions expected during a particular season.
description_plain: Your child can show weather data in tables and graphs to explain what weather is usually like in a season.

subject: Science
description: Models - A system can be described in terms of its components and their interactions.
description_plain: Your child understands that a system can be described by its parts and how they work together.

subject: Science
description: Biodiversity and Humans - Populations live in a variety of habitats, and changes in those habitats affect the organisms living there.
description_plain: Your child understands that animals and plants live in different habitats, and changes to those habitats affect the living things there.

subject: Science
description: Ecosystem Dynamics, Functioning, and Resilience - When the environment changes in ways that affect a place's physical characteristics, temperature, or the availability of resources, some organisms survive and reproduce, others move to new locations, yet others move into the transformed environment, and some die.
description_plain: Your child understands that when the environment changes, some organisms survive and reproduce, some move away, some new ones move in, and some die.

subject: Science
description: Engaging in Argument from Evidence - Make a claim about the merit of a solution to a problem by citing relevant evidence about how it meets the criteria and constraints of the problem.
description_plain: Your child can explain how well a solution works by using evidence about how it meets the problem’s goals and limits.

subject: Science
description: Make a claim about the merit of a solution to a problem caused when the environment changes and the types of plants and animals that live there may change.
description_plain: Your child can explain if a solution is good for helping plants and animals when their environment changes.

subject: Science
description: Cause and Effect - Cause-and-effect relationships are routinely identified and used to explain change.
description_plain: Your child can find cause-and-effect relationships and use them to explain changes.

subject: Science
description: Adaptation - For any particular environment, some kinds of organisms survive well, some survive less well, and some cannot survive at all.
description_plain: Your child understands that some kinds of organisms survive better than others in a certain environment.

subject: Science
description: Engaging in Argument from Evidence - Construct an argument with evidence.
description_plain: Your child can explain their ideas by using evidence.

subject: Science
description: Construct an argument with evidence that in a particular habitat some organisms can survive well, some survive less well, and some cannot survive at all.
description_plain: Your child can explain with evidence why some animals live well in a habitat, some live less well, and some cannot live there at all.

subject: Science
description: Cause and Effect - Cause-and-effect relationships are routinely identified and used to explain change.
description_plain: Your child can find cause-and-effect relationships and use them to explain changes.

subject: Science
description: Natural Selection - Sometimes the differences in characteristics between individuals of the same species provide advantages in surviving, finding mates, and reproducing.
description_plain: Your child understands that differences between individuals of the same species can help them survive, find mates, and have babies.

subject: Science
description: Constructing Explanations and Designing Solutions - Use evidence (e.g., observations, patterns) to construct an explanation.
description_plain: Your child can use evidence like observations and patterns to explain something.

subject: Science
description: Use evidence to construct an explanation for how the variations in characteristics among individuals of the same species may provide advantages in surviving, finding mates, and reproducing.
description_plain: Your child understands how differences in animals or plants help them survive, find mates, and have babies.

subject: Science
description: Cause and Effect - Cause-and-effect relationships are routinely identified and used to explain change.
description_plain: Your child can find cause-and-effect relationships and use them to explain changes.

subject: Science
description: Variation of Traits - The environment also affects the traits that an organism develops.
description_plain: Your child understands that the environment can affect the traits an organism develops.

subject: Science
description: Inheritance of Traits - Other characteristics result from individuals' interactions with the environment, which can range from diet to learning. Many characteristics involve both inheritance and environment.
description_plain: Your child understands that some traits come from parents and some come from experiences like diet or learning, and many traits come from both.

subject: Science
description: Constructing Explanations and Designing Solutions - Use evidence (e.g., observations, patterns) to support an explanation.
description_plain: Your child can use evidence like observations and patterns to support an explanation.

subject: Science
description: Use evidence to support the explanation that traits can be influenced by the environment.
description_plain: Your child can use evidence to show how the environment can change traits in living things.

subject: Science
description: Patterns - Similarities and differences in patterns can be used to sort and classify natural phenomena.
description_plain: Your child can use similarities and differences in patterns to sort and group natural things.

subject: Science
description: Variation of Traits - Different organisms vary in how they look and function because they have different inherited information.
description_plain: Your child understands that living things look and work differently because they inherit different traits from their parents.

subject: Science
description: Inheritance of Traits - Many characteristics of organisms are inherited from their parents.
description_plain: Your child knows that many traits of plants and animals come from their parents.

subject: Science
description: Analyzing and Interpreting Data - Analyze and interpret data to make sense of phenomena using logical reasoning.
description_plain: Your child can look at data and use thinking skills to understand what it shows about how things happen.

subject: Science
description: Analyze and interpret data to provide evidence that plants and animals have traits inherited from parents and that variation of these traits exists in a group of similar organisms.
description_plain: Your child can look at data to show that plants and animals get traits from their parents and that these traits can vary.

subject: Science
description: Cause and Effect - Cause-and-effect relationships are routinely identified and used to explain change.
description_plain: Your child can find and explain how one thing causes another to change.

subject: Science
description: Social Interactions and Group Behavior - Being part of a group helps animals obtain food, defend themselves, and cope with changes. Groups may serve different functions and vary dramatically in size.
description_plain: Your child understands that animals living in groups can find food, protect themselves, and handle changes better.

subject: Science
description: Engaging in Argument from Evidence - Construct an argument with evidence, data, and/or a model.
description_plain: Your child can make a clear argument using facts, data, or models to support their ideas.

subject: Science
description: Construct an argument that some animals form groups that help members survive.
description_plain: Your child can explain why some animals live in groups to help each other survive.

subject: Science
description: Patterns - Patterns of change can be used to make predictions.
description_plain: Your child can use patterns they see to guess what might happen next.

subject: Science
description: Growth and Development of Organisms - Reproduction is essential to the continued existence of every kind of organism. Plants and animals have unique and diverse life cycles.
description_plain: Your child knows that plants and animals have babies to keep their species alive and that their life cycles are different.

subject: Science
description: Developing and Using Models - Develop models to describe phenomena.
description_plain: Your child can create simple models to explain how things work or happen.

subject: Science
description: Develop models to describe that organisms have unique and diverse life cycles but all have in common birth, growth, reproduction, and death.
description_plain: Your child can create models to show that all living things have life stages like birth, growth, reproduction, and death.

subject: Science
description: Cause and Effect - Identify and test causal relationships and use these relationships to explain change.
description_plain: Your child can find and test causes and effects to explain why things change.

subject: Science
description: Types of Interactions - Electric and magnetic forces between a pair of objects do not require that the objects be in contact. The sizes of the forces in each situation depend on the properties of the objects and their distances apart, and for forces between two magnets, on their orientation relative to each other.
description_plain: Your child understands that electric and magnetic forces can act between objects even if they are not touching, and these forces depend on the objects’ properties and distance.

subject: Science
description: Asking Questions and Defining Problems - Define a simple problem that can be solved through the development of a new or improved object or tool.
description_plain: Your child can describe a simple problem that can be solved by making a new or better tool or object.

subject: Science
description: Define a simple design problem that can be solved by applying scientific ideas about magnets.
description_plain: Your child can describe a simple problem that can be solved using ideas about magnets.

subject: Science
description: Cause and Effect - Cause-and-effect relationships are routinely identified, tested, and used to explain change.
description_plain: Your child can find, test, and explain cause-and-effect relationships to understand changes.

subject: Science
description: Types of Interactions - Electric and magnetic forces between a pair of objects do not require that the objects be in contact. The sizes of the forces in each situation depend on the properties of the objects and their distances apart and, for forces between two magnets, on their orientation relative to each other.
description_plain: Your child knows that electric and magnetic forces can work between objects without touching, and the strength depends on their properties, distance, and how magnets are positioned.

subject: Science
description: Asking Questions and Defining Problems - Ask questions that can be investigated based on patterns, such as cause-and-effect relationships.
description_plain: Your child can ask questions that can be answered by looking at patterns and cause-and-effect.

subject: Science
description: Ask questions to determine cause-and-effect relationships of electric or magnetic interactions between two objects not in contact with each other.
description_plain: Your child can ask questions to find out how electric or magnetic forces cause changes between objects that are not touching.

subject: Science
description: Patterns - Patterns of change can be used to make predictions.
description_plain: Your child can use patterns of change to help predict what will happen next.

subject: Science
description: Forces and Motion - The patterns of an object's motion in various situations can be observed and measured; when that past motion exhibits a regular pattern, future motion can be predicted from it. (Boundary: Technical terms, such as "magnitude," "velocity," "momentum," and "vector quantity," are not introduced at this level, but the concept that some quantities need both size and direction to be described is developed.)
description_plain: Your child can watch and measure how things move and use patterns from past motion to guess what will happen next.

subject: Science
description: Planning and Carrying Out Investigations - Make observations and/or measurements to produce data to serve as the basis for evidence for an explanation of a phenomenon or to test a design solution.
description_plain: Your child can make observations or measurements to collect data that helps explain something or test a solution.

subject: Science
description: Make observations and/or measurements of an object's motion to provide evidence that a pattern can be used to predict future motion.
description_plain: Your child can watch and measure how things move to find patterns that help predict future motion.

subject: Science
description: Cause and Effect - Cause-and-effect relationships are routinely identified.
description_plain: Your child can find cause-and-effect relationships regularly.

subject: Science
description: Types of Interactions - Objects in contact exert forces on each other.
description_plain: Your child understands that objects touching each other push or pull on one another.

subject: Science
description: Forces and Motion - Each force acts on one particular object and has both strength and a direction. An object at rest typically has multiple forces acting on it, but they add up to zero net force on the object. Forces that do not sum to zero can cause changes in the object's speed or direction of motion. (Boundary: Qualitative and conceptual but not quantitative addition of forces are used at this level.)
description_plain: Your child knows that forces have strength and direction, and when forces don’t balance, they can change how an object moves.

subject: Science
description: Planning and Carrying Out Investigations - Plan and conduct an investigation collaboratively in order to produce data to serve as the basis for evidence, using fair tests in which variables are controlled and the number of trials is considered.
description_plain: Your child can plan and do an investigation with others, making sure to test fairly and repeat trials to gather good data.

subject: Science
description: Plan and conduct an investigation to provide evidence of the effects of balanced and unbalanced forces on the motion of an object.
description_plain: Your child can plan and do an experiment to show how balanced and unbalanced forces change how things move.

subject: Science
description: Planning and Carrying Out Investigations - Plan and conduct an investigation collaboratively in order to produce data to serve as the basis for evidence, using fair tests in which variables are controlled and the number of trials is considered.
description_plain: Your child can work with others to plan and do fair tests that control variables and repeat trials to collect good information.

subject: Science
description: Plan and carry out fair tests in which variables are controlled and failure points are considered to identify aspects of a model or prototype that can be improved.
description_plain: Your child can plan and do fair tests by controlling variables and thinking about what might go wrong to find ways to improve a model or prototype.

subject: Science
description: Developing Possible Solutions - Research on a problem should be carried out before beginning to design a solution. Testing a solution involves investigating how well it performs under a range of likely conditions. At whatever stage, communicating with peers about proposed solutions is an important part of the design process, and shared ideas can lead to improved designs.
description_plain: Your child can research a problem before designing a solution, test how well the solution works in different situations, and share ideas with others to improve the design.

subject: Science
description: Constructing Explanations and Designing Solutions - Generate and compare multiple solutions to a problem based on how well they meet the criteria and constraints of the design problem.
description_plain: Your child can come up with several solutions to a problem and compare how well each one meets the goals and limits of the problem.

subject: Science
description: Generate and compare multiple possible solutions to a problem based on how well each is likely to meet the criteria and constraints of the problem.
description_plain: Your child can come up with and compare several possible solutions to a problem to see which ones best meet the goals and limits.

subject: Science
description: Defining and Delimiting Engineering Problems - Possible solutions to a problem are limited by available materials and resources (constraints). The success of a designed solution is determined by considering the desired features of a solution (criteria). Different proposals for solutions can be compared on the basis of how well each one meets the specified criteria for success or how well each takes the constraints into account.
description_plain: Your child understands that solutions to problems depend on what materials and resources are available, and can compare different ideas based on how well they meet the goals and limits.

subject: Science
description: Asking Questions and Defining Problems - Define a simple design problem that can be solved through the development of an object, tool, process, or system and includes several criteria for success and constraints on materials, time, or cost.
description_plain: Your child can define a simple design problem that includes goals to meet and limits like materials, time, or cost.

subject: Science
description: Define a simple design problem reflecting a need or a want that includes specified criteria for success and constraints on materials, time, or cost.
description_plain: Your child can describe a simple design problem based on a need or want, including what success looks like and limits like materials, time, or cost.

subject: Science
description: Cause and Effect - Cause-and-effect relationships are routinely identified, tested, and used to explain change.
description_plain: Your child can find, test, and explain cause-and-effect relationships to understand changes.

subject: Science
description: Designing Solutions to Engineering Problems - Testing a solution involves investigating how well it performs under a range of likely conditions.
description_plain: Your child knows that testing a solution means checking how well it works in different situations.

subject: Science
description: Natural Hazards - A variety of hazards result from natural processes (e.g., earthquakes, tsunamis, volcanic eruptions). Humans cannot eliminate the hazards but can take steps to reduce their impacts.
description_plain: Your child understands that natural events like earthquakes and volcanoes can be dangerous, but people can take steps to lessen their effects.

subject: Science
description: Constructing Explanations and Designing Solutions - Generate and compare multiple solutions to a problem based on how well they meet the criteria and constraints of the design solution.
description_plain: Your child can come up with and compare different solutions to a problem to see which one works best.

subject: Science
description: Generate and compare multiple solutions to reduce the impacts of natural Earth processes on humans.
description_plain: Your child comes up with and compares different ideas to lessen how natural Earth events impact people.

subject: Science
description: Cause and Effect - Cause-and-effect relationships are routinely identified and used to explain change.
description_plain: Your child can find and use cause-and-effect relationships to explain changes.

subject: Science
description: Natural Resources - Energy and fuels that humans use are derived from natural sources, and their use affects the environment in multiple ways. Some resources are renewable over time, and others are not.
description_plain: Your child knows that energy and fuels come from natural sources, and using them can affect the environment; some resources can be replaced over time, and others cannot.

subject: Science
description: Obtaining, Evaluating, and Communicating Information - Obtain and combine information from books and other reliable media to explain phenomena or solutions to a design problem.
description_plain: Your child can gather and combine information from books and other trusted sources to explain how things happen or solve problems.

subject: Science
description: Obtain and combine information to describe that energy and fuels are derived from natural resources and that their uses affect the environment.
description_plain: Your child will gather information to explain that energy and fuels come from natural resources and that using them can affect the environment.

subject: Science
description: Patterns - Patterns can be used as evidence to support an explanation.
description_plain: Your child can use patterns as clues to help explain things.

subject: Science
description: Plate Tectonics and Large-Scale System Interactions - The locations of mountain ranges, deep ocean trenches, ocean floor structures, earthquakes, and volcanoes occur in patterns. Most earthquakes and volcanoes occur in bands that are often along the boundaries between continents and oceans. Major mountain chains form inside continents or near their edges. Maps can help locate the different land and water features of Earth.
description_plain: Your child understands that mountains, ocean trenches, earthquakes, and volcanoes happen in patterns, often near edges of continents and oceans, and maps can show where these features are.

subject: Science
description: Analyzing and Interpreting Data - Analyze and interpret data to make sense of phenomena using logical reasoning.
description_plain: Your child can look at data carefully and use thinking skills to understand what it means.

subject: Science
description: Analyze and interpret data from maps to describe patterns of Earth's features.
description_plain: Your child looks at maps and data to describe patterns in Earth’s features.

subject: Science
description: Cause and Effect - Cause-and-effect relationships are routinely identified, tested, and used to explain change.
description_plain: Your child can find, test, and explain cause-and-effect relationships to understand changes.

subject: Science
description: Biogeology - Living things affect the physical characteristics of their regions.
description_plain: Your child knows that living things can change the land and environment where they live.

subject: Science
description: Earth Materials and Systems - Rainfall helps to shape the land and affects the types of living things found in a region. Water, ice, wind, living organisms, and gravity break rocks, soils, and sediments into smaller particles and move them around.
description_plain: Your child understands that rain and other forces like wind and living things break down rocks and move soil, shaping the land and affecting what lives there.

subject: Science
description: Planning and Carrying Out Investigations - Make observations and/or measurements in order to produce data to serve as the basis for evidence for an explanation of a phenomenon.
description_plain: Your child can make observations or measurements to collect data that helps explain something.

subject: Science
description: Make observations and/or measurements to provide evidence of the effects of weathering or the rate of erosion by water, ice, wind, or vegetation.
description_plain: Your child observes or measures how weathering or erosion by water, ice, wind, or plants changes the land.

subject: Science
description: Patterns - Patterns can be used as evidence to support an explanation.
description_plain: Your child can use patterns as evidence to help explain things.

subject: Science
description: The History of Planet Earth - Local, regional, and global patterns of rock formations reveal changes over time due to earth forces, such as earthquakes. The presence and location of certain fossil types indicate the order in which rock layers were formed.
description_plain: Your child understands that patterns in rocks and fossils show how Earth has changed over time.

subject: Science
description: Constructing Explanations and Designing Solutions - Identify the evidence that supports particular points in an explanation.
description_plain: Your child can find the evidence that supports parts of an explanation.

subject: Science
description: Identify evidence from patterns in rock formations and fossils in rock layers to support an explanation for changes in a landscape over time.
description_plain: Your child uses patterns in rocks and fossils to explain how a landscape has changed over time.

subject: Science
description: Scale, Proportion, and Quantity - Observable phenomena exist from very short to very long time periods.
description_plain: Your child understands that events and changes in nature can happen very quickly or take a very long time.

subject: Science
description: Evidence of Common Ancestry and Diversity - Some kinds of plants and animals that once lived on Earth are no longer found anywhere. Fossils provide evidence about the types of organisms that lived long ago and also about the nature of their environments.
description_plain: Your child knows that some plants and animals that lived long ago are now extinct, and fossils help us learn about them and their environments.

subject: Science
description: Analyzing and Interpreting Data - Analyze and interpret data to make sense of phenomena using logical reasoning.
description_plain: Your child can look at information and data carefully to understand what is happening and explain it logically.

subject: Science
description: Analyze and interpret data from fossils to provide evidence of the organisms and the environments in which they lived long ago.
description_plain: Your child can study fossils to find clues about what kinds of living things existed long ago and where they lived.

subject: Science
description: Systems and System Models - A system can be described in terms of its components and their interactions.
description_plain: Your child understands that a system is made up of parts that work together and affect each other.

subject: Science
description: Information Processing Different sense receptors are specialized for particular kinds of information, which may then be processed by the animal's brain. Animals are able to use their perceptions and memories to guide their actions.
description_plain: Your child knows that animals have special senses to gather information, and their brains use this information and memories to decide what to do.

subject: Science
description: Developing and Using Models - Use a model to test interactions concerning the functioning of a natural system.
description_plain: Your child can use a model to test how parts of a natural system work together.

subject: Science
description: Use a model to describe that animals receive different types of information through their senses, process the information in their brain, and respond to the information in different ways.
description_plain: Your child uses a model to show how animals get information through their senses, process it in their brain, and respond in different ways.

subject: Science
description: System and System Models - A system can be described in terms of its components and their interactions
description_plain: Your child understands that a system can be explained by its parts and how they interact.

subject: Science
description: Structure and Function-Plants and animals have both internal and external structures that serve various functions in growth, survival, behavior, and reproduction.
description_plain: Your child knows that plants and animals have inside and outside parts that help them grow, survive, behave, and reproduce.

subject: Science
description: Engaging in Argument from Evidence - Construct an argument with evidence, data, and/or a model.
description_plain: Your child can make a clear argument using evidence, data, or models to support their ideas.

subject: Science
description: Construct an argument that plants and animals have internal and external structures that function to support survival, growth, behavior, and reproduction.
description_plain: Your child explains how plants and animals have body parts inside and outside that help them survive, grow, behave, and reproduce.

subject: Science
description: Patterns - Similarities and differences in patterns can be used to sort and classify designed products.
description_plain: Your child can use similarities and differences in patterns to group and organize man-made objects.

subject: Science
description: Optimizing the Design Solution - Different solutions need to be tested in order to determine which of them best solves the problem, given the criteria and the constraints.
description_plain: Your child understands that trying out different solutions helps find the best way to solve a problem within limits and goals.

subject: Science
description: Information Technologies and Instrumentation - Patterns can encode, send, receive, and decode information.
description_plain: Your child knows that patterns can be used to send, receive, and understand information.

subject: Science
description: Constructing Explanations and Designing Solutions - Generate and compare multiple solutions to a problem based on how well they meet the criteria and constraints of the design solution.
description_plain: Your child can come up with several solutions to a problem and compare how well each one meets the goals and limits.

subject: Science
description: Generate and compare multiple solutions that use patterns to transfer information.
description_plain: Your child thinks of and compares different ways patterns can be used to send information.

subject: Science
description: Cause and Effect - Cause and effect relationships are routinely identified.
description_plain: Your child can identify cause and effect relationships in everyday situations.

subject: Science
description: Electromagnetic Radiation - An object can be seen when light reflected from its surface enters the eyes.
description_plain: Your child understands that we see objects when light bounces off their surfaces and enters our eyes.

subject: Science
description: Developing and Using Models - Develop a model to describe phenomena.
description_plain: Your child can create a model to explain how something happens.

subject: Science
description: Develop a model to describe that light reflecting from objects and entering the eye allows objects to be seen.
description_plain: Your child makes a model to show that we see objects because light reflects off them and enters our eyes.

subject: Science
description: Patterns - Similarities and differences in patterns can be used to sort and classify natural phenomena.
description_plain: Your child can use patterns to sort and classify things found in nature.

subject: Science
description: Wave Properties - Waves, which are regular patterns of motion, can be made in water by disturbing the surface. When waves move across the surface of deep water, the water goes up and down in place; there is no net motion in the direction of the wave except when the water meets a beach. Waves of the same type can differ in amplitude (height of the wave) and wavelength (spacing between wave peaks).
description_plain: Your child knows that waves are regular up-and-down movements in water, and waves can be different heights and distances apart.

subject: Science
description: Developing and Using Models - Develop a model using an analogy, example, or abstract representation to describe a scientific principle.
description_plain: Your child can build a model using examples or comparisons to explain a science idea.

subject: Science
description: Develop a model of waves to describe patterns in terms of amplitude and wavelength and that waves can cause objects to move.
description_plain: Your child can build a simple model to show how waves move, including their height and length, and explain how waves can make things move.

subject: Science
description: Energy and Matter - Energy can be transferred in various ways and between objects.
description_plain: Your child understands that energy can move from one object to another in different ways.

subject: Science
description: Defining Engineering Problems - Possible solutions to a problem are limited by available materials and resources (constraints). The success of a designed solution is determined by considering the desired features of a solution (criteria). Different proposals for solutions can be compared on the basis of how well each one meets the specified criteria for success or how well each takes the constraints into account.
description_plain: Your child knows that the materials and resources available limit possible solutions, and the best solution meets the goals and fits those limits.

subject: Science
description: Energy in Chemical Processes and Everyday Life - The expression "produce energy" typically refers to the conversion of stored energy into a desired form for practical use.
description_plain: Your child understands that 'producing energy' means changing stored energy into a useful form.

subject: Science
description: Conservation of Energy and Energy Transfer - Energy can also be transferred from place to place by electric currents, which can then be used locally to produce motion, sound, heat, or light. The currents may have been produced to begin with by transforming the energy of motion into electrical energy.
description_plain: Your child knows that electric currents can move energy from one place to another and be used to make things move, make sound, heat, or light.

subject: Science
description: Constructing Explanations and Designing Solutions - Apply scientific ideas to solve design problems.
description_plain: Your child can use scientific ideas to help solve design problems.

subject: Science
description: Apply scientific ideas to design, test, and refine a device that converts energy from one form to another
description_plain: Your child can design, test, and improve a device that changes energy from one type to another.

subject: Science
description: Energy and Matter - Energy can be transferred in various ways and between objects.
description_plain: Your child understands that energy can be passed between objects in many ways.

subject: Science
description: Relationship Between Energy and Forces - When objects collide, the contact forces transfer energy so as to change the objects' motions.
description_plain: Your child knows that when objects hit each other, energy moves between them and changes how they move.

subject: Science
description: Conservation of Energy and Energy Transfer - Energy is present whenever there are moving objects, sound, light, or heat. When objects collide, energy can be transferred from one object to another, thereby changing their motion. In such collisions, some energy is typically also transferred to the surrounding air; as a result, the air gets heated and sound is produced.
description_plain: Your child understands that energy is present with moving objects, sound, light, or heat, and collisions transfer energy that can heat air and make sound.

subject: Science
description: Definitions of Energy - Energy can be moved from place to place by moving objects or through sound, light, or electric currents.
description_plain: Your child knows that energy can move from place to place by moving objects, sound, light, or electric currents.

subject: Science
description: Asking Questions and Defining Problems - Ask questions that can be investigated and predict reasonable outcomes based on patterns such as cause-and-effect relationships.
description_plain: Your child can ask questions that can be tested and make predictions based on cause and effect.

subject: Science
description: Ask questions and predict outcomes about the changes in energy that occur when objects collide.
description_plain: Your child will ask questions and guess what will happen to energy when objects bump into each other.

subject: Science
description: Energy and Matter - Energy can be transferred in various ways and between objects.
description_plain: Your child understands that energy can be transferred in different ways and between objects.

subject: Science
description: Conservation of Energy and Energy Transfer - Energy is present whenever there are moving objects, sound, light, or heat. When objects collide, energy can be transferred from one object to another, thereby changing their motion. In such collisions, some energy is typically also transferred to the surrounding air; as a result, the air gets heated and sound is produced. Light also transfers energy from place to place. Energy can also be transferred from place to place by electric currents, which can then be used locally to produce motion, sound, heat, or light. The currents may have been produced to begin with by transforming the energy of motion into electrical energy.
description_plain: Your child knows that energy is present with moving objects, sound, light, or heat, and that collisions and electric currents transfer energy and can produce motion, sound, heat, or light.

subject: Science
description: Definitions of Energy - Energy can be moved from place to place by moving objects or through sound, light, or electric currents.
description_plain: Your child understands that energy can move from one place to another by moving objects, sound, light, or electric currents.

subject: Science
