description: Planning and Carrying Out Investigations - Make observations in order to produce data to serve as the basis for evidence for an explanation of a phenomenon or test a design solution.
description_plain: Your child can make observations to collect information that helps explain something or test a design.

subject: Science
description: Make observations to provide evidence that energy can be transferred from place to place by sound, light, heat, and electric currents.
description_plain: Your child can watch and describe how energy moves from one place to another through sound, light, heat, and electricity.

subject: Science
description: Energy and Matter - Energy can be transferred in various ways and between objects.
description_plain: Your child understands that energy can move between objects in many ways.

subject: Science
description: Definitions of Energy - The faster a given object is moving, the more energy it possesses.
description_plain: Your child knows that the faster an object moves, the more energy it has.

subject: Science
description: Constructing Explanations and Designing Solutions - Use evidence (e.g., measurements, observations, patterns) to construct an explanation.
description_plain: Your child can use evidence like measurements and observations to explain what is happening.

subject: Science
description: Use evidence to construct an explanation relating the speed of an object to the energy of that object.
description_plain: Your child will use evidence to explain how the speed of something relates to its energy.

subject: Science
description: Optimizing the Design Solution - Different solutions need to be tested in order to determine which of them best solves the problem, given the criteria and the constraints.
description_plain: Your child understands that testing different solutions helps find the best way to solve a problem within limits and goals.

subject: Science
description: Developing Possible Solutions - Tests are often designed to identify failure points or difficulties, which suggest the elements of the design that need to be improved.
description_plain: Your child knows that tests can show what parts of a design need to be improved.

subject: Science
description: Planning and Carrying Out Investigations - Plan and conduct an investigation collaboratively in order to produce data to serve as the basis for evidence, using fair tests in which variables are controlled and the number of trials is considered.
description_plain: Your child can work with others to plan and do fair tests that control variables and repeat trials to collect good information.

subject: Science
description: Plan and carry out fair tests in which variables are controlled and failure points are considered to identify aspects of a model or prototype that can be improved.
description_plain: Your child can plan and do fair tests by controlling variables and thinking about what might go wrong to find ways to improve a model or prototype.

subject: Science
description: Developing Possible Solutions - Research on a problem should be carried out before beginning to design a solution. Testing a solution involves investigating how well it performs under a range of likely conditions. At whatever stage, communicating with peers about proposed solutions is an important part of the design process, and shared ideas can lead to improved designs.
description_plain: Your child can research a problem before designing a solution, test how well the solution works in different situations, and share ideas with others to improve the design.

subject: Science
description: Constructing Explanations and Designing Solutions - Generate and compare multiple solutions to a problem based on how well they meet the criteria and constraints of the design problem.
description_plain: Your child can come up with several solutions to a problem and compare how well each one meets the goals and limits of the problem.

subject: Science
description: Generate and compare multiple possible solutions to a problem based on how well each is likely to meet the criteria and constraints of the problem.
description_plain: Your child can come up with and compare several possible solutions to a problem to see which ones best meet the goals and limits.

subject: Science
description: Defining and Delimiting Engineering Problems - Possible solutions to a problem are limited by available materials and resources (constraints). The success of a designed solution is determined by considering the desired features of a solution (criteria). Different proposals for solutions can be compared on the basis of how well each one meets the specified criteria for success or how well each takes the constraints into account.
description_plain: Your child understands that solutions to problems depend on what materials and resources are available, and can compare different ideas based on how well they meet the goals and limits.

subject: Science
description: Asking Questions and Defining Problems - Define a simple design problem that can be solved through the development of an object, tool, process, or system and includes several criteria for success and constraints on materials, time, or cost.
description_plain: Your child can define a simple design problem that includes goals to meet and limits like materials, time, or cost.

subject: Science
description: Define a simple design problem reflecting a need or a want that includes specified criteria for success and constraints on materials, time, or cost.
description_plain: Your child can describe a simple design problem based on a need or want, including what success looks like and limits like materials, time, or cost.

subject: Science
description: Systems and System Models - A system can be described in terms of its components and their interactions.
description_plain: Your child understands that a system is made up of parts that work together and affect each other.

subject: Science
description: Defining and Delimiting Engineering Problems - Possible solutions to a problem are limited by available materials and resources (constraints). The success of a designed solution is determined by considering the desired features of a solution (criteria). Different proposals for solutions can be compared on the basis of how well each one meets the specified criteria for success or how well each takes the constraints into account.
description_plain: Your child can understand that when solving a problem, the materials and resources available limit the possible solutions, and they can compare different ideas based on how well they meet the goals and fit those limits.

subject: Science
description: Human Impacts on Earth Systems - Human activities in agriculture, industry, and everyday life have had major effects on the land, vegetation, streams, oceans, air, and even outer space. But individuals and communities are doing things to help protect Earth's resources and environments.
description_plain: Your child understands that people’s activities affect the land, water, air, and even space, but individuals and communities can take actions to protect Earth's resources.

subject: Science
description: Obtaining, Evaluating, and Communicating Information - Obtain and combine information from books and/or other reliable media to explain phenomena or solutions to a design problem.
description_plain: Your child can find and combine information from books or trusted sources to explain natural events or how to solve design problems.

subject: Science
description: Obtain and combine information about solutions individual communities use to protect the Earth's resources and environment.
description_plain: Your child can gather and combine information about how different communities protect Earth's resources and environment.

subject: Science
description: Scale, Proportion, and Quantity - Standard units are used to measure and describe physical quantities such as weight and volume.
description_plain: Your child can use standard units to measure and describe things like weight and volume.

subject: Science
description: The Roles of Water in Earth's Surface Processes - Nearly all of Earth's available water is in the ocean. Most fresh water is in glaciers or underground; only a tiny fraction is in streams, lakes, wetlands, and the atmosphere.
description_plain: Your child understands that most of Earth's water is in the ocean, most fresh water is frozen or underground, and only a small amount is found in streams, lakes, wetlands, and the air.

subject: Science
description: Using Mathematics and Computational Thinking - Describe and graph quantities such as area and volume to address scientific questions.
description_plain: Your child can describe and make graphs of measurements like area and volume to answer science questions.

subject: Science
description: Describe and graph the amounts and percentages of salt water and fresh water in various reservoirs to provide evidence about the distribution of water on Earth.
description_plain: Your child can describe and graph how much salt water and fresh water are in different places on Earth to show how water is spread out.

subject: Science
description: Systems and System Models - A system can be described in terms of its components and their interactions.
description_plain: Your child understands that a system is made up of parts that work together.

subject: Science
description: Earth Materials and Systems - Earth's major systems are the geosphere (solid and molten rock, soil, and sediments), the hydrosphere (water and ice), the atmosphere (air), and the biosphere (living things, including humans). These systems interact in multiple ways to affect Earth's surface materials and processes. The ocean supports a variety of ecosystems and organisms, shapes landforms, and influences climate. Winds and clouds in the atmosphere interact with the landforms to determine patterns of weather.
description_plain: Your child understands that Earth's major parts include land, water, air, and living things, and these parts interact to shape the surface, weather, and climate.

subject: Science
description: Developing and Using Models - Develop a model using an example to describe a scientific principle.
description_plain: Your child can create a model using an example to explain a science idea.

subject: Science
description: Develop a model using an example to describe ways the geosphere, biosphere, hydrosphere, and/or atmosphere interact.
description_plain: Your child can build a model to show how land, living things, water, and air work together.

subject: Science
description: Patterns - Similarities and differences in patterns can be used to sort, classify, communicate, and analyze simple rates of change for natural phenomena.
description_plain: Your child can use patterns to sort, classify, and understand simple changes in nature.

subject: Science
description: Earth and the Solar System - The orbits of Earth around the sun and of the moon around Earth, together with the rotation of Earth about an axis between its north and south poles, cause observable patterns. These include day and night; daily changes in the length and direction of shadows; and different positions of the sun, moon, and stars at different times of the day, month, and year.
description_plain: Your child understands that Earth's movement around the sun and the moon's movement around Earth cause patterns like day and night, changing shadows, and the positions of the sun, moon, and stars.

subject: Science
description: Analyzing and Interpreting Data - Represent data in graphical displays (bar graphs, pictographs, and/or pie charts) to reveal patterns that indicate relationships.
description_plain: Your child can show data in graphs like bar graphs or pie charts to find patterns and relationships.

subject: Science
description: Represent data in graphical displays to reveal patterns of daily changes in the length and direction of shadows, day and night, and the seasonal appearance of some stars in the night sky.
description_plain: Your child can make graphs to show daily changes in shadows, day and night, and how some stars appear in different seasons.

subject: Science
description: Scale, Proportion, and Quantity - Natural objects exist from the very small to the immensely large.
description_plain: Your child understands that natural things can be very small or very large.

subject: Science
description: The Universe and Its Stars - The sun is a star that appears larger and brighter than other stars because it is closer. Stars range greatly in their distance from Earth.
description_plain: Your child understands that the sun is a star that looks bigger and brighter because it is closer to Earth than other stars.

subject: Science
description: Engaging in Argument from Evidence - Support an argument with evidence, data, or a model.
description_plain: Your child can support their ideas by using evidence, data, or models.

subject: Science
description: Support an argument that differences in the apparent brightness of the sun compared to other stars is due to their relative distances from Earth.
description_plain: Your child can explain why the sun looks brighter than other stars because it is closer to Earth.

subject: Science
description: Systems and System Models - A system can be described in terms of its components and their interactions.
description_plain: Your child understands that a system has parts that interact with each other.

subject: Science
description: Cycles of Matter and Energy Transfer in Ecosystems - Matter cycles between the air and soil and among plants, animals, and microbes as these organisms live and die. Organisms obtain gases and water from the environment, and then release waste matter (gas, liquid, or solid) back into the environment.
description_plain: Your child understands that matter moves between air, soil, plants, animals, and microbes as they live and die, and organisms take in gases and water and release waste back into the environment.

subject: Science
description: Interdependent Relationships in Ecosystems - The food of almost any kind of animal can be traced back to plants. Organisms are related in food webs in which some animals eat plants for food and other animals eat the animals that eat plants. Some organisms, such as fungi and bacteria, break down dead organisms (both plants or plant parts and animals) and therefore operate as "decomposers." Decomposition eventually restores (recycles) some materials to the soil. Organisms can survive only in environments in which their particular needs are met. A healthy ecosystem is one in which multiple species of different types are each able to meet their needs in a relatively stable web of life. Newly introduced species can damage the balance of an ecosystem.
description_plain: Your child understands that animals get their food from plants or other animals, decomposers break down dead things to recycle materials, and a healthy ecosystem has many species living together in balance.

subject: Science
description: Developing and Using Models - Develop a model to describe phenomena.
description_plain: Your child can create a model to explain how something works or happens.

subject: Science
description: Develop a model to describe the movement of matter among plants, animals, decomposers, and the environment.
description_plain: Your child can create a model to show how matter moves between plants, animals, decomposers, and the environment.

subject: Science
description: Energy and Matter - Matter is transported into, out of, and within systems.
description_plain: Your child understands that matter moves into, out of, and within living systems.

subject: Science
description: Organization for Matter and Energy Flow in Organisms - Plants acquire their material for growth chiefly from air and water.
description_plain: Your child understands that plants get most of the materials they need to grow from air and water.

subject: Science
description: Engaging in Argument from Evidence - Support an argument with evidence, data, or a model.
description_plain: Your child can support their ideas using evidence, data, or models.

subject: Science
description: Support an argument that plants get the materials they need for growth chiefly from air and water.
description_plain: Your child can explain that plants get most of what they need to grow from air and water.

subject: Science
description: Energy and Matter - Energy can be transferred in various ways and between objects.
description_plain: Your child understands that energy can move in different ways and between objects.

subject: Science
description: Organization for Matter and Energy Flow in Organisms - Food provides animals with the materials they need for body repair and growth and the energy they need to maintain body warmth and for motion.
description_plain: Your child understands that animals get materials from food to grow and repair their bodies and get energy to stay warm and move.

subject: Science
description: Energy in Chemical Processes and Everyday Life - The energy released [from] food was once energy from the sun that was captured by plants in the chemical process that forms plant matter (from air and water).
description_plain: Your child understands that the energy in food originally comes from the sun, which plants capture to make their matter.

subject: Science
description: Developing and Using Models - Use models to describe phenomena.
description_plain: Your child can use models to explain how things happen.

subject: Science
description: Use models to describe that energy in animals' food (used for body repair, growth, motion, and to maintain body warmth) was once energy from the sun.
description_plain: Your child can use models to show that the energy animals get from food originally comes from the sun.

subject: Science
description: Cause and Effect - Cause-and-effect relationships are routinely identified and used to explain change.
description_plain: Your child understands that cause-and-effect relationships help explain why things change.

subject: Science
description: Types of Interactions - The gravitational force of Earth acting on an object near Earth's surface pulls that object toward the planet's center.
description_plain: Your child understands that Earth's gravity pulls objects toward its center.

subject: Science
description: Engaging in Argument from Evidence - Support an argument with evidence, data, or a model.
description_plain: Your child can support their ideas with evidence, data, or models.

subject: Science
description: Support an argument that the gravitational force exerted by Earth on objects is directed down.
description_plain: Your child can explain that Earth's gravity pulls objects straight down.

subject: Science
description: Cause and Effect - Cause-and-effect relationships are routinely identified, tested, and used to explain change.
description_plain: Your child understands that cause-and-effect relationships can be tested and used to explain changes.

subject: Science
description: Chemical Reactions - When two or more different substances are mixed, a new substance with different properties may be formed.
description_plain: Your child understands that when different substances are mixed, they can create a new substance with new properties.

subject: Science
description: Planning and Carrying Out Investigations - Conduct an investigation collaboratively to produce data to serve as the basis for evidence, using fair tests in which variables are controlled and the number of trials is considered.
description_plain: Your child can work with others to do an investigation that produces data using fair tests where variables are controlled and enough trials are done.

subject: Science
description: Conduct an investigation to determine whether the mixing of two or more substances results in new substances.
description_plain: Your child can do an experiment to find out if mixing substances creates new substances.

subject: Science
description: Scale, Proportion, and Quantity - Standard units are used to measure and describe physical quantities such as weight, time, temperature, and volume.
description_plain: Your child can use standard units to measure and describe things like weight, time, temperature, and volume.

subject: Science
description: Structure and Properties of Matter - Measurements of a variety of properties can be used to identify materials. (Boundary: At this grade level, mass and weight are not distinguished, and no attempt is made to define the unseen particles or explain the atomic-scale mechanism of evaporation and condensation.)
description_plain: Your child understands that measuring different properties helps identify materials.

subject: Science
description: Planning and Carrying Out Investigations - Make observations and measurements in order to produce data to serve as the basis for evidence for an explanation of a phenomenon.
description_plain: Your child can make observations and measurements to collect data that helps explain a science event.

subject: Science
description: Make observations and measurements to identify materials based on their properties.
description_plain: Your child can observe and measure materials to identify them by their properties.

subject: Science
description: Scale, Proportion, and Quantity - Standard units are used to measure and describe physical quantities such as weight, time, temperature, and volume.
description_plain: Your child can use standard units to measure and describe weight, time, temperature, and volume.

subject: Science
description: Chemical Reactions - No matter what reaction or change in properties occurs, the total weight of the substances does not change. (Boundary: Mass and weight are not distinguished at this grade level.)
description_plain: Your child understands that no matter what changes happen in a reaction, the total weight of the substances stays the same.

subject: Science
description: Structure and Properties of Matter - Measurements of a variety of properties can be used to identify materials. (Boundary: At this grade level, mass and weight are not distinguished, and no attempt is made to define the unseen particles or explain the atomic-scale mechanism of evaporation and condensation.)
description_plain: Your child can use different measurements to identify materials, like how heavy or dense they are.

subject: Science
description: Using Mathematics and Computational Thinking - Measure and graph quantities such as weight to address scientific and engineering questions and problems.
description_plain: Your child can measure things like weight and make graphs to help answer science questions.

subject: Science
description: Measure and graph quantities to provide evidence that regardless of the type of change that occurs when heating, cooling, or mixing substances, the total weight of matter is conserved.
description_plain: Your child can measure and graph amounts to show that matter's weight stays the same during heating, cooling, or mixing.

subject: Science
description: Scale, Proportion, and Quantity - Natural objects exist from the very small to the immensely large.
description_plain: Your child understands that natural things come in all sizes, from very tiny to very huge.

subject: Science
description: Structure and Properties of Matter - Matter of any type can be subdivided into particles that are too small to see, but even then, the matter still exists and can be detected by other means. A model showing that gases are made from matter particles that are too small to see and are moving freely around in space can explain many observations, including the inflation and shape of a balloon and the effects of air on larger particles or objects.
description_plain: Your child understands that all matter is made of tiny particles too small to see, and these particles move around, which explains things like how balloons stay inflated.

subject: Science
description: Developing and Using Models - Develop a model to describe phenomena.
description_plain: Your child can create a simple model to explain how something works or happens.

subject: Science
description: Develop a model to describe that matter is made of particles too small to be seen.
description_plain: Your child can create a model to show that matter is made of tiny particles too small to see.

subject: Science
description: Optimizing the Design Solution - The iterative process of testing the most promising solutions and modifying what is proposed on the basis of the test results leads to greater refinement and ultimately to an optimal solution.
description_plain: Your child learns to test ideas, make changes based on results, and improve designs step by step to find the best solution.

subject: Science
description: Developing Possible Solutions - A solution needs to be tested, and then modified on the basis of the test results, in order to improve it. Models of all kinds are important for testing solutions.
description_plain: Your child understands that solutions need to be tested and changed based on test results, and that models help with testing ideas.

subject: Science
description: Developing and Using Models - Develop a model to generate data to test ideas about designed systems, including those representing inputs and outputs.
description_plain: Your child can build a model to collect data and test how a system works, including what goes in and what comes out.

subject: Science
description: Develop a model to generate data for iterative testing and modification of a proposed object, tool, or process such that an optimal design can be achieved.
description_plain: Your child can create a model to test and improve a design step-by-step until it works well.

subject: Science
description: Optimizing the Design Solution - Although one design may not perform the best across all tests, identifying the characteristics of the design that performed the best in each test can provide useful information for the redesign process—that is, some of those characteristics may be incorporated into the new design.
description_plain: Your child knows that even if one design isn’t perfect in every way, the best parts can be used to make a better new design.

subject: Science
description: Developing Possible Solutions - There are systematic processes for evaluating solutions with respect to how well they meet the criteria and constraints of a problem. Sometimes parts of different solutions can be combined to create a solution that is better than any of its predecessors.
description_plain: Your child can compare different solutions to see which meet the problem’s needs best and combine good parts from each to make a better solution.

subject: Science
description: Analyzing and Interpreting Data - Analyze and interpret data to determine similarities and differences in findings.
description_plain: Your child can look at data carefully to find what is the same and what is different in the results.

subject: Science
description: Analyze data from tests to determine similarities and differences among several design solutions to identify the best characteristics of each that can be combined into a new solution to better meet the criteria for success.
description_plain: Your child can study test results to find the best parts of different designs and combine them to make a better solution.

subject: Science
description: Developing Possible Solutions - There are systematic processes for evaluating solutions with respect to how well they meet the criteria and constraints of a problem.
description_plain: Your child understands how to check solutions step by step to see if they meet the problem’s requirements and limits.

subject: Science
description: Engaging in Argument from Evidence - Evaluate competing design solutions based on jointly developed and agreed-upon design criteria.
description_plain: Your child can compare different design ideas and decide which is better based on agreed rules.

subject: Science
description: Evaluate competing design solutions using a systematic process to determine how well they meet the criteria and constraints of the problem.
description_plain: Your child can compare different design ideas carefully to see which one best meets the problem's needs and limits.

subject: Science
description: Defining and Delimiting Engineering Problems - The more precisely a design task's criteria and constraints can be defined, the more likely it is that the designed solution will be successful. Specification of constraints includes consideration of scientific principles and other relevant knowledge that are likely to limit possible solutions.
description_plain: Your child knows that clearly stating what a design must do and what limits it has helps make a successful solution.

subject: Science
description: Asking Questions and Defining Problems - Define a design problem that can be solved through the development of an object, tool, process or system and includes multiple criteria and constraints, including scientific knowledge that may limit possible solutions.
description_plain: Your child can describe a problem that can be solved by making something new, including rules and limits based on science.

subject: Science
description: Define the criteria and constraints of a design problem with sufficient precision to ensure a successful solution, taking into account relevant scientific principles and potential impacts on people and the natural environment that may limit possible solutions.
description_plain: Your child can clearly describe the goals and limits of a design problem, considering science and how it affects people and the environment.

subject: Science
description: Systems and System Models - Models can be used to represent systems and their interactions—such as inputs, processes and outputs—and energy, matter, and information flows within systems.
description_plain: Your child can use models to show how parts of a system work together, including how energy, matter, and information move through it.

subject: Science
description: Weather and Climate - Weather and climate are influenced by interactions involving sunlight, the ocean, the atmosphere, ice, landforms, and living things. These interactions vary with latitude, altitude, and local and regional geography, all of which can affect oceanic and atmospheric flow patterns. The ocean exerts a major influence on weather and climate by absorbing energy from the sun, releasing it over time, and globally redistributing it through ocean currents.
description_plain: Your child understands that weather and climate are affected by how sunlight, oceans, air, ice, land, and living things interact in different places.

subject: Science
description: The Roles of Water in Earth's Surface Processes - Variations in density due to variations in temperature and salinity drive a global pattern of interconnected ocean currents.
description_plain: Your child knows that differences in water temperature and saltiness cause ocean currents that connect around the world.

subject: Science
description: Developing and Using Models - Develop and use a model to describe phenomena.
description_plain: Your child can build and use a model to explain how something happens.

subject: Science
description: Develop and use a model to describe how unequal heating and rotation of the Earth cause patterns of atmospheric and oceanic circulation that determine regional climates.
description_plain: Your child can build and use a model to show how uneven heating and Earth's spinning cause patterns in air and ocean movement that affect climate.

subject: Science
description: Cause and Effect - Cause-and-effect relationships may be used to predict phenomena in natural or designed systems.
description_plain: Your child understands that cause-and-effect relationships can help predict what will happen in natural or designed systems.

subject: Science
description: Weather and Climate - Because these patterns are so complex, weather can only be predicted probabilistically.
description_plain: Your child knows that because weather is very complex, it can only be predicted as a chance or probability.

subject: Science
description: The Roles of Water in Earth's Surface Processes - The complex patterns of the changes and the movement of water in the atmosphere, determined by winds, landforms, and ocean temperatures and currents, are major determinants of local weather patterns.
description_plain: Your child understands that local weather depends on how water moves in the air, affected by winds, land shapes, and ocean temperatures.

subject: Science
description: Planning and Carrying Out Investigations - Collect data to produce data to serve as the basis for evidence to answer scientific questions or test design solutions under a range of conditions.
description_plain: Your child can collect data to help answer science questions or test ideas under different conditions.

subject: Science
description: Collect data to provide evidence for how the motions and complex interactions of air masses result in changes in weather conditions.
description_plain: Your child can gather information to show how moving air masses cause changes in weather.

subject: Science
description: Energy and Matter - Within a natural or designed system, the transfer of energy drives the motion and/or cycling of matter.
description_plain: Your child understands that energy moving through a system causes matter to move or change.

subject: Science
description: The Roles of Water in Earth's Surface Processes - Water continually cycles among land, ocean, and atmosphere via transpiration, evaporation, condensation and crystallization, and precipitation, as well as downhill flows on land. Global movements of water and its changes in form are propelled by sunlight and gravity.
description_plain: Your child knows that water moves between land, ocean, and air through processes like evaporation and precipitation, powered by sunlight and gravity.

subject: Science
description: Developing and Using Models - Develop a model to describe unobservable mechanisms.
description_plain: Your child can create a model to explain things that can’t be seen directly.

subject: Science
description: Develop a model to describe the cycling of water through Earth's systems driven by energy from the sun and the force of gravity.
description_plain: Your child can create a model to show how water moves through Earth's systems using energy from the sun and gravity.

subject: Science
description: Patterns - Patterns in rates of change and other numerical relationships can provide information about natural systems.
description_plain: Your child can find patterns in how things change over time to learn about natural systems.

subject: Science
description: Plate Tectonics and Large-Scale System Interactions - Maps of ancient land and water patterns, based on investigations of rocks and fossils, make clear how Earth's plates have moved great distances, collided, and spread apart.
description_plain: Your child understands that maps of old land and water show how Earth’s plates have moved, crashed, and spread apart over time.

subject: Science
description: The History of Planet Earth - Tectonic processes continually generate new ocean sea floor at ridges and destroy old sea floor at trenches.
description_plain: Your child knows that Earth’s tectonic activity creates new ocean floor at ridges and destroys old ocean floor at trenches.

subject: Science
description: Analyzing and Interpreting Data - Analyze and interpret data to provide evidence for phenomena.
description_plain: Your child can study and explain data to support ideas about natural events.

subject: Science
description: Analyze and interpret data on the distribution of fossils and rocks, continental shapes, and seafloor structures to provide evidence of past plate motions.
description_plain: Your child can look at fossils, rocks, and land shapes to find evidence of how Earth’s plates have moved in the past.

subject: Science
description: Scale, Proportion, and Quantity- Time, space, and energy phenomena can be observed at various scales using models to study systems that are too large or too small.
description_plain: Your child understands that time, space, and energy can be studied at different sizes using models to learn about systems too big or small to see.

subject: Science
description: Biogeology - The evolution and proliferation of living things over geological time have in turn changed the rates of weathering and erosion of land surfaces, altered the composition of Earth's soils and atmosphere, and affected the distribution of water in the hydrosphere.
description_plain: Your child knows that living things have changed how land wears down, soil forms, air is made, and water moves over Earth’s history.

subject: Science
description: The Roles of Water in Earth's Surface Processes - Water's movements—both on the land and underground—cause weathering and erosion, which change the land's surface features and create underground formations.
description_plain: Your child understands that water moving on land and underground causes weathering and erosion, changing land shapes and creating underground features.

subject: Science
description: Earth's Materials and Systems - The planet's systems interact over scales that range from microscopic to global in size, and they operate over fractions of a second to billions of years. These interactions have shaped Earth's history and will determine its future.
description_plain: Your child knows that Earth’s systems work together at all sizes and times, shaping Earth’s past and future.

subject: Science
description: Constructing Explanations and Designing Solutions - Construct a scientific explanation based on valid and reliable evidence obtained from sources (including the students' own experiments) and the assumption that theories and laws that describe nature operate today as they did in the past and will continue to do so in the future.
description_plain: Your child can explain scientific ideas using good evidence from experiments and trusted sources, knowing that natural laws work the same now and in the past.

subject: Science
description: Construct an explanation based on evidence for how biological and geoscience processes have changed Earth's surface at varying time and spatial scales.
description_plain: Your child can explain how living things and Earth processes have changed Earth’s surface over different times and places using evidence.

subject: Science
description: Stability and Change - Explanations of stability and change in natural or designed systems can be constructed by examining the changes over time and processes at different scales, including the atomic scale.
description_plain: Your child can explain how things stay the same or change in natural or designed systems by looking at changes over time and at different sizes.

subject: Science
description: Earth's Materials and Systems - All Earth processes are the result of energy flowing and matter cycling within and among the planet's systems. This energy is derived from the sun and Earth's hot interior. The energy that flows and matter that cycles produce chemical and physical changes in Earth's materials and living organisms.
description_plain: Your child understands that all Earth processes happen because energy from the sun and Earth’s inside moves and matter cycles, causing changes in Earth and living things.

subject: Science
description: Developing and Using Models - Develop and use a model to describe phenomena.
description_plain: Your child can create and use a simple model to explain how something works or happens.

subject: Science
description: Develop a model to describe the cycling of Earth's materials and the flow of energy that drives this process.
description_plain: Your child will build a model to show how Earth's materials move and how energy makes this happen.

subject: Science
description: Scale, Proportion, and Quantity - Time, space, and energy phenomena can be observed at various scales using models to study systems that are too large or too small.
description_plain: Your child understands that things like time, space, and energy can be studied using models when they are too big or too small to see.

subject: Science
description: Earth and the Solar System - The solar system consists of the sun and a collection of objects, including planets, their moons, and asteroids that are held in orbit around the sun by its gravitational pull on them.
description_plain: Your child understands that the solar system has the sun, planets, moons, and asteroids all held in place by the sun’s gravity.

subject: Science
description: Analyzing and Interpreting Data - Analyze and interpret data to determine similarities and differences in findings.
description_plain: Your child can look at data and figure out what is the same and what is different in the results.

subject: Science
description: Analyze and interpret data to determine scale properties of objects in the solar system.
description_plain: Your child can study data to understand the sizes and distances of objects in the solar system.

subject: Science
description: Systems and System Models - Models can be used to represent systems and their interactions.
description_plain: Your child understands that models can show how different parts of a system work together.

subject: Science
description: Earth and the Solar System - The solar system consists of the sun and a collection of objects, including planets, their moons, and asteroids that are held in orbit around the sun by its gravitational pull on them. The solar system appears to have formed from a disk of dust and gas, drawn together by gravity.
description_plain: Your child understands that the solar system formed from a spinning disk of dust and gas pulled together by gravity.

subject: Science
description: The Universe and Its Stars - Earth and its solar system are part of the Milky Way galaxy, which is one of many galaxies in the universe.
description_plain: Your child understands that Earth and the solar system are part of the Milky Way galaxy, which is one of many galaxies.

subject: Science
description: Developing and Using Models - Develop and use a model to describe phenomena.
description_plain: Your child can create and use a model to explain how something happens.

subject: Science
description: Develop and use a model to describe the role of gravity in the motions within galaxies and the solar system.
description_plain: Your child will build a model to show how gravity affects the movement of things in galaxies and the solar system.

subject: Science
description: Patterns - Patterns can be used to identify cause-and-effect relationships.
description_plain: Your child understands that patterns can help find cause-and-effect relationships.

subject: Science
description: Earth and the Solar System - This model of the solar system can explain eclipses of the sun and the moon. Earth's spin axis is fixed in direction over the short-term but tilted relative to its orbit around the sun. The seasons are a result of that tilt and are caused by the differential intensity of sunlight on different areas of Earth across the year.
description_plain: Your child understands that the solar system model explains eclipses and that Earth's tilt causes seasons by changing sunlight intensity.

subject: Science
description: The Universe and Its Stars - Patterns of the apparent motion of the sun, the moon, and stars in the sky can be observed, described, predicted, and explained with models.
description_plain: Your child can observe and explain patterns in how the sun, moon, and stars move in the sky using models.

subject: Science
description: Developing and Using Models - Develop and use a model to describe phenomena.
description_plain: Your child can make and use a model to explain how something works.

subject: Science
description: Develop and use a model of the Earth-sun-moon system to describe the cyclic patterns of lunar phases, eclipses of the sun and moon, and seasons.
description_plain: Your child will create a model of the Earth, sun, and moon to explain lunar phases, eclipses, and seasons.

subject: Science
description: Energy and Matter - The transfer of energy can be tracked as energy flows through a natural system.
description_plain: Your child understands that energy moves through natural systems and causes matter to move or change.

subject: Science
description: Cycle of Matter and Energy Transfer in Ecosystems - Food webs are models that demonstrate how matter and energy are transferred between producers, consumers, and decomposers as the three groups interact within an ecosystem. Transfers of matter into and out of the physical environment occur at every level. Decomposers recycle nutrients from dead plant or animal matter back to the soil in terrestrial environments or to the water in aquatic environments. The atoms that make up the organisms in an ecosystem are cycled repeatedly between the living and non-living parts of the ecosystem.
description_plain: Your child understands that food webs show how energy and matter move between plants, animals, and decomposers in an ecosystem.

subject: Science
description: Developing and Using Models - Develop a model to describe phenomena.
description_plain: Your child can create a model to explain how something happens.

subject: Science
description: Develop a model to describe the cycling of matter and flow of energy among living and non-living parts of an ecosystem.
description_plain: Your child will build a model to show how matter cycles and energy flows between living things and their environment.

subject: Science
description: Patterns - Patterns can be used to identify cause-and-effect relationships.
description_plain: Your child understands that patterns help explain cause-and-effect in nature.

subject: Science
description: Interdependent Relationships in Ecosystems - Predatory interactions may reduce the number of organisms or eliminate whole populations of organisms. Mutually beneficial interactions, in contrast, may become so interdependent that each organism requires the other for survival. Although the species involved in these competitive, predatory, and mutually beneficial interactions vary across ecosystems, the patterns of interactions of organisms with their environments, both living and non-living, are shared.
description_plain: Your child understands that animals interact in different ways like hunting or helping each other, and these patterns happen in all ecosystems.

subject: Science
description: Constructing Explanations and Designing Solutions - Construct an explanation that includes qualitative or quantitative relationships between variables that predict phenomena.
description_plain: Your child can explain and predict how changes in one thing affect another using observations or measurements.

subject: Science
description: Construct an explanation that predicts patterns of interaction among organisms across multiple ecosystems.
description_plain: Your child will explain how animals and plants interact in different ecosystems and predict patterns in these interactions.

subject: Science
description: Cause and Effect - Cause-and-effect relationships may be used to predict phenomena in natural or designed systems.
description_plain: Your child understands that cause-and-effect helps predict what will happen in natural or designed systems.

subject: Science
description: Interdependent Relationships in Ecosystems - Organisms and populations of organisms are dependent on their environmental interactions both with other living things and with non-living factors. In any ecosystem, organisms and populations with similar requirements for food, water, oxygen, or other resources may compete with each other for limited resources, access to which consequently constrains their growth and reproduction. The growth of organisms and population increases are limited by access to resources.
description_plain: Your child understands that living things depend on their environment and compete for resources like food and water, which limits their growth.

subject: Science
description: Analyzing and Interpreting Data - Analyze and interpret data to provide evidence for phenomena.
description_plain: Your child can study data and explain what it shows about how things happen.

subject: Science
description: Analyze and interpret data to provide evidence for the effects of resource availability on organisms and populations of organisms in an ecosystem.
description_plain: Your child will look at data to understand how the amount of resources affects animals and plants in an ecosystem.

subject: Science
description: Energy and Matter - Within a natural system, the transfer of energy drives the motion and/or cycling of matter.
description_plain: Your child understands that energy moving through a system causes matter to move or change.

subject: Science
description: Organization for Matter and Energy Flow in Organisms - Plants, algae (including phytoplankton), and many microorganisms use the energy from light to make sugars (food) from carbon dioxide in the atmosphere and water through the process of photosynthesis, which also releases oxygen. These sugars can be used immediately or stored for growth or later use.
description_plain: Your child understands that plants and some tiny organisms use sunlight to make food and oxygen through photosynthesis.

subject: Science
description: Constructing Explanations and Designing Solutions - Construct a scientific explanation based on valid and reliable evidence obtained from sources (including the students' own experiments) and the assumption that theories and laws that describe the natural world operate today as they did in the past and will continue to do so in the future.
description_plain: Your child can explain scientific ideas using evidence from experiments and reliable sources.

subject: Science
description: Construct a scientific explanation based on evidence for the role of photosynthesis in the cycling of matter and flow of energy into and out of organisms.
description_plain: Your child will explain how photosynthesis helps move matter and energy in living things using evidence.

subject: Science
description: Systems and System Models - Models can be used to represent systems and their interactions—such as inputs, processes, and outputs—and energy and matter flows within systems.
description_plain: Your child understands that models can show how systems work, including how energy and matter move through them.

subject: Science
description: Types of Interactions - Gravitational forces are always attractive. There is a gravitational force between any two masses, but it is very small except when one or both of the objects have a large mass—e.g., Earth and the sun.
description_plain: Your child understands that gravity always pulls objects together and is stronger when objects have more mass.

subject: Science
description: Engaging in Argument from Evidence - Construct and present oral and written arguments supported by empirical evidence and scientific reasoning to support or refute an explanation or a model for a phenomenon or a solution to a problem.
description_plain: Your child can make and share arguments using evidence to support or disagree with an explanation or solution.

subject: Science
description: Construct and present arguments using evidence to support the claim that gravitational interactions are attractive and depend on the masses of interacting objects.
description_plain: Your child will use evidence to explain that gravity pulls objects together and depends on their masses.

subject: Science
description: Systems and System Models - Models can be used to represent systems and their interactions—such as inputs, processes, and outputs—and energy and matter flows within systems.
description_plain: Your child understands that models can show how systems work, including inputs, processes, and outputs.

subject: Science
description: Forces and Motion - For any pair of interacting objects, the force exerted by the first object on the second object is equal in strength to the force that the second object exerts on the first, but in the opposite direction (Newton's third law).
description_plain: Your child understands that when two objects push or pull on each other, the forces are equal in strength but opposite in direction.

subject: Science
description: Constructing Explanations and Designing Solutions - Apply scientific ideas or principles to design an object, tool, process, or system.
description_plain: Your child can use scientific ideas to design a tool, process, or system to solve a problem.

subject: Science
description: Apply Newton's third law to design a solution to a problem involving the motion of two colliding objects.
description_plain: Your child will use Newton’s third law to design a solution for a problem involving two objects that collide.

subject: Science
description: Cause and Effect - Cause-and-effect relationships may be used to predict phenomena in natural or designed systems.
description_plain: Your child can use cause-and-effect ideas to guess what might happen in natural or man-made systems.

subject: Science
description: Definitions of Energy - The term "heat" as used in everyday language refers both to thermal energy (the motion of atoms or molecules within a substance) and the transfer of that thermal energy from one object to another. In science, heat is used only for this second meaning; it refers to the energy transferred due to the temperature difference between two objects. The temperature of a system is proportional to the average internal kinetic energy and potential energy per atom or molecule (whichever is the appropriate building block for the system's material). The details of that relationship depend on the type of atom or molecule and the interactions among the atoms in the material. Temperature is not a direct measure of a system's total thermal energy. The total thermal energy (sometimes called the total internal energy) of a system depends jointly on the temperature, the total number of atoms in the system, and the state of the material.
description_plain: Your child understands that 'heat' means energy moving from one object to another because of temperature differences, and that temperature shows how fast atoms or molecules move inside something.

subject: Science
description: Structure and Properties of Matter - Gases and liquids are made of molecules or inert atoms that are moving about relative to each other. In a liquid, the molecules are constantly in contact with others; in a gas, they are widely spaced except when they happen to collide. In a solid, atoms are closely spaced and may vibrate in position but do not change relative locations. The changes of state that occur with variations in temperature or pressure can be described and predicted using these models of matter.
description_plain: Your child knows that gases, liquids, and solids are made of tiny particles that move differently, and that changes in temperature or pressure can change these states.

subject: Science
description: Developing and Using Models - Develop a model to predict and/or describe phenomena.
description_plain: Your child can create a model to explain or predict how something works or happens.

subject: Science
description: Develop a model that predicts and describes changes in particle motion, temperature, and state of a pure substance when thermal energy is added or removed.
description_plain: Your child can build a model to show how particles move, temperature changes, and states of matter change when heat is added or taken away.

subject: Science
description: Scale, Proportion, and Quantity - Time, space, and energy phenomena can be observed at various scales using models to study systems that are too large or too small.
description_plain: Your child understands that things like time, space, and energy can be studied at different sizes using models when they are too big or too small to see directly.

subject: Science
description: Structure and Properties of Matter - Substances are made from different types of atoms, which combine with one another in various ways. Atoms form molecules that range in size from two to thousands of atoms. Solids may be formed from molecules, or they may be extended structures with repeating subunits (e.g., crystals).
description_plain: Your child knows that substances are made of different atoms that join together to form molecules or crystals.

subject: Science
description: Developing and Using Models - Develop a model to predict and/or describe phenomena.
description_plain: Your child can make a model to explain or predict how something happens.

subject: Science
description: Develop models to describe the atomic composition of simple molecules and extended structures.
description_plain: Your child can create models that show what atoms make up simple molecules and larger structures.

subject: Science
description: Optimizing the Design Solution - The iterative process of testing the most promising solutions and modifying what is proposed on the basis of the test results leads to greater refinement and ultimately to an optimal solution.
description_plain: Your child learns to test ideas, make changes based on results, and improve designs step by step to find the best solution.

subject: Science
description: Developing Possible Solutions - A solution needs to be tested, and then modified on the basis of the test results, in order to improve it. Models of all kinds are important for testing solutions.
description_plain: Your child understands that solutions need to be tested and changed based on test results, and that models help with testing ideas.

subject: Science
description: Developing and Using Models - Develop a model to generate data to test ideas about designed systems, including those representing inputs and outputs.
description_plain: Your child can build a model to collect data and test how a system works, including what goes in and what comes out.

subject: Science
description: Develop a model to generate data for iterative testing and modification of a proposed object, tool, or process such that an optimal design can be achieved.
description_plain: Your child can create a model to test and improve a design step-by-step until it works well.

subject: Science
description: Optimizing the Design Solution - Although one design may not perform the best across all tests, identifying the characteristics of the design that performed the best in each test can provide useful information for the redesign process—that is, some of those characteristics may be incorporated into the new design.
description_plain: Your child knows that even if one design isn’t perfect in every way, the best parts can be used to make a better new design.

subject: Science
description: Developing Possible Solutions - There are systematic processes for evaluating solutions with respect to how well they meet the criteria and constraints of a problem. Sometimes parts of different solutions can be combined to create a solution that is better than any of its predecessors.
description_plain: Your child can compare different solutions to see which meet the problem’s needs best and combine good parts from each to make a better solution.

subject: Science
description: Analyzing and Interpreting Data - Analyze and interpret data to determine similarities and differences in findings.
description_plain: Your child can look at data carefully to find what is the same and what is different in the results.

subject: Science
description: Analyze data from tests to determine similarities and differences among several design solutions to identify the best characteristics of each that can be combined into a new solution to better meet the criteria for success.
description_plain: Your child can study test results to find the best parts of different designs and combine them to make a better solution.

subject: Science
description: Developing Possible Solutions - There are systematic processes for evaluating solutions with respect to how well they meet the criteria and constraints of a problem.
description_plain: Your child understands how to check solutions step by step to see if they meet the problem’s requirements and limits.

subject: Science
description: Engaging in Argument from Evidence - Evaluate competing design solutions based on jointly developed and agreed-upon design criteria.
description_plain: Your child can compare different design ideas and decide which is better based on agreed rules.

subject: Science
description: Evaluate competing design solutions using a systematic process to determine how well they meet the criteria and constraints of the problem.
description_plain: Your child can compare different design ideas carefully to see which one best meets the problem's needs and limits.

subject: Science
description: Defining and Delimiting Engineering Problems - The more precisely a design task's criteria and constraints can be defined, the more likely it is that the designed solution will be successful. Specification of constraints includes consideration of scientific principles and other relevant knowledge that are likely to limit possible solutions.
description_plain: Your child knows that clearly stating what a design must do and what limits it has helps make a successful solution.

subject: Science
description: Asking Questions and Defining Problems - Define a design problem that can be solved through the development of an object, tool, process or system and includes multiple criteria and constraints, including scientific knowledge that may limit possible solutions.
description_plain: Your child can describe a problem that can be solved by making something new, including rules and limits based on science.

subject: Science
description: Define the criteria and constraints of a design problem with sufficient precision to ensure a successful solution, taking into account relevant scientific principles and potential impacts on people and the natural environment that may limit possible solutions.
description_plain: Your child can clearly describe the goals and limits of a design problem, considering science and how it affects people and the environment.

subject: Science
description: Cause and Effect - Cause-and-effect relationships may be used to predict phenomena in natural systems.
description_plain: Your child can use cause-and-effect ideas to predict what will happen in natural systems.

subject: Science
description: Information Processing - Each sense receptor responds to different inputs (electromagnetic, mechanical, chemical), transmitting them as signals that travel along nerve cells to the brain. The signals are then processed in the brain, resulting in immediate behaviors or memories.
description_plain: Your child understands that different senses send signals to the brain, which then causes actions or memories.

subject: Science
description: Obtaining, Evaluating, and Communicating Information - Gather, read, and synthesize information from multiple appropriate sources and assess the credibility, accuracy, and possible bias of each publication and methods used, and describe how they are supported or not supported by evidence.
description_plain: Your child can gather information from different sources, check if it is trustworthy, and explain if the evidence supports the ideas.

subject: Science
description: Gather and synthesize information that sensory receptors respond to stimuli by sending messages to the brain for immediate behavior or storage as memories.
description_plain: Your child knows that sensory receptors send messages to the brain when they detect something, leading to quick actions or memories.

subject: Science
description: Energy and Matter - Matter is conserved because atoms are conserved in physical and chemical processes.
description_plain: Your child understands that matter is not lost during physical or chemical changes because atoms stay the same.

subject: Science
description: Energy in Chemical Processes and Everyday Life - Cellular respiration in plants and animals involve chemical reactions with oxygen that release stored energy. In these processes, complex molecules containing carbon react with oxygen to produce carbon dioxide and other materials.
description_plain: Your child knows that plants and animals use oxygen in chemical reactions to release energy from food.

subject: Science
description: Organization for Matter and Energy Flow in Organisms - Within individual organisms, food moves through a series of chemical reactions in which it is broken down and rearranged to form new molecules, to support growth, or to release energy.
description_plain: Your child understands that food is broken down and changed inside living things to help them grow or get energy.

subject: Science
description: Developing and Using Models - Develop a model to describe unobservable mechanisms.
description_plain: Your child can build a model to explain things that cannot be seen directly.

subject: Science
description: Develop a model to describe how food is rearranged through chemical reactions forming new molecules that support growth and/or release energy as this matter moves through an organism.
description_plain: Your child can create a model to explain how food changes through chemical reactions to help an organism grow or get energy.

subject: Science
description: Systems and System Models - Systems may interact with other systems; they may have sub-systems and be a part of larger complex systems.
description_plain: Your child knows that systems can have smaller parts and can connect with other systems.

subject: Science
description: Structure and Function - In multicellular organisms, the body is a system of multiple interacting subsystems. These subsystems are groups of cells that work together to form tissues and organs that are specialized for particular body functions.
description_plain: Your child understands that in living things, groups of cells work together to form tissues and organs that do specific jobs.

subject: Science
description: Engaging in Argument from Evidence - Use an oral and written argument supported by evidence to support or refute an explanation or a model for a phenomenon.
description_plain: Your child can explain their ideas clearly using evidence to support or disagree with an explanation or model.

subject: Science
description: Use argument supported by evidence for how the body is a system of interacting subsystems composed of groups of cells.
description_plain: Your child can explain how the body is made of interacting parts that are groups of cells, using evidence.

subject: Science
description: Structure and Function - Complex and microscopic structures and systems can be visualized, modeled, and used to describe how their function depends on the relationships among its parts, therefore complex natural and designed structures/systems can be analyzed to determine how they function.
description_plain: Your child understands that complex structures can be studied and modeled to see how their parts work together.

subject: Science
description: Structure and Function - Within cells, special structures are responsible for particular functions, and the cell membrane forms the boundary that controls what enters and leaves the cell.
description_plain: Your child knows that cells have special parts that do specific jobs, and the cell membrane controls what goes in and out.

subject: Science
description: Developing and Using Models - Develop and use a model to describe phenomena.
description_plain: Your child can create and use a model to explain how something works.

subject: Science
description: Develop and use a model to describe the function of a cell as a whole and ways the parts of cells contribute to the function.
description_plain: Your child can build and use a model to show how a cell works and how its parts help it function.

subject: Science
description: Scale, Proportion, and Quantity - Phenomena that can be observed at one scale may not be observable at another scale.
description_plain: Your child understands that some things can be seen at one size but not at another.

subject: Science
description: Structure and Function - All living things are made up of cells, which is the smallest unit that can be said to be alive. An organism may consist of one single cell (unicellular) or many different numbers and types of cells (multicellular).
description_plain: Your child knows that all living things are made of cells, which are the smallest units of life, and organisms can have one or many cells.

subject: Science
description: Planning and Carrying Out Investigations - Conduct an investigation to produce data to serve as the basis for evidence that meet the goals of an investigation.
description_plain: Your child can carry out an investigation to collect data that helps answer a scientific question.

subject: Science
description: Conduct an investigation to provide evidence that living things are made of cells; either one cell or many different numbers and types of cells.
description_plain: Your child can do an investigation to show that living things are made of one or many cells.

subject: Science
description: Structure and Function - Structures can be designed to serve particular functions.
description_plain: Your child understands that things can be designed to do specific jobs.

subject: Science
description: Information Technologies and Instrumentation - Technologies allow us to detect and interpret waves and signals in waves that cannot be detected directly.
description_plain: Your child knows that technology helps us detect and understand waves and signals we cannot see directly.

subject: Science
description: Obtaining, Evaluating, and Communicating Information - Integrate qualitative scientific and technical information in written text with that contained in media and visual displays to clarify claims and findings.
description_plain: Your child can combine written, visual, and media information to explain scientific ideas clearly.

subject: Science
description: Integrate qualitative scientific and technical information to support the claim that designed technologies can transmit digital information as wave pulses.
description_plain: Your child can use scientific information to show that technologies send digital information using wave pulses.

subject: Science
description: Structure and Function - Structures can be designed to serve particular functions by taking into account properties of different materials, and how materials can be shaped and used.
description_plain: Your child understands that things can be designed to work well by choosing the right materials and shapes.

subject: Science
description: Electromagnetic Radiation - When light shines on an object, it is reflected, absorbed, or transmitted through the object, depending on the object's material and the frequency (color) of the light. The path that light travels can be traced as straight lines, except at surfaces between different transparent materials (e.g., air and water, air and glass) where the light path bends. A wave model of light is useful for explaining brightness, color, and the frequency-dependent bending of light at a surface between media. However, because light can travel through space, it cannot be a matter wave, like sound or water waves.
description_plain: Your child knows that when light hits an object, it can bounce off, be absorbed, or pass through, and that light bends when it moves between different materials.

subject: Science
description: Wave Properties - A sound wave needs a medium through which it is transmitted.
description_plain: Your child understands that sound needs something to travel through, like air or water.

subject: Science
description: Developing and Using Models - Develop and use a model to describe phenomena.
description_plain: Your child can create and use a model to explain how something happens.

subject: Science
description: Develop and use a model to describe that waves are reflected, absorbed, or transmitted through various materials.
description_plain: Your child can build and use a model to show how waves can bounce off, be absorbed by, or pass through different materials.

subject: Science
description: Patterns - Graphs and charts can be used to identify patterns in data.
description_plain: Your child knows that graphs and charts help find patterns in data.

subject: Science
description: Wave Properties - A simple wave has a repeating pattern with a specific wavelength, frequency, and amplitude.
description_plain: Your child understands that simple waves repeat in patterns with specific length, speed, and height.

subject: Science
description: Using Mathematics and Computational Thinking - Use mathematical representations to describe and/or support scientific conclusions and design solutions.
description_plain: Your child can use math to explain scientific ideas and help solve problems.

subject: Science
description: Use mathematical representations to describe a simple model for waves that includes how the amplitude of a wave is related to the energy in a wave.
description_plain: Your child understands how to use math to show how wave size relates to the energy in the wave.

subject: Science
description: Energy and Matter - Energy may take different forms (e.g., energy in fields, thermal energy, energy of motion).
description_plain: Your child knows that energy can exist in different forms like heat, motion, or fields.

subject: Science
description: Conservation of Energy and Energy Transfer - When the motion energy of an object changes, there is inevitably some other change in energy at the same time.
description_plain: Your child understands that when an object's motion energy changes, energy changes in other ways too.

subject: Science
description: Engaging in Argument from Evidence - Construct, use, and present oral and written arguments supported by empirical evidence and scientific reasoning to support or refute an explanation or a model for a phenomenon.
description_plain: Your child can make and share arguments using evidence and science to support or challenge explanations.

subject: Science
description: Construct, use, and present arguments to support the claim that when the kinetic energy of an object changes, energy is transferred to or from the object.Engaging in Argument from Evidence
description_plain: Your child can explain that when an object's kinetic energy changes, energy moves to or from that object.

subject: Science
description: Scale, Proportion, and Quantity - Proportional relationships (e.g., speed as the ratio of distance traveled to time taken) among different types of quantities provide information about the magnitude of properties and processes.
description_plain: Your child understands that comparing amounts, like speed as distance over time, helps explain how things work.

subject: Science
description: Conservation of Energy and Energy Transfer - The amount of energy transfer needed to change the temperature of a matter sample by a given amount depends on the nature of the matter, the size of the sample, and the environment.
description_plain: Your child knows that how much energy is needed to change temperature depends on the material, its size, and surroundings.

subject: Science
description: Definitions of Energy - Temperature is a measure of the average kinetic energy of particles of matter. The relationship between the temperature and the total energy of a system depends on the types, states, and amounts of matter present.
description_plain: Your child understands that temperature measures how fast particles move and that total energy depends on the kind and amount of matter.

subject: Science
description: Planning and Carrying Out Investigations - Plan an investigation individually and collaboratively, and in the design: identify independent and dependent variables and controls, what tools are needed to do the gathering, how measurements will be recorded, and how many data are needed to support a claim.
description_plain: Your child can plan experiments by choosing what to change, what to measure, what to keep the same, what tools to use, and how much data to collect.

subject: Science
description: Plan an investigation to determine the relationships among the energy transferred, the type of matter, the mass, and the change in the average kinetic energy of the particles as measured by the temperature of the sample.
description_plain: Your child can plan an experiment to find out how energy transfer relates to the type and amount of matter and temperature changes.

subject: Science
description: Energy and Matter - The transfer of energy can be tracked as energy flows through a designed or natural system.
description_plain: Your child understands that energy moves through systems and can be followed as it flows.

subject: Science
description: Developing Possible Solutions - A solution needs to be tested, and then modified on the basis of the test results in order to improve it. There are systematic processes for evaluating solutions with respect to how well they meet criteria and constraints of a problem.
description_plain: Your child knows that solutions should be tested and improved based on test results using clear steps.

subject: Science
description: Defining and Delimiting an Engineering Problem - The more precisely a design task's critria and constraints can be defined, the more likely it is that the designed solution will be successful. Specification of constraints includes consideration of scientific principles and other relevant knowledge that is likely to limit possible solutions.
description_plain: Your child understands that clearly defining what a design must do and its limits helps create better solutions.

subject: Science
description: Conservation of Energy and Energy Transfer - Energy is spontaneously transferred out of hotter regions or objects and into colder ones.
description_plain: Your child knows that energy naturally moves from hotter places or objects to colder ones.

subject: Science
description: Definitions of Energy - Temperature is a measure of the average kinetic energy of particles of matter. The relationship between the temperature and the total energy of a system depends on the types, states, and amounts of matter present.
description_plain: Your child understands that temperature shows how fast particles move and that total energy depends on the kind and amount of matter.

subject: Science
description: Constructing Explanations and Designing Solutions - Apply scientific ideas or principles to design, construct, and test a design of an object, tool, process or system.
description_plain: Your child can use scientific ideas to design, build, and test objects or systems.

subject: Science
description: Apply scientific principles to design, construct, and test a device that either minimizes or maximizes thermal energy transfer.
description_plain: Your child can design and test a device that either reduces or increases heat transfer.

subject: Science
description: Systems and System Models - Models can be used to represent systems and their interactions – such as inputs, processes, and outputs – and energy and matter flows within systems.
