description_plain: Your child understands that models can show how systems work, including how energy and matter move through them.

subject: Science
description: Relationship Between Energy and Forces - When two objects interact, each one exerts a force on the other that can cause energy to be transferred to or from the object.
description_plain: Your child knows that when two objects push or pull on each other, energy can move between them.

subject: Science
description: Definitions of Energy - A system of objects may also contain stored (potential) energy, depending on their relative positions.
description_plain: Your child understands that objects can store energy based on their positions.

subject: Science
description: Developing and Using Models - Develop a model to describe unobservable mechanisms.
description_plain: Your child can create models to explain things that can't be seen directly.

subject: Science
description: Develop a model to describe that when the arrangement of objects interacting at a distance changes, different amounts of potential energy are stored in the system.
description_plain: Your child can make a model to show how changing the positions of objects affects the energy stored between them.

subject: Science
description: Scale, Proportion, and Quantity - Proportional relationships (e.g., speed as the ratio of distance traveled to time taken) among different types of quantities provide information about the magnitude of properties and processes.
description_plain: Your child understands that comparing amounts, like speed as distance over time, helps explain how things work.

subject: Science
description: Definitions of Energy - Motion energy is properly called kinetic energy; it is proportional to the mass of the moving object and grows with the square of its speed.
description_plain: Your child knows that motion energy (kinetic energy) depends on how heavy an object is and how fast it moves.

subject: Science
description: Analyzing and Interpreting Data - Construct and interpret graphical displays of data to identify linear and nonlinear relationships.
description_plain: Your child can make and read graphs to find patterns in data, including straight and curved relationships.

subject: Science
description: Construct and interpret graphical displays of data to describe the relationships of kinetic energy to the mass of an object and to the speed of an object.
description_plain: Your child can create and understand graphs that show how kinetic energy relates to an object's mass and speed.

subject: Science
description: Cause and Effect - Cause-and-effect relationships may be used to predict phenomena in natural or designed systems.
description_plain: Your child understands that cause-and-effect helps predict what will happen in natural or designed systems.

subject: Science
description: Types of Interactions - Forces that act at a distance (electric, magnetic, and gravitational) can be explained by fields that extend through space and can be mapped by their effect on a test object (a charged object, or a ball, respectively).
description_plain: Your child knows that forces like electric, magnetic, and gravity act through space and can be mapped by their effects on objects.

subject: Science
description: Planning and Carrying Out Investigations - Conduct an investigation and evaluate the experimental design to produce data to serve as the basis for evidence that can meet the goals of the investigation.
description_plain: Your child can carry out experiments and check if the design works well to collect data that answers questions.

subject: Science
description: Conduct an investigation and evaluate the experimental design to provide evidence that fields exist between objects exerting forces on each other even though the objects are not in contact.
description_plain: Your child can do an experiment to show that forces act between objects even when they don't touch.

subject: Science
description: Cause and Effect - Cause-and-effect relationships may be used to predict phenomena in natural or designed systems.
description_plain: Your child understands that cause-and-effect helps predict what will happen in natural or designed systems.

subject: Science
description: Types of Interactions - Electric and magnetic (electromagnetic) forces can be attractive or repulsive, and their sizes depend on the magnitudes of the charges, currents, or magnetic strengths involved and on the distances between the interacting objects.
description_plain: Your child knows that electric and magnetic forces can pull together or push apart, and their strength depends on charge, current, magnetism, and distance.

subject: Science
description: Asking Questions and Defining Problems - Ask questions that can be investigated within the scope of the classroom, outdoor environment, and museums and other public facilities with available resources and, when appropriate, frame a hypothesis based on observations and scientific principles.
description_plain: Your child can ask questions that can be tested using resources like the classroom or outdoors and make guesses based on observations.

subject: Science
description: Ask questions about data to determine the factors that affect the strength of electric and magnetic forces.
description_plain: Your child can ask questions about what affects how strong electric and magnetic forces are.

subject: Science
description: Stability and Change - Explanations of stability and change in natural or designed systems can be constructed by examining the changes over time and forces at different scales.
description_plain: Your child understands that looking at changes over time and forces at different sizes helps explain why things stay the same or change.

subject: Science
description: Forces and Motion - The motion of an object is determined by the sum of the forces acting on it; if the total force on the object is not zero, its motion will change. The greater the mass of the object, the greater the force needed to achieve the same change in motion. For any given object, a larger force causes a larger change in motion. All positions of objects and the directions of forces and motions must be described in an arbitrarily chosen units of size. In order to share information with other people, these choices must also be shared.
description_plain: Your child knows that an object's motion changes when forces push or pull on it, and heavier objects need more force to change motion; also, bigger forces cause bigger changes.

subject: Science
description: Planning and Carrying Out Investigations - Plan an investigation individually and collaboratively, and in the design: identify independent and dependent variables and controls, what tools are needed to do the gathering, how measurements will be recorded, and how many data are needed to support a claim.
description_plain: Your child can plan experiments by deciding what to change, what to measure, what to keep the same, what tools to use, and how much data to collect.

subject: Science
description: Plan an investigation to provide evidence that the change in an object's motion depends on the sum of the forces on the object and the mass of the object.
description_plain: Your child can plan an experiment to show that how an object's motion changes depends on the total forces on it and its mass.

subject: Science
description: Energy and Matter - The transfer of energy can be tracked as energy flows through a designed or natural system.
description_plain: Your child understands that energy moves through systems and can be followed as it flows.

subject: Science
description: Optimizing the Design Solution - Although one design may not perform the best across all tests, identifying the characteristics of the design that performed the best in each test can provide useful information for the redesign process—that is, some of the characteristics may be incorporated into the new design. The iterative process of testing the most promising solutions and modifying what is proposed on the basis of the test results leads to greater refinement and ultimately to an optimal solution.
description_plain: Your child can find the best parts of different designs from tests and use them to improve a new design by testing and changing it again and again.

subject: Science
description: Developing Possible Solutions - A solution needs to be tested, and then modified on the basis of the test results, in order to improve it.
description_plain: Your child will test a solution and then change it based on the test results to make it better.

subject: Science
description: Chemical Reactions - Some chemical reactions release energy, others store energy.
description_plain: Your child understands that some chemical reactions give off energy while others store energy.

subject: Science
description: Constructing Explanations and Designing Solutions - Undertake a design project, engaging in the design cycle, to construct and/or implement a solution that meets specific design criteria and constraints.
description_plain: Your child can work through the steps of designing and building something that meets specific goals and limits.

subject: Science
description: Undertake a design project to construct, test, and modify a device that either releases or absorbs thermal energy.*
description_plain: Your child can design, build, test, and improve a device that uses chemical reactions to give off or take in heat.

subject: Science
description: Energy and Matter - Matter is conserved because atoms are conserved in physical and chemical processes.
description_plain: Your child understands that matter doesn’t disappear because atoms stay the same during physical and chemical changes.

subject: Science
description: Chemical Reactions - Substances react chemically in characteristic ways. In a chemical process, the atoms that make up the original substances are regrouped into different molecules, and these new substances have different properties from those of the reactants. The total number of each type of atom is conserved, and thus the mass does not change.
description_plain: Your child knows that in chemical reactions, atoms rearrange to make new substances with different properties, but the total number of atoms and mass stay the same.

subject: Science
description: Developing and Using Models - Develop a model to describe unobservable mechanisms.
description_plain: Your child can create a model to explain things that can’t be seen directly.

subject: Science
description: Develop and use a model to describe how the total number of atoms does not change in a chemical reaction and thus mass is conserved.
description_plain: Your child can use a model to show that the total number of atoms stays the same during a chemical reaction, so mass doesn’t change.

subject: Science
description: Patterns - Macroscopic patterns are related to the nature of microscopic and atomic-level structure.
description_plain: Your child understands that patterns we see in materials come from how tiny particles and atoms are arranged.

subject: Science
description: Chemical Reactions - Substances react chemically in characteristic ways. In a chemical process, the atoms that make up the original substances are regrouped into different molecules, and these new substances have different properties from those of the reactants.
description_plain: Your child knows that substances react in specific ways, and during these reactions, atoms rearrange into new molecules with different properties.

subject: Science
description: Structure and Properties of Matter - Each pure substance has characteristic physical and chemical properties (for any bulk quantity under given conditions) that can be used to identify it.
description_plain: Your child understands that each pure substance has unique physical and chemical traits that help identify it.

subject: Science
description: Analyzing and Interpreting Data - Analyze and interpret data to determine similarities and differences in findings.
description_plain: Your child can look at data and figure out what is similar and what is different in the results.

subject: Science
description: Analyze and interpret data on the properties of substances before and after the substances interact to determine if a chemical reaction has occurred.
description_plain: Your child can look at data about substances before and after they react to decide if a chemical reaction happened.

subject: Science
description: Optimizing the Design Solution - The iterative process of testing the most promising solutions and modifying what is proposed on the basis of the test results leads to greater refinement and ultimately to an optimal solution.
description_plain: Your child learns to test ideas, make changes based on results, and improve designs step by step to find the best solution.

subject: Science
description: Developing Possible Solutions - A solution needs to be tested, and then modified on the basis of the test results, in order to improve it. Models of all kinds are important for testing solutions.
description_plain: Your child understands that solutions need to be tested and changed based on test results, and that models help with testing ideas.

subject: Science
description: Developing and Using Models - Develop a model to generate data to test ideas about designed systems, including those representing inputs and outputs.
description_plain: Your child can build a model to collect data and test how a system works, including what goes in and what comes out.

subject: Science
description: Develop a model to generate data for iterative testing and modification of a proposed object, tool, or process such that an optimal design can be achieved.
description_plain: Your child can create a model to test and improve a design step-by-step until it works well.

subject: Science
description: Optimizing the Design Solution - Although one design may not perform the best across all tests, identifying the characteristics of the design that performed the best in each test can provide useful information for the redesign process—that is, some of those characteristics may be incorporated into the new design.
description_plain: Your child knows that even if one design isn’t perfect in every way, the best parts can be used to make a better new design.

subject: Science
description: Developing Possible Solutions - There are systematic processes for evaluating solutions with respect to how well they meet the criteria and constraints of a problem. Sometimes parts of different solutions can be combined to create a solution that is better than any of its predecessors.
description_plain: Your child can compare different solutions to see which meet the problem’s needs best and combine good parts from each to make a better solution.

subject: Science
description: Analyzing and Interpreting Data - Analyze and interpret data to determine similarities and differences in findings.
description_plain: Your child can look at data carefully to find what is the same and what is different in the results.

subject: Science
description: Analyze data from tests to determine similarities and differences among several design solutions to identify the best characteristics of each that can be combined into a new solution to better meet the criteria for success.
description_plain: Your child can study test results to find the best parts of different designs and combine them to make a better solution.

subject: Science
description: Developing Possible Solutions - There are systematic processes for evaluating solutions with respect to how well they meet the criteria and constraints of a problem.
description_plain: Your child understands how to check solutions step by step to see if they meet the problem’s requirements and limits.

subject: Science
description: Engaging in Argument from Evidence - Evaluate competing design solutions based on jointly developed and agreed-upon design criteria.
description_plain: Your child can compare different design ideas and decide which is better based on agreed rules.

subject: Science
description: Evaluate competing design solutions using a systematic process to determine how well they meet the criteria and constraints of the problem.
description_plain: Your child can compare different design ideas carefully to see which one best meets the problem's needs and limits.

subject: Science
description: Defining and Delimiting Engineering Problems - The more precisely a design task's criteria and constraints can be defined, the more likely it is that the designed solution will be successful. Specification of constraints includes consideration of scientific principles and other relevant knowledge that are likely to limit possible solutions.
description_plain: Your child knows that clearly stating what a design must do and what limits it has helps make a successful solution.

subject: Science
description: Asking Questions and Defining Problems - Define a design problem that can be solved through the development of an object, tool, process or system and includes multiple criteria and constraints, including scientific knowledge that may limit possible solutions.
description_plain: Your child can describe a problem that can be solved by making something new, including rules and limits based on science.

subject: Science
description: Define the criteria and constraints of a design problem with sufficient precision to ensure a successful solution, taking into account relevant scientific principles and potential impacts on people and the natural environment that may limit possible solutions.
description_plain: Your child can clearly describe the goals and limits of a design problem, considering science and how it affects people and the environment.

subject: Science
description: Stability and Change - Stability might be disturbed either by sudden events or gradual changes that accumulate over time.
description_plain: Your child understands that things can stay the same until something sudden or slow changes them over time.

subject: Science
description: Global Climate Change - Human activities, such as the release of greenhouse gases from burning fossil fuels, are major factors in the current rise in Earth's mean surface temperature (global warming). Reducing the level of climate change and reducing human vulnerability to whatever climate changes do occur depend on the understanding of climate science, engineering capabilities, and other kinds of knowledge, such as understanding of human behavior and on applying that knowledge wisely in decisions and activities.
description_plain: Your child knows that human activities like burning fossil fuels cause Earth to warm, and that understanding climate science and human behavior helps us make better choices to reduce harm.

subject: Science
description: Asking Questions and Defining Problems - Ask questions to identify and clarify evidence of an argument.
description_plain: Your child can ask questions to better understand the evidence behind an argument.

subject: Science
description: Ask questions to clarify evidence of the factors that have caused the rise in global temperatures over the past century.
description_plain: Your child can ask questions to understand what caused Earth's temperatures to rise over the last 100 years.

subject: Science
description: Cause and Effect - Cause-and-effect relationships may be used to predict phenomena in natural or designed systems.
description_plain: Your child understands that cause-and-effect relationships help predict what will happen in natural or human-made systems.

subject: Science
description: Human Impacts on Earth Systems - Typically, as human populations and per-capita consumption of natural resources increase, so do the negative impacts on Earth unless the activities and technologies involved are engineered otherwise.
description_plain: Your child knows that as people and their use of resources grow, negative effects on Earth usually increase unless we use better technology and methods.

subject: Science
description: Engaging in Argument from Evidence - Construct an oral and written argument supported by empirical evidence and scientific reasoning to support or refute an explanation or a model for a phenomenon or a solution to a problem.
description_plain: Your child can make a spoken or written argument using evidence and scientific thinking to support or oppose an explanation or solution.

subject: Science
description: Construct an argument supported by evidence for how increases in human population and per-capita consumption of natural resources impact Earth's systems.
description_plain: Your child can explain with evidence how more people and higher resource use affect Earth's systems.

subject: Science
description: Cause and Effect - Relationships can be classified as causal or correlational, and correlation does not necessarily imply causation.
description_plain: Your child understands that some relationships show cause and effect, while others just show things happen together but don’t cause each other.

subject: Science
description: Human Impacts on Earth Systems - Human activities have significantly altered the biosphere, sometimes damaging or destroying natural habitats and causing the extinction of other species. But changes to Earth's environments can have different impacts (negative and positive) for different living things. Typically, as human populations and per-capita consumption of natural resources increase, so do the negative impacts on Earth unless the activities and technologies involved are engineered otherwise.
description_plain: Your child knows that humans have changed habitats and caused some species to disappear, and that these changes can affect living things in good and bad ways.

subject: Science
description: Constructing Explanations and Designing Solutions - Apply scientific principles to design an object, tool, process or system.
description_plain: Your child can use science ideas to design a tool, process, or system.

subject: Science
description: Apply scientific principles to design a method for monitoring and minimizing a human impact on the environment.
description_plain: Your child can design a way to watch and reduce human harm to the environment using science principles.

subject: Science
description: Patterns - Graphs, charts, and images can be used to identify patterns in data.
description_plain: Your child can use graphs, charts, and pictures to find patterns in data.

subject: Science
description: Natural Hazards - Mapping the history of natural hazards in a region, combined with an understanding of related geologic forces can help forecast the locations and likelihoods of future events.
description_plain: Your child understands that studying past natural disasters and Earth’s forces helps predict where and when future events might happen.

subject: Science
description: Analyzing and Interpreting Data - Analyze and interpret data to determine similarities and differences in findings.
description_plain: Your child can look at data to find what is alike and different in results.

subject: Science
description: Analyze and interpret data to forecast future catastrophic events to inform the development of technologies to mitigate the effects of natural hazards.
description_plain: Your child can use data to predict future natural disasters and help create ways to lessen their damage.

subject: Science
description: Cause and Effect - Cause-and-effect relationships may be used to predict phenomena in natural or designed systems.
description_plain: Your child understands that cause-and-effect helps predict what will happen in natural or designed systems.

subject: Science
description: Natural Resources - Humans depend on Earth's land, ocean, atmosphere, and biosphere for many different resources. Minerals, fresh water, and biosphere resources are limited, and many are not renewable or replaceable over human lifetimes. These resources are distributed unevenly around the planet as a result of past geologic processes.
description_plain: Your child knows that people rely on Earth's land, water, air, and living things for resources, many of which are limited and unevenly spread because of Earth's history.

subject: Science
description: Constructing Explanations and Designing Solutions - Construct a scientific explanation based on valid and reliable evidence obtained from sources (including the students' own experiments) and the assumption that theories and laws that describe the natural world operate today as they did in the past and will continue to do so in the future.
description_plain: Your child can explain scientific ideas using trustworthy evidence from experiments and reliable sources.

subject: Science
description: Construct a scientific explanation based on evidence for how the uneven distributions of Earth's mineral, energy, and groundwater resources are the result of past and current geoscience processes.
description_plain: Your child can explain how Earth's mineral, energy, and water resources are unevenly spread because of past and present Earth processes.

subject: Science
description: Scale Proportion and Quantity - Time, space, and energy phenomena can be observed at various scales using models to study systems that are too large or too small.
description_plain: Your child understands that we can study big or tiny things by using models to see how time, space, and energy work.

subject: Science
description: The History of Planet Earth - The geologic time scale interpreted from rock strata provides a way to organize Earth's history. Analyses of rock strata and the fossil record provide only relative dates, not an absolute scale.
description_plain: Your child knows that scientists use rock layers and fossils to organize Earth's history by relative time, not exact dates.

subject: Science
description: Constructing Explanations and Designing Solutions - Construct a scientific explanation based on valid and reliable evidence obtained from sources (including the students' own experiments) and the assumption that theories and laws that describe the natural world operate today as they did in the past and will continue to do so in the future.
description_plain: Your child can explain scientific ideas using solid evidence from experiments and reliable sources, knowing that natural laws work the same now as in the past.

subject: Science
description: Construct a scientific explanation based on evidence from rock strata for how the geologic time scale is used to organize Earth's 4.6-billion-year-old history.
description_plain: Your child can explain how scientists use layers of rock to organize Earth’s 4.6-billion-year history.

subject: Science
description: Cause and Effect - Phenomena may have more than one cause, and some cause-and-effect relationships in systems can only be described using probability.
description_plain: Your child understands that events can have more than one cause and that some cause-and-effect relationships are based on chance.

subject: Science
description: Adaptation - Adaptation by natural selection acting over generations is one important process by which species change over time in response to changes in environmental conditions. Traits that support successful survival and reproduction in the new environment become more common; those that do not become less common. Thus, the distribution of traits in a population changes.
description_plain: Your child knows that species change over time because traits that help survival become more common through natural selection.

subject: Science
description: Using Mathematics and Computational Thinking - Use mathematical representations to support scientific conclusions and design solutions.
description_plain: Your child can use math to help explain scientific ideas and design solutions.

subject: Science
description: Use mathematical representations to support explanations of how natural selection may lead to increases and decreases of specific traits in populations over time.
description_plain: Your child can use math to explain how natural selection can cause certain traits in a group of animals or plants to increase or decrease over time.

subject: Science
description: Cause and Effect - Phenomena may have more than one cause, and some cause-and-effect relationships in systems can only be described using probability.
description_plain: Your child understands that events can have multiple causes and some cause-and-effect relationships involve probability.

subject: Science
description: Natural Selection - In artificial selection, humans have the capacity to influence certain characteristics of organisms by selective breeding. One can choose desired parental traits determined by genes, which are then passed on to offspring.
description_plain: Your child knows that humans can change traits in plants and animals by choosing which ones breed.

subject: Science
description: Obtaining, Evaluating, and Communicating Information - Gather, read, and synthesize information from multiple appropriate sources and assess the credibility, accuracy, and possible bias of each publication and methods used, and describe how they are supported or not supported by evidence.
description_plain: Your child can gather and understand information from different sources, check if it is trustworthy, and explain how the evidence supports or doesn’t support ideas.

subject: Science
description: Gather and synthesize information about technologies that have changed the way humans influence the inheritance of desired traits in organisms.
description_plain: Your child can find and combine information about technologies that help people change traits in living things.

subject: Science
description: Cause and Effect - Phenomena may have more than one cause, and some cause-and-effect relationships in systems can only be described using probability.
description_plain: Your child understands that events can have more than one cause and that some cause-and-effect relationships happen by chance.

subject: Science
description: Natural Selection - Natural selection leads to the predominance of certain traits in a population, and the suppression of others.
description_plain: Your child understands that natural selection causes some traits to become more common in a group while others become less common.

subject: Science
description: Constructing Explanations and Designing Solutions - Construct an explanation that includes qualitative or quantitative relationships between variables that describe phenomena.
description_plain: Your child can explain how different factors relate to each other to describe how something happens.

subject: Science
description: Construct an explanation based on evidence that describes how genetic variations of traits in a population increase some individuals' probability of surviving and reproducing in a specific environment.
description_plain: Your child can explain how differences in traits help some individuals survive and have babies better in their environment.

subject: Science
description: Patterns - Graphs, charts, and images can be used to identify patterns in data.
description_plain: Your child can use graphs, charts, and pictures to find patterns in information.

subject: Science
description: Evidence of Common Ancestry and Diversity - Comparison of the embryological development of different species also reveals similarities that show relationships not evident in the fully-formed anatomy.
description_plain: Your child understands that comparing how embryos develop in different species shows hidden relationships between them.

subject: Science
description: Analyzing and Interpreting Data - Analyze displays of data to identify linear and nonlinear relationships.
description_plain: Your child can study data displays to find straight-line or curved relationships between things.

subject: Science
description: Analyze data to compare patterns in the embryological development across multiple species to identify relationships not evident in the fully formed adult anatomy.
description_plain: Your child can look at data about embryo development in different species to find connections not obvious in adults.

subject: Science
description: Patterns - Patterns can be used to identify cause-and-effect relationships.
description_plain: Your child understands that patterns can help find cause-and-effect relationships.

subject: Science
description: Evidence of Common Ancestry and Diversity - Anatomical similarities and differences between various organisms living today and between them and organisms in the fossil record, enable the reconstruction of evolutionary history and the inference of lines of evolutionary descent.
description_plain: Your child understands that similarities and differences in body parts of living and fossil organisms help us learn about their evolutionary history.

subject: Science
description: Constructing Explanations and Designing Solutions - Apply scientific ideas to construct an explanation for real-world phenomena, examples, or events.
description_plain: Your child can use scientific ideas to explain real-world events or examples.

subject: Science
description: Apply scientific ideas to construct an explanation for the anatomical similarities and differences among modern organisms and between modern and fossil organisms to infer evolutionary relationships.
description_plain: Your child can explain how similarities and differences in body parts of living and fossil organisms show their family relationships.

subject: Science
description: Patterns - Graphs, charts, and images can be used to identify patterns in data.
description_plain: Your child can use graphs, charts, and images to find patterns in data.

subject: Science
description: Evidence of Common Ancestry and Diversity - The collection of fossils and their placement in chronological order (e.g., through the location of the sedimentary layers in which they are found or through radioactive dating) is known as the fossil record. It documents the existence, diversity, extinction, and change of many life forms throughout the history of life on Earth.
description_plain: Your child understands that fossils arranged by age show how life on Earth has changed over time.

subject: Science
description: Analyzing and Interpreting Data - Analyze and interpret data to determine similarities and differences in findings.
description_plain: Your child can study and explain data to find what is similar and what is different.

subject: Science
description: Analyze and interpret data for patterns in the fossil record that document the existence, diversity, extinction, and change of life forms throughout the history of life on Earth under the assumption that natural laws operate today as in the past.
description_plain: Your child can study fossil data to understand how life on Earth has changed, including new species, extinction, and diversity.

subject: Science
description: Cause and Effect - Cause-and-effect relationships may be used to predict phenomena in natural systems.
description_plain: Your child understands that knowing cause-and-effect helps predict what will happen in natural systems.

subject: Science
description: Variation of Traits - In sexually reproducing organisms, each parent contributes half of the genes acquired (at random) by the offspring. Individuals have two of each chromosome and hence two alleles of each gene, one acquired from each parent. These versions may be identical or may differ from each other.
description_plain: Your child understands that in sexual reproduction, each parent gives half the genes, and offspring get two versions of each gene that may be the same or different.

subject: Science
description: Inheritance of Traits - Variations of inherited traits between parent and offspring arise from genetic differences that result from the subset of chromosomes (and therefore genes) inherited.
description_plain: Your child understands that differences in traits between parents and children come from the specific genes they inherit.

subject: Science
description: Growth and Development of Organisms - Organisms reproduce, either sexually or asexually, and transfer their genetic information to their offspring.
description_plain: Your child understands that living things reproduce and pass their genetic information to their offspring.

subject: Science
description: Developing and Using Models - Develop and use a model to describe phenomena.
description_plain: Your child can create and use models to explain how things happen.

subject: Science
description: Develop and use a model to describe why asexual reproduction results in offspring with identical genetic information and sexual reproduction results in offspring with genetic variation.
description_plain: Your child can make and use a model to show why asexual reproduction makes identical offspring and sexual reproduction makes varied offspring.

subject: Science
description: Structure and Function - Complex and microscopic structures and systems can be visualized, modeled, and used to describe how their function depends on the shapes, composition, and relationships among its parts, therefore complex natural structures/systems can be analyzed to determine how they function.
description_plain: Your child understands that complex structures can be studied and modeled to explain how their parts work together.

subject: Science
description: Variation of Traits - In addition to variations that arise from sexual reproduction, genetic information can be altered because of mutations. Though rare, mutations may result in changes to the structure and function of proteins. Some changes are beneficial, others harmful, and some neutral to the organism.
description_plain: Your child understands that genetic mutations can change proteins, which may help, harm, or not affect an organism.

subject: Science
description: Inheritance of Traits - Genes are located in the chromosomes of cells, with each chromosome pair containing two variants of each of many distinct genes. Each distinct gene chiefly controls the production of specific proteins, which in turn affects the traits of the individual. Changes (mutations) to genes can result in changes to proteins, which can affect the structures and functions of the organism and thereby change traits.
description_plain: Your child understands that genes control traits by making proteins, and changes in genes can change traits.

subject: Science
description: Developing and Using Models - Develop and use a model to describe phenomena.
description_plain: Your child can create and use models to explain how things happen.

subject: Science
description: Develop and use a model to describe why structural changes to genes (mutations) located on chromosomes may affect proteins and may result in harmful, beneficial, or neutral effects to the structure and function of the organism.
description_plain: Your child can use a model to show how changes in genes (mutations) can affect proteins and cause good, bad, or no changes in an organism.

subject: Science
description: Stability and Change - Small changes in one part of a system might cause large changes in another part.
description_plain: Your child understands that small changes in one part of a system can cause big changes in another part.

subject: Science
description: Developing Possible Solutions - There are systematic processes for evaluating solutions with respect to how well they meet the criteria and constraints of a problem.
description_plain: Your child understands there are step-by-step ways to check how well solutions meet a problem's needs and limits.

subject: Science
description: Biodiversity and Humans - Changes in biodiversity can influence humans' resources, such as food, energy, and medicines, as well as ecosystem services that humans rely on—for example, water purification and recycling.
description_plain: Your child understands that changes in biodiversity affect resources like food, energy, medicine, and important services like clean water.

subject: Science
description: Ecosystem Dynamics, Functioning, and Resilience - Biodiversity describes the variety of species found in Earth's terrestrial and oceanic ecosystems. The completeness or integrity of an ecosystem's biodiversity is often used as a measure of its health.
description_plain: Your child understands that biodiversity means the variety of species in ecosystems, and a healthy ecosystem has many different species.

subject: Science
description: Engaging in Argument from Evidence - Evaluate competing design solutions based on jointly developed and agreed-upon design criteria.
description_plain: Your child can compare different design ideas and decide which one works best based on agreed rules.

subject: Science
description: Evaluate competing design solutions for maintaining biodiversity and ecosystem services.
description_plain: Your child can compare different solutions for protecting biodiversity and ecosystem services.

subject: Science
description: Stability and Change - Small changes in one part of a system might cause large changes in another part.
description_plain: Your child understands that small changes in one part of a system can cause big changes in another part.

subject: Science
description: Social Interactions and Group Behavior - Groups often dissolve if they no longer function to meet individuals' needs, if dominant members lose their place, or if other key members are removed from the group through death, predation, or exclusion by other members.
description_plain: Your child understands that animal groups may break apart if they no longer meet members' needs or if important members are lost.

subject: Science
description: Ecosystem Dynamics, Functioning, and Resilience - Ecosystems are dynamic in nature; their characteristics can vary over time. Disruptions to any physical or biological component of an ecosystem can lead to shifts in all its populations
description_plain: Your child understands that ecosystems change over time and that disturbances can affect all living things in them.

subject: Science
description: Engaging in Argument from Evidence - Construct an oral and written argument supported by empirical evidence and scientific reasoning to support or refute an explanation or a model for a phenomenon or a solution to a problem.
description_plain: Your child can make spoken and written arguments using evidence to support or disagree with explanations or solutions.

subject: Science
description: Construct an argument supported by empirical evidence that changes to physical or biological components of an ecosystem affect populations.
description_plain: Your child can explain with evidence how changes in parts of an ecosystem affect the populations living there.

subject: Science
description: Cause and Effect - Phenomena may have more than one cause, and some cause-and-effect relationships in systems can only be described using probability.
description_plain: Your child understands that events can have more than one cause and that some cause-and-effect relationships happen by chance.

subject: Science
description: Growth and Development of Organisms - Genetic factors as well as local conditions affect the growth of the adult plant.
description_plain: Your child understands that both genes and local conditions affect how plants grow.

subject: Science
description: Constructing Explanations and Designing Solutions - Construct a scientific explanation based on valid and reliable evidence obtained from sources (including the students' own experiments) and the assumption that theories and laws that describe the natural world operate today as they did in the past and will continue to do so in the future.
description_plain: Your child can explain scientific ideas using trustworthy evidence and understand that natural laws work the same now as in the past and future.

subject: Science
description: Construct a scientific explanation based on evidence for how environmental and genetic factors influence the growth of organisms.
description_plain: Your child can explain how both environment and genes affect how living things grow.

subject: Science
description: Cause and Effect - Phenomena may have more than one cause, and some cause-and-effect relationships in systems can only be described using probability.
description_plain: Your child understands that events can have more than one cause and that some cause-and-effect relationships happen by chance.

subject: Science
description: Growth and Development of Organisms - Animals engage in characteristic behaviors that increase the odds of reproduction. Plants reproduce in a variety of ways, sometimes depending on animal behavior and specialized features for reproduction.
description_plain: Your child understands that animals behave in ways that help them reproduce, and plants reproduce in different ways, sometimes with help from animals or special parts.

subject: Science
description: Engaging in Argument from Evidence - Use an oral and written argument supported by empirical evidence and scientific reasoning to support or refute an explanation or a model for a phenomenon or a solution to a problem.
description_plain: Your child can explain their ideas clearly using evidence and scientific thinking to support or challenge explanations or solutions.

subject: Science
description: Use argument based on empirical evidence and scientific reasoning to support an explanation for how characteristic animal behaviors and specialized plant structures affect the probability of successful reproduction of animals and plants respectively.
description_plain: Your child can use evidence to explain how certain animal behaviors and special plant parts help animals and plants reproduce successfully.

subject: Science
description: Structure and Function - Structures can be designed to serve particular functions by taking into account properties of different materials, and how materials can be shaped and used.
description_plain: Your child understands that things can be designed to do specific jobs by choosing and shaping materials with the right properties.

subject: Science
description: Natural Resources - Humans depend on Earth's land, ocean, atmosphere, and biosphere for many different resources. Minerals, fresh water, and biosphere resources are limited, and many are not renewable or replaceable over human lifetimes. These resources are distributed unevenly around the planet as a result of past geological processes (link to ESS2.B). Renewable energy resources, and the technologies to exploit them, are being rapidly developed.
description_plain: Your child knows that people rely on Earth's land, water, air, and living things for resources, many of which are limited and unevenly spread, and that renewable energy is being developed quickly.

subject: Science
description: Chemical Reactions - Substances react chemically in characteristic ways. In a chemical process, the atoms that make up the original substances are regrouped into different molecules, and these new substances have different properties from those of the reactants.
description_plain: Your child understands that substances change in specific ways during chemical reactions, forming new substances with different properties.

subject: Science
description: Structure and Properties of Matter - Each pure substance has characteristic physical and chemical properties (for any bulk quantity under given conditions) that can be used to identify it.
description_plain: Your child knows that pure substances have unique physical and chemical features that help identify them.

subject: Science
description: Obtaining, Evaluating, and Communicating Information - Gather, read, and synthesize information from multiple appropriate sources and assess the credibility, accuracy, and possible bias of each publication and methods used, and describe how they are supported or not supported by evidence.
description_plain: Your child can find, read, and combine information from different sources, check how trustworthy and accurate they are, and explain if the evidence supports the ideas.

subject: Science
description: Gather and make sense of information to describe that synthetic materials come from natural resources and impact society.
description_plain: Your child can gather and understand information about how man-made materials come from natural resources and affect society.

subject: Science
description: Systems and System Models - Models (e.g., physical, mathematical, computer models) can be used to simulate systems and interactions— including energy, matter, and information flows— within and between systems at different scales.
description_plain: Your child understands that models like physical or computer versions can show how systems work and interact with energy, matter, and information.

subject: Science
description: Systems and System Models - Models (e.g., physical, mathematical, computer models) can be used to simulate systems and interactions— including energy, matter, and information flows— within and between systems at different scales.
description_plain: Your child understands that models like physical or computer versions can show how systems work and interact with energy, matter, and information.

subject: Science
description: Systems and System Models - Models (e.g., physical, mathematical, computer models) can be used to simulate systems and interactions— including energy, matter, and information flows— within and between systems at different scales.
description_plain: Your child understands that models like physical or computer versions can show how systems work and interact with energy, matter, and information.

subject: Science
description: Systems and System Models - Models (e.g., physical, mathematical, computer models) can be used to simulate systems and interactions— including energy, matter, and information flows— within and between systems at different scales.
description_plain: Your child understands that models like physical or computer versions can show how systems work and interact with energy, matter, and information.

subject: Science
description: Developing Possible Solutions - Both physical models and computers can be used in various ways to aid in the engineering design process. Computers are useful for a variety of purposes, such as running simulations to test different ways of solving a problem or to see which one is most efficient or economical; and in making a persuasive presentation to a client about how a given design will meet his or her needs.
description_plain: Your child knows that physical models and computer simulations help test and improve designs and can be used to explain ideas to others.

subject: Science
description: Developing Possible Solutions - Both physical models and computers can be used in various ways to aid in the engineering design process. Computers are useful for a variety of purposes, such as running simulations to test different ways of solving a problem or to see which one is most efficient or economical; and in making a persuasive presentation to a client about how a given design will meet his or her needs.
description_plain: Your child knows that physical models and computer simulations help test and improve designs and can be used to explain ideas to others.

subject: Science
description: Developing Possible Solutions - Both physical models and computers can be used in various ways to aid in the engineering design process. Computers are useful for a variety of purposes, such as running simulations to test different ways of solving a problem or to see which one is most efficient or economical; and in making a persuasive presentation to a client about how a given design will meet his or her needs.
description_plain: Your child knows that physical models and computer simulations help test and improve designs and can be used to explain ideas to others.

subject: Science
description: Developing Possible Solutions - Both physical models and computers can be used in various ways to aid in the engineering design process. Computers are useful for a variety of purposes, such as running simulations to test different ways of solving a problem or to see which one is most efficient or economical; and in making a persuasive presentation to a client about how a given design will meet his or her needs.
description_plain: Your child knows that physical models and computer simulations help test and improve designs and can be used to explain ideas to others.

subject: Science
description: Using Mathematics and Computational Thinking - Use mathematical models and/or computer simulations to predict the effects of a design solution on systems and/or the interactions between systems.

description: The Roles of Water in Earth's Surface Processes - The abundance of liquid water on Earth's surface and its unique combination of physical and chemical properties are central to the planet's dynamics. These properties include water's exceptional capacity to absorb, store, and release large amounts of energy, transmit sunlight, expand upon freezing, dissolve and transport materials, and lower the viscosities and melting points of rocks.
description_plain: Your child understands that Earth's liquid water has special properties that help shape the planet by storing energy, moving sunlight, and changing materials.

description: Earth Materials and Systems - The geological record shows that changes to global and regional climate can be caused by interactions among changes in the sun's energy output or Earth's orbit, tectonic events, ocean circulation, volcanic activity, glaciers, vegetation, and human activities. These changes can occur on a variety of time scales from sudden (e.g., volcanic ash clouds) to intermediate (ice ages) to very long-term tectonic cycles.
description_plain: Your child understands that Earth's climate has changed over time because of things like the sun’s energy, volcanoes, oceans, glaciers, plants, and human actions.

description: Earth and the Solar System - Cyclical changes in the shape of Earth's orbit around the sun, together with changes in the tilt of the planet's axis of rotation, both occurring over hundreds of thousands of years, have altered the intensity and distribution of sunlight falling on the earth. These phenomena cause a cycle of ice ages and other gradual climate changes.
description_plain: Your child understands that slow changes in Earth's orbit and tilt cause ice ages and other long-term climate changes.

description_plain: Your child understands that heat from radioactive decay inside Earth causes movement in the mantle, which drives plate tectonics.

description: Earth Materials and Systems - Evidence from deep probes and seismic waves, reconstructions of historical changes in Earth's surface and its magnetic field, and an understanding of physical and chemical processes lead to a model of Earth with a hot but solid inner core, a liquid outer core, a solid mantle and crust. Motions of the mantle and its plates occur primarily through thermal convection, which involves the cycling of matter due to the outward flow of energy from Earth's interior and gravitational movement of denser materials toward the interior.
description_plain: Your child understands that Earth has layers like a hot solid inner core, liquid outer core, and solid mantle and crust, and that heat causes these layers to move.

description: Plate Tectonics and Large-Scale System Interactions - Plate tectonics is the unifying theory that explains the past and current movements of the rocks at Earth's surface and provides a framework for understanding its geologic history. Plate movements are responsible for most continental and ocean-floor features and for the distribution of most rocks and minerals within Earth's crust.
description_plain: Your child understands that plate tectonics explains how Earth's surface moves and shapes continents and ocean floors.

description: The History of Planet Earth - Although active geologic processes, such as plate tectonics and erosion, have destroyed or altered most of the very early rock record on Earth, other objects in the solar system, such as lunar rocks, asteroids, and meteorites, have changed little over billions of years. Studying these objects can provide information about Earth's formation and early history.
description_plain: Your child understands that even though Earth’s oldest rocks are mostly gone, studying rocks from the moon and meteorites helps us learn about Earth’s early history.

subject: Science
description: The Universe and Its Stars - The study of stars' light spectra and brightness is used to identify compositional elements of stars, their movements, and their distances from Earth. Other than the hydrogen and helium formed at the time of the Big Bang, nuclear fusion within stars produces all atomic nuclei lighter than and including iron, and the process releases electromagnetic energy. Heavier elements are produced when certain massive stars achieve a supernova stage and explode.
description_plain: Your child understands that by studying the light from stars, scientists can tell what stars are made of, how they move, and how far away they are, and that stars create elements through nuclear fusion and explosions.

subject: Science
description: The Universe and Its Stars - The study of stars' light spectra and brightness is used to identify compositional elements of stars, their movements, and their distances from Earth. The Big Bang theory is supported by observations of distant galaxies receding from our own, of the measured composition of stars and non-stellar gases, and of the maps of spectra of the primordial radiation (cosmic microwave background) that still fills the universe. Other than the hydrogen and helium formed at the time of the Big Bang, nuclear fusion within stars produces all atomic nuclei lighter than and including iron, and the process releases electromagnetic energy. Heavier elements are produced when certain massive stars achieve a supernova stage and explode.
description_plain: Your child understands that studying starlight helps identify what stars are made of, how they move, and how far they are, and that the Big Bang theory is supported by observations of galaxies and cosmic radiation, with stars creating elements through fusion and explosions.

description: Developing Possible Solutions - When evaluating solutions, it is important to take into account a range of constraints, including cost, safety, reliability, and aesthetics, and to consider social, cultural, and environmental impacts. Both physical models and computers can be used in various ways to aid in the engineering design process. Computers are useful for a variety of purposes, such as running simulations to test different ways of solving a problem or to see which one is most efficient or economical; and in making a persuasive presentation to a client about how a given design will meet his or her needs.
description_plain: Your child can evaluate solutions by considering cost, safety, reliability, appearance, and effects on society and the environment, using models and computers to test and explain designs.

subject: Science
description: Biodiversity and Humans - Humans depend on the living world for the resources and other benefits provided by biodiversity. But human activity is also having adverse impacts on biodiversity through overpopulation, overexploitation, habitat destruction, pollution, introduction of invasive species, and climate change. Thus, sustaining biodiversity so that ecosystem functioning and productivity are maintained is essential to supporting and enhancing life on Earth. Sustaining biodiversity also aids humanity by preserving landscapes of recreational or inspirational value.
description_plain: Your child understands that people rely on biodiversity for resources and benefits, but human actions can harm it, so protecting biodiversity is important for life and enjoyment on Earth.

subject: Science
description: Adaptation - Changes in the physical environment, whether naturally occurring or human induced, have thus contributed to the expansion of some species, the emergence of new distinct species as populations diverge under different conditions, and the decline–and sometimes the extinction–of some species. Species become extinct because they can no longer survive and reproduce in their altered environment. If members cannot adjust to change that is too fast or drastic, the opportunity for the species' evolution is lost.
description_plain: Your child understands that environmental changes can cause some species to grow, new species to form, or others to die out if they can’t adapt quickly enough.

subject: Science
description: Adaptation - Natural selection leads to adaptation, that is, to a population dominated by organisms that are anatomically, behaviorally, and physiologically well suited to survive and reproduce in a specific environment. That is, the differential survival and reproduction of organisms in a population that have an advantageous heritable trait leads to an increase in the proportion of individuals in future generations that have the trait and to a decrease in the proportion of individuals that do not.
description_plain: Your child understands that natural selection causes populations to have more individuals with traits that help them survive and reproduce in their environment.

subject: Science
description: Adaptation - Natural selection leads to adaptation, that is, to a population dominated by organisms that are anatomically, behaviorally, and physiologically well suited to survive and reproduce in a specific environment. That is, the differential survival and reproduction of organisms in a population that have an advantageous heritable trait leads to an increase in the proportion of individuals in future generations that have the trait and to a decrease in the proportion of individuals that do not. Adaptation also means that the distribution of traits in a population can change when conditions change.
description_plain: Your child understands that natural selection changes which traits are common in a population, helping organisms survive and reproduce better in their environment.

subject: Science
description: Natural Selection - Natural selection occurs only if there is both (1) variation in the genetic information between organisms in a population and (2) variation in the expression of that genetic information—that is, trait variation—that leads to differences in performance among individuals. The traits that positively affect survival are more likely to be reproduced, and thus are more common in the population.
description_plain: Your child understands that natural selection happens when there are differences in genes and traits among individuals, and those with helpful traits survive and reproduce more.

subject: Science
description: Adaptation - Evolution is a consequence of the interaction of four factors: (1) the potential for a species to increase in number, (2) the genetic variation of individuals in a species due to mutation and sexual reproduction, (3) competition for an environment's limited supply of the resources that individuals need in order to survive and reproduce, and (4) the ensuing proliferation of those organisms that are better able to survive and reproduce in that environment.
description_plain: Your child understands that evolution happens because species can grow in number, have genetic differences, compete for limited resources, and those best suited survive and reproduce.

subject: Science
description: Evidence of Common Ancestry and Diversity - Genetic information provides evidence of evolution. DNA sequences vary among species, but there are many overlaps; in fact, the ongoing branching that produces multiple lines of descent can be inferred by comparing the DNA sequences of different organisms. Such information is also derivable from the similarities and differences in amino acid sequences and from anatomical and embryological evidence.
description_plain: Your child understands that DNA and other biological evidence show how species are related and have evolved over time.

description_plain: Your child understands that DNA and other biological evidence show how species are related and have evolved over time.

subject: Science
description: Variation of Traits - In sexual reproduction, chromosomes can sometimes swap sections during the process of meiosis (cell division), thereby creating new genetic combinations and thus more genetic variation. Although DNA replication is tightly regulated and remarkably accurate, errors do occur and result in mutations, which are also a source of genetic variation. Environmental factors can also cause mutations in genes, and viable mutations are inherited. Environmental factors also affect expression of traits, and hence affect the probability of occurrences of traits in a population. Thus, the variation and distribution of traits observed depends on both genetic and environmental factors.
description_plain: Your child understands that genetic variation comes from mixing genes during reproduction, mutations, and environmental effects, all of which influence traits in a population.

subject: Science
description: Inheritance of Traits - Each chromosome consists of a single very long DNA molecule, and each gene on the chromosome is a particular segment of that DNA. The instructions for forming species' characteristics are carried in DNA. All cells in an organism have the same genetic content, but the genes used (expressed) by the cell may be regulated in different ways. Not all DNA codes for a protein; some segments of DNA are involved in regulatory or structural functions, and some have no as-yet known function.
description_plain: Your child understands that chromosomes are made of long DNA molecules, with genes as specific parts that carry instructions for traits, and that not all DNA makes proteins—some control how genes work or have unknown roles.

subject: Science
description: Biodiversity and Humans - Biodiversity is increased by the formation of new species (speciation) and decreased by the loss of species (extinction). Humans depend on the living world for the resources and other benefits provided by biodiversity. But human activity is also having adverse impacts on biodiversity through overpopulation, overexploitation, habitat destruction, pollution, introduction of invasive species, and climate change. Thus, sustaining biodiversity so that ecosystem functioning and productivity are maintained is essential to supporting and enhancing life on Earth. Sustaining biodiversity also aids humanity by preserving landscapes of recreational or inspirational value.
description_plain: Your child understands that new species increase biodiversity and extinction decreases it, and that humans rely on biodiversity but also harm it through activities like pollution and habitat loss.

subject: Science
description: Ecosystem Dynamics, Functioning, and Resilience - A complex set of interactions within an ecosystem can keep its numbers and types of organisms relatively constant over long periods of time under stable conditions. If a modest biological or physical disturbance to an ecosystem occurs, it may return to its more or less original status (i.e., the ecosystem is resilient), as opposed to becoming a very different ecosystem. Extreme fluctuations in conditions or the size of any population, however, can challenge the functioning of ecosystems in terms of resources and habitat availability.
description_plain: Your child understands that ecosystems can stay balanced over time and recover from small changes, but big changes can disrupt them.

subject: Science
description: Cycles of Matter and Energy Transfer in Ecosystems - Plants or algae form the lowest level of the food web. At each link upward in a food web, only a small fraction of the matter consumed at the lower level is transferred upward, to produce growth and release energy in cellular respiration at the higher level. Given this inefficiency, there are generally fewer organisms at higher levels of a food web. Some matter reacts to release energy for life functions, some matter is stored in newly made structures, and much is discarded. The chemical elements that make up the molecules of organisms pass through food webs and into and out of the atmosphere and soil, and they are combined and recombined in different ways. At each link in an ecosystem, matter and energy are conserved.
description_plain: Your child understands that plants or algae start the food web, and as energy moves up the food chain, only a small part is passed on, so there are fewer animals at higher levels.

subject: Science
description: Interdependent Relationships in Ecosystems - Ecosystems have carrying capacities, which are limits to the numbers of organisms and populations they can support. These limits result from such factors as the availability of living and nonliving resources and from such challenges such as predation, competition, and disease. Organisms would have the capacity to produce populations of great size were it not for the fact that environments and resources are finite. This fundamental tension affects the abundance (number of individuals) of species in any given ecosystem.
description_plain: Your child understands that ecosystems can only support a limited number of organisms because resources and space are limited and challenges like predators and disease affect populations.

These limits result from such factors as the availability of living and nonliving resources and from such challenges such as predation, competition, and disease. Organisms would have the capacity to produce populations of great size were it not for the fact that environments and resources are finite. This fundamental tension affects the abundance (number of individuals) of species in any given ecosystem.
description_plain: Your child understands that ecosystems have limits on how many organisms they can support because of resources and challenges like competition and disease.

subject: Science
description: Organization for Matter and Energy Flow in Organisms - As matter and energy flow through different organizational levels of living systems, chemical elements are recombined in different ways to form different products. As a result of these chemical reactions, energy is transferred from one system of interacting molecules to another. Cellular respiration is a chemical process in which the bonds of food molecules and oxygen molecules are broken and new compounds are formed that can transport energy to muscles. Cellular respiration also releases the energy needed to maintain body temperature despite ongoing energy transfer to the surrounding environment.
description_plain: Your child understands that as matter and energy move through living things, chemical elements combine in new ways to make products like energy for muscles and to keep body temperature steady.

As matter and energy flow through different organizational levels of living systems, chemical elements are recombined in different ways to form different products.
description_plain: Your child understands that sugar molecules made in plants contain elements used to build bigger molecules like proteins and DNA, and that matter and energy move through living systems by recombining chemical elements.

subject: Science
description: Growth and Development of Organisms - In multicellular organisms individual cells grow and then divide via a process called mitosis, thereby allowing the organism to grow. The organism begins as a single cell (fertilized egg) that divides successively to produce many cells, with each parent cell passing identical genetic material (two variants of each chromosome pair) to both daughter cells. Cellular division and differentiation produce and maintain a complex organism, composed of systems of tissues and organs that work together to meet the needs of the whole organism.
description_plain: Your child understands that in organisms made of many cells, cells grow and divide to help the organism grow, and these cells work together as tissues and organs to keep the organism alive.

description: Information Processing - Some circuits give rise to emotions and memories that motivate organisms to seek rewards, avoid punishments, develop fears, or form attachments to members of their own species and, in some cases, to individuals of other species (e.g., mixed herds of mammals, mixed flocks of birds). The integrated functioning of all parts of the brain is important for successful interpretation of inputs and generation of behaviors in response to them.
description_plain: Your child understands that some brain circuits create emotions and memories that help organisms seek rewards, avoid dangers, and form bonds, and that the whole brain works together to interpret information and guide behavior.

description: Structure and Function - Feedback mechanisms maintain a living system's internal conditions within certain limits and mediate behaviors, allowing it to remain alive and functional even as external conditions change within some range. Feedback mechanisms can encourage (through positive feedback) or discourage (negative feedback) what is going on inside the living system.
description_plain: Your child understands that feedback helps living things keep their internal conditions steady and control behaviors so they can survive even when the environment changes.

description: Information Processing - In a more complex organism, the systems become more complex to provide more input to allow for decision making regarding events around the organism. The integration of the systems is important for the successful interpretation of inputs and generation of behaviors in response to them.

description: Structure and Function - Systems of specialized cells within organisms help them perform the essential functions of life. subject: Science
description: Definitions of Energy - Energy is a quantitative property of a system that depends on the motion and interactions of matter and radiation within that system. That there is a single quantity called energy is due to the fact that a system's total energy is conserved, even as, within the system, energy is continually transferred from one object to another and between its various possible forms. At the macroscopic scale, energy manifests itself in multiple ways, such as in motion, sound, light, and thermal energy. These relationships are better understood at the microscopic scale, at which all of the different manifestations of energy can be modeled as a combination of energy associated with the motion of particles and energy associated with the configuration (relative position of the particles). In some cases, the relative position energy can be thought of as stored in fields (which mediate interactions between particles). This last concept includes radiation, a phenomenon in which energy stored in fields moves across space.
description_plain: Your child knows that energy depends on how matter and radiation move and interact, and that total energy stays the same even as it changes form or moves between objects.

In some cases, the relative position energy can be thought of as stored in fields (which mediate interactions between particles). This last concept includes radiation, a phenomenon in which energy stored in fields moves across space.
description_plain: Your child knows that energy depends on how matter and radiation move and interact, and that total energy stays the same even as it changes form or moves between objects.

subject: Science
description: Definitions of Energy - Energy is a quantitative property of a system that depends on the motion and interactions of matter and radiation within that system. That there is a single quantity called energy is due to the fact that a system's total energy is conserved, even as, within the system, energy is continually transferred from one object to another and between its various possible forms. At the macroscopic scale, energy manifests itself in multiple ways, such as in motion, sound, light, and thermal energy. These relationships are better understood at the microscopic scale, at which all of the different manifestations of energy can be modeled as a combination of energy associated with the motion of particles and energy associated with the configuration (relative position of the particles). In some cases, the relative position energy can be thought of as stored in fields (which mediate interactions between particles). This last concept includes radiation, a phenomenon in which energy stored in fields moves across space.
description_plain: Your child knows that energy depends on how matter and radiation move and interact, and that total energy stays the same even as it changes form or moves between objects.

subject: Science
description: Conservation of Energy and Energy Transfer - Conservation of energy means that the total change of energy in any system is always equal to the total energy transferred into or out of the system. Energy cannot be created or destroyed, but it can be transported from one place to another and transferred between systems. Mathematical expressions, which quantify how the stored energy in a system depends on its configuration (e.g., relative positions of charged particles, compression of a spring) and how kinetic energy depends on mass and speed, allow the concept of conservation of energy to be used to predict and describe system behavior. The availability of energy limits what can occur in any system.
description_plain: Your child knows that energy in a system changes only when energy moves in or out, and math can help predict how energy stored in things like springs or moving objects affects the system.

description: Structure and Properties of Matter - Each atom has a charged substructure consisting of a nucleus, which is made of protons and neutrons, surrounded by electrons. The periodic table orders elements horizontally by the number of protons in the atom's nucleus and places those with similar chemical properties in columns. The repeating patterns of this table reflect patterns of outer electron states.
description_plain: Your child understands that atoms have a nucleus with protons and neutrons, surrounded by electrons, and the periodic table arranges elements by protons and similar properties based on electron patterns.
